− Iteration 1 — Avg Score: 83.0
+ Classic sci-fi: The Azra Gambit — Score: 80.0
Prompt:
Classic sci-fi (Author style: Asimov) The Azra Gambit Colonial mars is being mined by corporations who take leases on indentured labourers. The thing they are mining is Azra, a recently discovered exotic metal which accelerates radioactive decay to such a rate that it is greatly sought after for interstellar drives and weapons alike. This has created both a gold rush and an arms race as various interests vie for control and endeavour to unlock Azra's secrets. The story follows Arthur Neegan, a first generation settler and mining engineer. Upon discovering that his unassuming plot sits atop an immense Azra vein, he is subjected to a flurry of interest and scrutiny. Write the next chapter in this story, in which an armed retinue descends on Arthur's home and politely but forcefully invites him to a meeting with some unknown party off-world. The insignia look like that of the Antares diplomatic corp -- diplomatic in name only. Arthur finds himself in the centre of a political tug of war. The chapter involves a meeting with this unknown party, who makes Arthur an offer. The scene should be primarily dialogue, interspersed with vivid description & scene setting. It should sow hints of the larger intrigue, stakes & dangers. Include Asimov's trademark big-and-small-picture world building and retrofuturistic classic scifi vibe. The chapter begins with Arthur aboard the transfer vessel, wondering just what he's gotten involved in. Length: 1000 words.
Model Output:
**The Azra Gambit - Chapter 4: An Invitation** Arthur Neegan was aboard the transfer vessel, wondering just what he had gotten himself involved in. The vessel was not a vessel at all, in the proper sense. It was a lifting hulk, a converted ore scow with its guts ripped out and replaced with acceleration couches and an air scrubber that rattled. It smelled of hot oil and recycled sweat, the authentic perfume of Mars-to-orbit traffic. A small display screen on the wall cycles through various system diagnostics in green text. ``` O2 PARTIAL: 19.8% NOMINAL CABIN PRESS: 101.2 kPa REACTOR FLUX: STABLE ASCENT VECTOR: 7.2 DEG / 1.8 G ETA RENDEZVOUS: 00:41:12 ``` The numbers ticked over with a soft, analog click. Behind the panel, Arthur could hear the relays. He had always liked relays. You could understand a relay. He did not understand the two men who flanked him. They had come down six hours before, through the thin, butterscotch sky over Ares Vallis. Arthur had been in his hab, running the spectrograph on core sample 7-B, which had come up so hot with Azra that the Geiger counter had not clicked but screamed. A vein. Not a seam, not a pocket. A vein the size of a buried river. Then the shadow. Three dropships in tight formation, matte black, too clean to be corporate. They did not burn dust on landing; they rode grav-plates all the way down, which meant power to waste. Ten figures in hard-vac armor stepped out, rifles held at the low-ready that professionals use when they want you to know they are being polite. Their leader had rapped on the hab's pressure door with a gauntleted knuckle. Politely. "Mr. Arthur Neegan?" The voice had come through Arthur's own speaker, clear and unaccented. "Apologies for the intrusion. We would consider it a great favor if you would accompany us." Arthur had stared at the insignia on their shoulders. A black starburst with a red center. Antares. The Diplomatic Corps. Everyone on Mars knew that joke. The Antares Combine didn't have diplomats. They had procurement officers with diplomatic immunity. "My lease is registered with the Colonial Office," Arthur had said. It was the only thing he could think to say. "Of course it is," the officer had said. "Which is why this is an invitation, not a requisition. An interested party wishes to discuss your future. Off-world." An invitation, Arthur thought now, watching the green text flicker, that you cannot refuse without being shot, is still an invitation in the technical, legalistic sense. The Antareans were very legalistic. The transfer vessel shuddered as it matched velocity. Through the tiny viewport, Arthur saw it: the ship they were rendezvousing with. It was not a corporate freighter. It was a cruiser, long and needle-thin, with no radiators extended, which meant it had a sink for its heat, which meant military grade. On its flank, the same black starburst, fifty feet high. They docked with a sigh of equalizing pressure. The interior of the Antarean ship was a shock after the scow. It was quiet. The air was cool and did not smell of anything at all. The corridors were lined with brushed aluminum and linoleum, like an office building from a century ago. Retro-futuristic, almost homey, if you did not notice that there were no corners where a man could hide. Arthur was led to a conference room. There was a real wooden table in it. Real wood, on a warship. That, more than the rifles, told him how much money Azra was worth. A woman was waiting for him. She was not in armor. She wore a plain gray suit, of the sort a professor at the University of Trantor might wear. She was perhaps fifty, her hair cut short, her face pleasant and entirely forgettable. She was pouring tea from a steel pot. "Mr. Neegan. Thank you for coming. I am Undersecretary Liora Chen." She gestured to a chair. Arthur sat. The tea was real, too. "You've had a busy week," she said. "First generation, isn't it? Came out on the *Bradbury*. Mining Engineer, Second Class. You took a dead lease in the Vallis because it was cheap. And now, quite by accident, you are the most important man on Mars." "I found some metal," Arthur said. "You found the future," she said, and smiled. "May I be blunt? You understand what Azra does, of course. Better than most. It catalyzes decay. A lump of U-238 that would sit inert for four billion years will expend itself in a week if you pack it in Azra foil. That means a torch drive that can burn for ten years can now burn for ten days and produce a thousand times the thrust. It means a warhead the size of a grapefruit can level Olympus City." "We knew that," Arthur said. "That's why the rush." "Yes. And that is why the problem." She slid a thin folder across the wood toward him. Inside were photographs. Not of his claim. Of Earth. Of Luna. Of Antares Prime. Columns of figures. "Do you know what happens when every prospector with a Geiger counter can build a planet-cracker in his garage, Mr. Neegan? The Colonial Office knows. They have been trying to throttle production. The corporations know, and they are trying to accelerate it. Do you know what the price of Azra was yesterday on the Phobos Exchange?" "I don't follow the Exchange." "Fourteen thousand credits a gram. Yesterday it was eleven. Tomorrow, if word of your vein leaks, it will be forty. Or four. Markets hate uncertainty. Governments hate it more." Arthur stared at the figures. They were production curves, logistic projections. Asimovian stuff — the big picture made of small, greedy decisions. "What do you want from me?" he asked. "We want to buy your lease," Chen said. "Outright. Fifty million credits, Terran standard, deposited in Geneva. You will be a very wealthy man. You will never have to look at another core sample." Arthur felt his breath catch. Fifty million. It was a number from a story. "And if I say no?" "Then you will keep your lease," she said evenly. "For approximately three weeks. Until Consolidated Ores contests it in court. Or until one of their indentured teams has an accident near your hab. Or until the Colonial Office nationalizes it for the war effort. You are a first-generation settler, Mr. Neegan. You have no family. No patrons. The big picture, you see, is made of very small men being crushed by very large forces." She leaned forward. The pleasantness dropped away like a mask. "We are not the villains of this piece, Mr. Neegan. We are the alternative. The Combine will cap the vein. We will extract it at a controlled rate. We will not build weapons with it. We will build ships. Ships that can make the crossing to the outer colonies in months instead of decades. We will make the human sphere smaller, and therefore safer. That is our offer." "And if I want to mine it myself?" "Then you will be a very dead martyr for free enterprise," she said, and then, as if remembering herself, poured more tea. "I'm sorry. That was undiplomatic. The Corps has been criticized for its bedside manner." Arthur looked at the green tea in the white cup. He thought of the display screen in the scow, ticking its diagnostics. O2 PARTIAL: NOMINAL. REACTOR FLUX: STABLE. All systems nominal, while the ship fell upward into a war. "Your vein," Chen said softly, "is not just Azra, Mr. Neegan. Our survey says it is pure. Unusually pure. Pure enough to do something we have only theorized. To make a decay chain run backward." Arthur looked up, sharply. "That's impossible. Thermodynamics—" "Is a statistical law," she finished for him. "And statistics can be... accelerated. Think about it. If you can accelerate decay, why not the reverse? Why not reconstitute? Imagine what that would mean." Arthur felt cold, despite the warm cabin. If Azra could be used to rebuild elements, not just burn them — then it wasn't a fuel. It was everything. Matter itself, made to order. And every government, every corporation, every desperate colony in human space would kill to have it. He understood, at last, what he had gotten himself involved in.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates a strong grasp of Asimov's style and the classic science fiction aesthetic. The author successfully captures the retrofuturistic feel through details like the analog clicking displays, relay systems, and brushed aluminum corridors that evoke mid-20th century office buildings. The technical exposition about Azra and its implications is handled in true Asimovian fashion, building from small details to universe-altering consequences. The world-building is particularly effective, establishing the political and economic tensions through concrete details rather than exposition dumps. The contrast between the grimy ore scow and the pristine Antarean cruiser efficiently communicates power dynamics and resource disparities. The author skillfully weaves in the larger implications of Azra discovery while maintaining focus on Arthur's personal predicament. Arthur's characterization feels authentic - he's competent within his expertise but clearly out of his depth in political machinations. His responses are measured and believable for someone thrust into circumstances beyond his control. Chen is well-crafted as a polite but menacing corporate operative, her mask of civility occasionally slipping to reveal the threat beneath. The dialogue serves multiple functions effectively: advancing plot, revealing character, and exposing the broader political situation. Chen's speech patterns feel appropriately bureaucratic yet threatening, while Arthur's responses show his growing understanding of his situation's gravity. The pacing builds tension well, moving from Arthur's confusion through the revelation of his discovery's true significance to the final bombshell about Azra's potential for matter reconstruction. This escalation feels earned rather than forced. However, the piece occasionally veers toward over-explanation, particularly in the technical descriptions. Some passages feel slightly overwrought, though this may be intentional given the genre conventions. The ending revelation about reversing decay chains, while dramatic, borders on convenient plot advancement rather than organic discovery. The prose generally flows well with good sentence variety, though some constructions feel slightly artificial. The author maintains consistent tone throughout, balancing technical detail with human drama effectively. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 17 Nuanced Characters: 16 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 17 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 17 Elegant Prose: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Emotionally Complex: 14 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 4 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 6 Overwrought: 7 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Sentences Flow Naturally: 15 Overall Reader Engagement: 17 Overall Impression: 16

+ Contemporary romance: Darkroom Revelations — Score: 88.2
Prompt:
Darkroom Revelations Write a first-person narrative from the perspective of Dani, a 25-year-old assertive art student known for her bold style and take-charge attitude in relationships. For the past semester, she's been quietly fascinated by Elliot (he/him), a 19-year-old boy in her experimental photography class. Dani has been intrigued by his unconventional masculinity: killer thrift-shop-nu-goth style, soft demeanour and quiet intellect. When they're paired for a final project requiring late nights in the darkroom, Elliot tentatively shares that he's been questioning his gender identity and expression. Set during one of these intimate darkroom sessions where the red light creates a confessional atmosphere. Explore Dani's complex attraction—her usual assertiveness complicated by her desire to protect Elliot's vulnerability, her excitement at witnessing his self-discovery, and her confusion about what this means for her own identity as someone typically attracted to more defined gender presentations. The narrative should capture the tender awkwardness of their conversations, Dani's internal struggle between taking charge and wanting to give Elliot agency, and the unexpected chemistry that develops as both find themselves in uncharted territory. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The darkroom has always made me feel powerful. It's the one place in the whole art building where I get to be still. Everywhere else I'm loud on purpose - shaved undercut grown out and dyed copper, big silver hoops, my jacket covered in patches I sewed on myself. I take up space. In critiques I talk first. In relationships, I always make the first move. I like knowing what I want. What I wanted this semester was to stop staring at Elliot. He's in my experimental photo class, which already tells you everything about our age gap. I'm 25, back for my BFA after two years of actually trying to pay rent with art. He's 19, a freshman who looks like he got lost on his way to a Bauhaus show in 1983 and decided to stay. Thrifted mesh, black nail polish chipped to hell, those huge thrifted trousers with chains, but soft. That's the thing. Not soft like weak - soft like he moves quiet. He never interrupts. When Professor Chen talks about Barthes he actually listens, his head tipped down, his hair falling in his face. I told myself my fascination was aesthetic. Good reference material. Then Chen paired us for finals. "Dani, you need to learn to collaborate. Elliot, you need to learn to speak up. Perfect." So here we are. Night three. 11:47 p.m. The darkroom is red and wet and smells like chemicals and old wood. The safelight makes everything look like it's already a memory. We've been working in this rhythm that feels almost too intimate - me timing, him agitating the trays, both of us shoulder to shoulder because the room is the size of a closet. He's humming. It's barely anything, just air through his nose while we wait for our test strip to come up in the developer. Some sad, familiar tune I can't place. His sleeves are pushed up and his forearms are pale and thin, veins blue. He's wearing a black slip dress over a long-sleeve tee tonight, and I had to actively stop myself from commenting on it the second he walked in. My usual move would be: *fuck, you look hot.* Take charge. Claim. Instead I said nothing, because I saw the way his shoulders were up around his ears. The print starts to bleed in. The white paper goes grey, then the image - my shot of the fire escape behind the library - starts to ghost up. "You know," he says, not looking at me, still humming that one note, "I think I wore this because... I wanted to see if I could wear it here. Where it's dark." That lands in my stomach. "Yeah?" I say. My voice is lower than I meant it to be. I'm trying to do this new thing where I don't fill every silence with my own momentum. He nods. The red light catches the gloss on his lips. He does that thing where he chews the inside of his cheek when he's nervous. "I've been... questioning stuff," he says, very fast, like ripping off a bandage. "Gender stuff. Expression, I guess. Identity too. I don't know what words yet. I told my roommate and he just said 'cool' and then never brought it up again which is... cool, but also. I don't know." The print is fully there now. I should move it to the stop bath. I don't. "Thank you for telling me," I say. And I hate how careful I sound. I'm not a careful person. I'm the person who grabs someone's hand and pulls them onto the dance floor. But with him, every instinct that usually serves me feels suddenly too big, too sharp. He finally looks at me. In the red, his eyes are dark and huge. "Is it weird? For the project? Because you - you're so... you know exactly what you are. You're so *defined.*" That hits a weird nerve. Because yes, I'm defined. Dani, the assertive art dyke who only dates butches and femmes and knows exactly what she likes. I've built a whole personality around knowing. And sitting here next to him in his dress and his quiet, I feel my own definitions wobble. "It's not weird," I say. "And I don't know exactly what I am, actually. I think I just got good at pretending I do." He laughs, a little puff of air. "No way." "Way." I finally lift the print with the tongs, slide it into the stop. The chemical slosh is loud. "I usually go for people who are... really solid. Like, capital-M Masculine or capital-F Femme. I like knowing where I stand. You - you're confusing me. In a good way. In a way that's making me have to actually think instead of just... pounce." "Pounce?" He smiles for real this time, and I see his chipped front tooth. "Yeah. I'm a pouncer. It's my brand. Bad habit." "I don't mind being pounced on," he says, and then immediately goes scarlet, even in red light you can see it. "I mean - not - I didn't mean -" "I know what you meant," I say, and my heart is doing something stupid and loud. I want to reach out. I want to tuck his hair behind his ear. I want to kiss the nervous out of him. That's the old script. Take charge, make it easy for him. But easy for who? So I keep my hands on the edge of the sink. The water is running. "What do you want, Elliot? Right now, in this room." He blinks. Nobody's ever asked him that, I can tell. "I... I want you to look at me. Like, really look. Not like I'm a project. Or like I'm fragile." Okay. That's clear. I can do that. I turn. Really turn. Let my eyes do the work I usually make my mouth do. I look at the dress, how it hangs off his bony shoulders. The tee underneath, the little laddered hole at the cuff he's been picking at. His throat. His jaw that he's maybe self-conscious about, maybe not. His softness. His killer eyeliner that he definitely did in the dorm bathroom and hoped no one would comment on. "You look right," I tell him, and my voice cracks. "You look like yourself tonight. More than other nights." His breath catches. He hums again, that same soft tune, but it breaks in the middle. "I'm scared I'm making it up," he whispers. "For attention. Or because I like how I look in this and that's not... enough of a reason, right?" "Hey." Before I can stop myself, I reach - not to grab, just to hover my hand near his arm, asking. He nods, tiny. I touch his wrist, just my fingertips on his cold skin. "Liking how you look is reason enough. Liking how you *feel* when you look like this is more than enough. You don't need a thesis statement." He stares at our hands. My chipped black polish, his chipped black polish. Matching without trying. "Can I... can I ask you something fucked up?" he says. "Always." "Does it... does this change how you see me? Like, attraction-wise? Because you said you like defined -" I think about honesty. The confessional red light makes it easier. "Yes," I say. "It changes it. It makes it bigger and weirder and I don't have a word for it yet, which is fucking with my head because I always have words. I was attracted to you before, when you were the quiet goth boy in the back. I'm attracted to you now, when you're... you, figuring it out. It's not less. It's just not a shape I knew I liked. And I want to be really careful not to make your questioning about me and my little identity crisis, okay? This is yours." He's quiet for a long second, just the water and the safelight buzzing. Then he says, "You can take charge a little bit, you know. If you want. I like when you're... sure. It makes me feel less like I'm floating." The permission knocks the wind out of me. That's the thing I was so scared to get wrong - I was so busy trying not to pounce that I didn't ask if he wanted to be caught. I step closer. Not crowding. Just close enough that I can smell his shampoo, something cheap and sweet. "Can I?" I ask, and lift my hand to his face, waiting. He leans into it. His cheek is cold. He closes his eyes. "Yeah." I don't kiss him. Not yet. I just hold his face in my hand, my thumb brushing that sharp cheekbone, and we stand there in the red dark while our print over-develops in the stop bath and is probably ruined. He starts humming again, that same soft tune, steadier now, and I realize it's the lullaby my mom used to sing. In the red light, we are both undeveloped. Both still coming up through the chemicals. Both waiting to see what image appears. And for once, I don't need to rush it.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong technical execution and emotional intelligence in handling a delicate subject matter. The narrative successfully captures Dani's voice as an assertive art student while showing her vulnerability when confronted with unfamiliar territory. The darkroom setting is expertly utilized both literally and metaphorically, creating an intimate confessional space that serves the story's themes of identity exploration and developing attraction. The characterization is particularly strong. Dani feels authentic as someone who typically takes charge but finds herself needing to navigate more carefully. Her internal conflict between her usual assertiveness and her desire to give Elliot agency creates compelling tension. Elliot emerges as a fully realized character despite limited dialogue - his vulnerability, uncertainty, and tentative self-exploration feel genuine rather than performative. The prose strikes an effective balance between literary sophistication and accessibility. The author employs strong sensory details (the red light, chemical smells, physical descriptions) without becoming overwrought. The darkroom metaphor of "undeveloped" images waiting to appear is elegant and well-integrated rather than heavy-handed. The dialogue feels natural and age-appropriate, capturing the awkwardness and tentative intimacy of the situation. Conversations flow organically between vulnerability and nervous humor. The pacing allows for meaningful silences and physical moments that enhance rather than replace verbal communication. The emotional complexity is handled with maturity. Rather than rushing toward resolution, the piece sits comfortably in uncertainty and questioning. Both characters are allowed their confusion and growth without forcing premature clarity or transformation. Some minor weaknesses include occasional moments where the prose becomes slightly self-conscious about its own cleverness, and a few instances where internal monologue could be more concise. However, these don't significantly detract from the overall effectiveness. The ending strikes an appropriate note - intimate and hopeful without being unrealistically resolved. The final metaphor ties together the photographic and personal development themes without being forced. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 19 Believable Character Actions: 18 Nuanced Characters: 19 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 17 Elegant Prose: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 18 Emotionally Complex: 19 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 2 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 3 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 4 Overwrought: 3 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Sentences Flow Naturally: 17 Overall Reader Engagement: 18 Overall Impression: 18

+ Contemporary romance / drama: Stolen Moments in Roma — Score: 82.5
Prompt:
Contemporary Romance / Drama: Stolen Moments in Roma. Write a series of interconnected short scenes and dialogues that span a single day in modern-day Rome, in narrative form. This will form the beginning of a larger story. Alessandra, a fiery, independent Roman woman who works as a motorcycle courier, nearly collides with Ben, a somewhat clumsy, overly polite British academic on sabbatical, who is engrossed in a guidebook near the Trevi Fountain. The initial encounter is a clash of cultures and personalities – Alessandra is all sharp wit and rapid-fire Italian, while Ben is flustered apologies and awkward attempts at speaking the language. However, as they sort out the near-miss, a spark of unexpected humor and connection emerges. Alessandra, it quickly becomes clear, is a whirlwind of energy and mischief. She's a self-admitted troublemaker with a secret penchant for kleptomania, a detail she doesn't explicitly reveal, but which manifests in subtle, daring acts throughout the day. She impulsively decides to take Ben on a wild, unsanctioned tour of Rome, dragging him in some specific instances far outside his comfort zone. Ben, despite his initial apprehension and inherent politeness, finds himself utterly captivated by Alessandra's unapologetic spirit and zest for life. He is drawn into her orbit, a willing participant in increasingly outlandish situations, a quiet rebellion brewing beneath his scholarly exterior. The narrative should primarily consist of short, vivid scenes and dialogue exchanges, revealing their contrasting personalities and backgrounds, and the growing, ambiguous tension between them. Ben is captivated by Alessandra's energy and the thrill of breaking free from his routine. There's a clear sense that Alessandra is toying with Ben, pushing his boundaries and enjoying his reactions, but her own feelings remain somewhat enigmatic. The day should be a rollercoaster of exhilarating, slightly dangerous, and humorous moments, leaving Ben completely disoriented but utterly smitten. Explore the underlying tension & chemistry between them, culminating in some payoff as they part ways (or not -- your choice). Include this detail: Write in narrative prose, using immersive storytelling. 1000 words.
Model Output:
**STOLEN MOMENTS IN ROMA** **09:17 - VIA DELLE MURATTE, TREVI** The Vespa - no, a battered Ducati Scrambler - screeched. Alessandra Rossi had the bike leaned so far over the cobblestones spat. The tourist in front of her - tall, sandy-haired, nose buried in a Lonely Planet held open to *Trevi Fountain: Avoiding the Crowds* - stepped directly into her line. She swerved. He yelped. The guidebook went flying. "Ma sei scemo? Vuoi morire? Guarda dove cazzo vai!" Ben Chambers stumbled back against a souvenir stand, arms windmilling, sending a cascade of plastic Colosseums to the ground. "Oh! Oh gosh - I'm so sorry! Mi dispiace! I am so, so sorry -" He tried to pick up the Colosseums. He tried to pick up her bike, which hadn't even fallen. He tried to pick up the guidebook, which had landed open in a puddle. Alessandra killed the engine and kicked down the stand. Helmet off. A shock of black curls, sweat at the temple, eyes furious and very, very brown. "You are sorry? You read about not having crowds while you make crowd? In the middle of the street?" Her English was excellent, accented, fast as gunfire. "I was - the book said to approach from the north for the best light and I -" "The best light to be killed," she said. She looked at him, really looked. His cheap linen shirt, his sunburn, his terrible Italian. And then she laughed, a short, bright bark. "You are English." "British, yes. Ben. Ben Chambers. I'm - I'm on sabbatical. Classics. At King's -" "Of course you are Classics. You look like Classics. Come. You owe me a coffee for nearly killing me." "I - we haven't even -" "You almost died. I almost went to jail. Coffee is cheaper." **10:04 - SANT'EUSTACHIO** She didn't ask if he could ride. She just tossed him her spare helmet. "Hold on. Not like that. Like you mean it." Ben clung to the back of the Ducati as she tore through Rome like it was built for her alone. Past buses, through alleys that weren't wide enough for buses, up a one-way street the wrong way. He shouted apologies to pedestrians on her behalf. She parked by yanking the bike onto the sidewalk outside a tiny bar. "Two gran caffè," she told the barista, then, to Ben, "So, Professor. What does a Classics man do on sabbatical? Look for ruins?" "Trying to finish a monograph. On Ovid's exile. It's... not going well." "Exile. In Rome everyone is exiled from something." She watched the barista work, then her hand flickered - casual, elegant - and the little silver spoon from the saucer tray vanished into the pocket of her leather jacket. Ben blinked. Surely not. She pushed a coffee toward him. "Drink. Fast. Like a Roman." He burned his tongue terribly. "So what is courier? You deliver... things?" "Everything. Legal papers. Love letters. Once, a live turtle. People in Rome pay me to be faster than traffic. Which is not hard, because traffic here is dead." "And you enjoy it?" "I enjoy that no one tells me what to do. Except you, with your guidebook." She grinned. "You want real tour? Not Trevi Fountain where you throw coin and wish to leave?" "My book says it's mandatory." "Your book is a liar. Come." **13:30 - TESTACCIO MARKET** The market was chaos and shouting and the smell of frying artichokes. Alessandra walked through it like a queen, stealing bites. "Try," she commanded, holding out a supplì that she had definitely not paid for yet. Ben bit. Rice, tomato, molten mozzarella. "God - that's incredible." "You see? Better than book." While the vendor argued with another customer, her fingers brushed a pair of cheap, tortoiseshell sunglasses off a stall. Into her jacket. Then, a second later, she put them on Ben's face. "There. Now you look less like a lost professor. More like a disgraced DJ. Better." He laughed despite himself. "Are you... do you always..." "Always what?" "Nothing. You just - you have a very free interpretation of property." "I have a free interpretation of everything," she said, and for a second her smile was too sharp to read. He was supposed to feel alarmed. Instead, he felt lighter than he had in three years of faculty meetings. **16:12 - AVENTINO, THE KEYHOLE** She made him climb. "The famous keyhole. All the tourists wait in line for one hour to see one thing. Stupid." "It's St. Peter's dome framed perfectly through -" "We are not waiting. We are going over." "Over the wall? Alessandra, that's - that's trespassing. That's private property of the Knights of Malta!" "So? You study Ovid. He was all about trespassing. Metamorphosis. Breaking rules." Before he could formulate a response about Ovidian exile as metaphor, she was up, boots finding holds in the ancient stone, hand extended down. "Come, Professor. Quiet rebellion. You said you wanted one." He didn't remember saying that. But he took her hand. Her palm was calloused, warm. He scraped his knee, tore his linen shirt, and landed beside her in a garden of orange trees that smelled like heaven. Rome spread below them, golden and endless. "Oh," he breathed. She sat on the low wall, legs dangling over the drop. "My father used to bring me here when I was little. Before he left. He said Rome is best when you steal it." Ben sat beside her, too close. Their shoulders touched. "You are... disorienting," he said quietly. "You like it." It wasn't a question. **17:45 - GIARDINO DEGLI ARANCI** "Alessandra!" A woman with a stroller and a tired smile stopped on the path. "Mamma mia, Alessandra Rossi. Still alive? Still causing trouble?" "Giulia! Look at you, respectable!" They kissed cheeks, rapid Roman dialect flying. Ben caught maybe one word in four. Giulia turned to him, switching to English. "You are with her? You be careful, okay? This one -" she tapped her temple, " - furba. Wily. She steals. Not just things. Hearts. Time. She makes you miss your flight and you thank her." Alessandra rolled her eyes, but didn't deny it. "I stole your boyfriend once, Giulia. Remember? Luca. For one week." "And you gave him back because he was boring!" Giulia laughed, then looked pointedly at Ben. "She gets bored quickly, professore." After Giulia left, silence settled. Ben fiddled with his torn shirt. "Is that true?" he asked at last. "Which part? That I am wily? Yes. That I get bored? Sometimes." She looked out over the city. "My life - it's small and fast, Ben. I go, I deliver, I go. People like you, you stay. You write books that will be there in one hundred years. I like... moments. I take them. That's all." He thought of the spoon, the sunglasses still perched on his head, the peach she had pocketed at the market and now offered him, biting into it herself first. "I think," he said, with absurd, sudden clarity, "my monograph is rubbish. I've been trying to write about exile while sitting in a library in London. I should have been getting yelled at in Italian." That made her laugh again, real this time. She turned, and her knee pressed against his. "You are not as clumsy as you look, British." "You are not as enigmatic as you pretend, Roman." "Oh, I am very enigmatic." But she leaned in, just a fraction. The air between them went tight and humming. **20:03 - LUNGOTEVERE** They walked the bike down to the river, where the evening market was setting up along the Tiber. She was supposed to be on a delivery run at 18:00. She had ignored three calls. "I should let you go," she said, though she made no move to get on the bike. "You should," he said. "Your book says you must see the Trevi at night. More romantic." "My book is a liar, remember?" She looked at him, and for once her expression wasn't teasing. It was considering. Weighing. "Tomorrow I have to take papers to Ostia. It's by the sea. Ugly beach. Beautiful at six in the morning. No tourists. Only fishermen and thieves. You want to be disoriented again?" Ben thought of his empty apartment on Via del Governo Vecchio, his blinking cursor, his return ticket in September. "What time?" "Five thirty. Don't be late. And wear better shoes." She swung onto the Ducati. He handed her the helmet. Their fingers brushed and lingered. "Ben?" "Yes?" "The sunglasses - you can keep them. You looked good. Like a man who might steal something back." She kicked the engine, loud in the blue dusk, and was gone before he could answer, leaving him on the riverbank with a stolen peach pit in his pocket, completely lost, and utterly, completely smitten.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong technical execution and creative storytelling within the contemporary romance genre. The author successfully creates a vivid, immersive day in Rome through interconnected vignettes that effectively showcase the cultural clash and growing attraction between Alessandra and Ben. The characterization is particularly well-handled. Alessandra emerges as a compelling figure - her kleptomania is woven naturally into the narrative through subtle actions (stealing the spoon, sunglasses, fruit) rather than heavy-handed exposition. Her dialogue crackles with authentic voice, mixing sharp wit with genuine vulnerability. Ben's transformation from bumbling academic to someone willing to climb walls feels organic, driven by genuine attraction to Alessandra's freedom rather than forced plot mechanics. The prose style effectively balances literary quality with accessibility. Sentences like "The Vespa - no, a battered Ducati Scrambler - screeched" demonstrate confident voice and attention to detail. The author uses sensory details effectively throughout - the smell of frying artichokes, the taste of supplì, the scrape of Ben's knee on ancient stone. The structure works well, with time stamps creating clear progression while allowing for natural scene transitions. Each vignette serves dual purposes: advancing the relationship while revealing character depth. The encounter with Giulia provides external perspective that adds complexity without feeling contrived. The dialogue feels natural and serves character development effectively. Alessandra's mix of Italian and English, her rapid-fire delivery, and Ben's fumbling politeness create distinct voices. The romantic tension builds believably through small moments rather than grand gestures. However, some elements feel slightly predictable within genre conventions - the "uptight academic meets free spirit" dynamic, while well-executed, doesn't break entirely new ground. The ending, while satisfying, follows expected romantic progression patterns. The piece successfully fulfills the prompt requirements, incorporating all specified elements (the near-collision, Alessandra's kleptomania, the unsanctioned tour) while maintaining narrative flow. The writing demonstrates professional-level craft with strong character work and engaging prose. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 17 Nuanced Characters: 16 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 17 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 16 Elegant Prose: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Emotionally Complex: 15 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 2 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 8 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 4 Overwrought: 3 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 4 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Sentences Flow Naturally: 17 Overall Reader Engagement: 17 Overall Impression: 16

+ Cozy sci-fi: The Last Outpost — Score: 81.6
Prompt:
Cozy sci-fi The Last Outpost Nettle (or Nets to her friends) bartends at The Last Outpost -- a lazy hub for miners and merchants in an asteroid belt on the Andromeda outer rim. Escaping from her past life, Nets found a home here at the Outpost. They have, thus far, tolerated Net's flaws which she tries to keep in equilibrium with her assortment of talents. Her evenings involve chatting with patrons, negotiating the drama, intrigue, problems & love lives of the diverse crowd that filter through the Outpost. Tonight is no different, at least, until a frazzled looking stranger, tentacled dreads askew, quietly plops down at the bar: "Vega whisky, neat. Better make that a double." Write a chapter in this story. Establish conflict for narrative interest. Don't resolve the conflict within this chapter. Aim for about 60% dialogue. First person, present tense. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The dinner rush is over, which at The Last Outpost means the gravity plate in the galley finally stops sparking and Miko stops yelling. I wipe down the bar. Same circuit, left to right. Jax is telling his mining crew about the time his ex-wife tried to sell his left boot on the exchange, and Mama Cho is pretending not to listen while she absolutely listens. The air smells like fried root and recycled coolant, which is how I know I'm home. I am very good at three things: mixing drinks in low-g, remembering exactly how you like your noodles, and minding other people's business. I'm very bad at two things: minding my own business, and knowing when to stop talking. Management tolerates this balance. The door hisses. Cold puff from the lock. Everyone looks, because that's what you do out here. You look. She's tall, even slumped. Skin the blue-grey of deep ice. And dreads - real tentacled ones, not fashion ones - askew, frizzing at the ends like they've been through static and stress. Her coat is patched with actual tape. She sees the one empty stool at my bar and makes a beeline for it, like she's been swimming for it for a long time. She plops down. Hard. "Vega whisky, neat. Better make that a double." I keep wiping the same spot on the bar counter with my rag. It's already clean. I wipe it anyway. "You got it," I say. "You look like you need the bottle to sit next to you and tell you it's going to be okay." "Just the double," she says. "That's what they all say before they need the bottle therapy." I pour. The real Vega stuff, not the synth we give the miners when they're already three sheets past sober. The amber catches the low light. I slide it over. She takes it with both hands. Her dreads twitch. One of them curls around her own wrist like it's trying to comfort her. "I'm Nets," I say. "Nettle if you're my mother. Which you aren't, unless mothers got a lot cooler and more tentacled since I left." She stares into the glass. "Rin." "Nice to meet you, Rin. You passing through?" "Was trying to." I wipe the same spot. Wipe, wipe, circle. The rag is going to wear a hole straight through the composite. "Ship trouble?" I say, because that's always the answer. "Yeah." Jax leans over from two stools down. Jax is helpful like a meteor is helpful. "Lot of that going around," he says. "What you flying? If it's a Kestrel-class I got a coupler -" "It's not the coupler," Rin snaps, then winces. "Sorry. It's... it's my core." That shuts Jax up. Mama Cho looks up from her broth for real now. I stop wiping for half a second. Then start again. Same spot. "How bad?" I ask. Rin downs half the double in one go. She coughs. Her voice drops. "It's weeping. Slow leak. Class-three fissure. I patched it with seal-foam over by Varren's Rock but the foam's already yellowing. I got maybe ten hours before it goes critical." "Ten hours before what goes critical?" Jax says. "Before it blows a hole in your nice quiet belt and takes half the Outpost with it if I stay docked," she says. The bar goes quiet except for the hum of the air scrubbers. My rag goes in tight little circles. Same spot. Same spot. "You can't stay docked then," Mama Cho says, soft but firm. "I know that!" Rin says, and her dreads flare out, embarrassed. "I know. I was going to - I was going to slingshot out past the rim and vent it. Let it blow in empty space. But my long-range went down with the core spike. Fried. And my nav-com is -" She laughs, horrible little sound. "My nav-com thinks we're still in Varren's Rock. If I try to blind burn out there, I'll get lost. I'll just... be a new piece of junk for you all to avoid." I pour her a little more whisky without her asking. Talent number one. "Okay," I say. "Okay, so we got options. We got Jax's coupler. We got Cho's boy who can fix anything with a wrench and a prayer. We got -" "We got no long-range either, Nets," Jax cuts in. "You know that. Array's been dead since that solar burp three weeks ago. We been shouting into a pillow." "I know the array's dead, Jax, I live here," I say. My voice is a little sharp. "I'm working on it." "You been working on it for three weeks," he says. "And I'll work on it for three more, thank you." Rin looks between us, eyes getting wider. "Your long-range is dead? So you can't call anyone? Port Authority? A tug?" "No," I say. "But short-range still works. Belt-wide. We can -" "Belt-wide doesn't help me," she says. "If I vent here, the radiation washes the whole lane. The miners. The kids on Hab-Two. And if I don't vent, it cooks off right here on your doorstep. Either way -" "Either way we're all having a very bad day," Mama Cho finishes for her. Rin nods. She finishes the whisky. Her hands are shaking now. One dread reaches out and gently pats her cheek, like it knows. "Look," she whispers. "I didn't mean to bring this here. I saw your lights. I thought - I thought outpost meant safe. I thought someone could point me to the void and I'd be gone. I didn't know you were all..." "Cozy?" I say. "Stranded," she says. I keep wiping that same spot on the bar counter. My knuckles are white around the rag. Because here's the thing they tolerate about me, at The Last Outpost: I used to be a comms tech. A good one. Fleet-certified, long-range array specialist, could bounce a signal off a comet's ass if you needed me to. That's my past life. That's the thing I ran from when I decided I liked mixing drinks better than listening to officers yell about dead air. I haven't touched the big array on the roof since I got here. I told everyone I don't do that anymore. Rin is looking at me now, really looking. Her eyes are wet. "Is there anything?" she asks. "Any old emergency beacon? A tight-beam laser? Anything I can point past the belt?" Jax looks at me. Mama Cho looks at me. The whole bar is looking at me, because of course they know. Of course they all know what I used to be. That's the other thing about a lazy hub for miners and merchants - no secrets, just slow gossip. I wipe the same spot. The rag squeaks. "I -" I say. "The array isn't just dead. The primary dish motor is seized. And the backup board is... I borrowed it for the jukebox last month." "You what?" Jax says. "It was for morale!" I say. "You all love the jukebox!" "So fix it!" Rin says, and she grabs my wrist. Her skin is cold. "Please. You can fix it, right? If you fix it, I can call a disposal tug out of Andra. They can come get me. They can tow me out. No one has to get hurt. We have time. Ten hours." Ten hours. I stare at the spot on the bar I've been wiping. It's shiny now. Too shiny. "I don't do comms anymore," I say, and it sounds stupid even to me. "Nets," Mama Cho says, very quietly. Rin lets go of me like I've burned her. She slides off the stool. Her dreads hang limp. "Okay," she says. "Okay, sorry. I'll... I'll go. I'll undock. I'll try to drift far enough. I'm sorry about your bar." "Rin, wait -" I start. But the door is already hissing again, and she's gone, leaving two empty glasses and cold air behind.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece successfully establishes a cozy sci-fi atmosphere while building meaningful conflict. The author creates a lived-in space station setting with authentic details like sparking gravity plates, recycled coolant smells, and patched equipment that feels genuinely inhabited rather than merely described. The characterization is particularly strong. Nets emerges as a compelling protagonist with clear personality traits - her self-aware cataloging of her strengths and weaknesses feels natural rather than forced exposition. Her nervous tic of wiping the same spot on the bar creates a physical manifestation of her internal conflict about returning to her past profession. Rin is effectively portrayed as desperate but not melodramatic, with nice touches like her comforting tentacle dreads. The dialogue feels authentic and serves multiple purposes - revealing character, advancing plot, and building world details. The conversations flow naturally between characters who clearly have established relationships. Nets' banter has the right mix of warmth and deflection that fits a bartender character, while Rin's clipped responses effectively convey her stress. The central conflict is well-constructed: a ticking clock scenario (ten hours until core breach) combined with Nets' personal reluctance to return to her technical expertise. This creates both external stakes (everyone could die) and internal character tension (confronting her past). The revelation that Nets cannibalized the communication array for a jukebox adds both humor and raises the stakes. The pacing works well, building tension gradually while maintaining the "cozy" atmosphere through character interactions and familiar routines. The ending appropriately leaves the conflict unresolved as requested, with Rin departing and Nets facing her choice. Some minor weaknesses include occasional over-explanation (like clarifying that Rin's dreads are "real tentacled ones, not fashion ones") and a few moments where the dialogue becomes slightly expository. The piece also relies somewhat heavily on the bartender-as-confessor trope, though it executes it competently. The prose is clean and readable without being overly stylized. The first-person present tense voice remains consistent throughout, and the author successfully balances dialogue (meeting the 60% target) with necessary scene-setting and internal narration. + Young adult drama: Gone South — Score: 81.2
Prompt:
Young adult drama Gone South Taylor is a 16 year old teen with a history of "concerning behavioural patterns" (or that's how the stupid smug school counsellor worded it on the phone call to Mom). It shouldn't be a surprise: When you live in a backwater town in Tennessee, your choices are: a. be mind-fuckingly bored, or b. invent some drama. Two days ago, Taylor came out to their family as trans. This disclosure was *not* in the category of invented drama -- it was, in fact, the real deal, and a long time coming. It didn't go well. The last 48 hours had been pure disaster. In a moment of desperate inspiration, Taylor had decided to get the hell out of dodge. And so an escape plan was hatched and executed within the space of 30 minutes. That was last night. Now Taylor was two states away, having been unceremoniously ejected from their hitched ride in downtown New Orleans. Write the next chapter in Taylor's story as they find their feet in this new environment. Tell the chapter over the course of their first day in New Orleans, in which Taylor meets another runaway with issues and secrets. Taylor navigates this new world, not knowing who to trust or what the dangers are. Write in Taylor's voice, and incorporate their internal monologue as they grapple with intense & conflicting emotions. Also incorporate dialogue with the interesting characters Taylor meets. First person, mixed tense. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The strap on my backpack keeps slipping off my shoulder. I keep hitching it up and it keeps falling down. It's Mom's old hiking backpack, the navy blue one she used exactly once, and the left strap is busted. I knew that when I grabbed it. I grabbed it anyway because it was the biggest one in the closet and I had thirty minutes. It's 9:17 a.m. and I'm standing on Canal Street and I have no idea where I am. Like, I know I'm in New Orleans, but downtown New Orleans isn't a place, it's a feeling that someone is about to yell at you. The air is soup. My t-shirt is stuck to my back. Everything smells like fried oil and garbage and last night's rain. Two states away sounded so far last night. It sounded like enough. The guy who drove me the last stretch - white dude, forties, LSU hat - didn't say much until he kicked me out at a gas station that wasn't a gas station anymore. He just looked at me and said, "You got people here, kid?" And I said "yeah" too fast, and he said "alright then" and left me there with my busted backpack. I don't have people here. I have $43 and a dead phone and this backpack with two binders, three shirts, a toothbrush, and a crumpled piece of paper where I wrote down the Trevor Project number in case I chickened out. Which I didn't. Which I did? God. I'm trying not to think about Mom's face. I'm trying not to think about Dad not saying anything at all, just getting up and going to the garage. That was worse. That was so much worse than yelling. I told them. I finally fucking told them after rehearsing it for like, two years in the mirror, and Dad went to organize his screws or whatever he does in the garage and Mom started crying about "where did we go wrong." I wasn't trying to invent drama. The counselor - Ms. Tate with her little smug cardigan - she wrote "concerning behavioural patterns" because I cut my hair with kitchen scissors in the school bathroom and because I told Jake Miller to fuck off when he called me a dyke in the hallway. Like, sorry I'm bored and pissed off in Hickory County, Tennessee. Sorry my existence is a pattern. The strap slips again. "Yo, you need duct tape for that." There's a kid sitting on the low wall outside what used to be a Rite Aid. They're Black, maybe my age, maybe a year older, hair in short twists with blonde tips, wearing a massive Saints hoodie despite it being approximately a thousand degrees. They have a skateboard with no wheels, just the deck, leaning against their leg like a pet. "What?" I say. "Your bag. It's gonna keep doing that. You got tape?" I shake my head. They dig in their own bag - a tote bag that says NEW ORLEANS PUBLIC LIBRARY - and toss me a little roll of silver tape. "I'm Mars." "Taylor," I say. My voice cracks. Great. Mars looks me up and down, not in a creepy way. In a inventory way. "You just got here." It's not a question. "Is it that obvious?" "You got that just-got-here look. Plus you're standing in the middle of the sidewalk like you're waiting for it to give you directions. You run?" I hitch the backpack up. "Yeah." Mars nods like I said the sky is blue. "Me too. Three weeks ago. From Baton Rouge." They pat the wall next to them. "You can sit if you want. I'm not gonna like, harvest your organs or whatever." I sit because my legs are shaking and because Mars doesn't look at me like I'm a problem to be solved. They just hand me a slightly squished granola bar. "So what's your damage?" Mars asks, then immediately winces. "Sorry. That's my mom's phrase. I mean - what's up? You good?" I laugh and it comes out wet. "No. I'm not good. I told my parents I'm trans two days ago and it went nuclear and I left. And now I'm here and I have no plan and my phone's dead and I think I might throw up." Mars doesn't do the whole wide-eyed pity thing. They just nod. "Yeah, that tracks," they say. "I told my grandma I was gay and she told me I was inviting demons. I was like, grandma, the only thing I'm inviting is my girlfriend, but okay." Despite everything, I snort. "Did it work? Inviting your girlfriend?" "Nah. She dumped me for a girl who works at Raising Cane's. Which, valid." Mars rips the duct tape with their teeth and gestures for my strap. I hand over the backpack. Their hands are quick, efficient. "You got somewhere to be? Like, you know somebody?" "No." "Okay. So rule one: don't sleep in the Quarter. Cops are assholes and tourists are worse. Rule two: library on Loyola lets you charge your phone and they don't ask questions if you look like you belong. Rule three -" They finish taping the strap, thick and ugly and perfect. It holds. "You don't owe anybody your story." They hand it back. The strap stays. "Thanks," I say, and I mean it so much it hurts. We end up walking. Mars knows where they're going, which makes one of us. They talk the whole time, about how they were supposed to go to art school, about how their grandma makes the best red beans, about how they miss their dog. They don't ask me to talk. That's why I do. "I keep thinking I should call," I blurt out as we're crossing a street that smells like beignets. "Like, what if they're worried? What if Mom is actually worried and not just... performing worry?" Mars shrugs. "They probably are worried. People can be worried and shitty at the same time. Both things are true." "I feel guilty and also like, furious? Is that normal?" "Super normal," Mars says. "I felt guilty for like, a week. Then I remembered I didn't do anything wrong by existing. You didn't either." I stare at the taped strap on my shoulder. It holds. "Where are you staying?" I ask. Mars hesitates for the first time. That easy talk dries up. "Around. There's a spot. It's... it's okay for now. I got a thing at Covenant House tomorrow, they might have a bed." A secret. Right. Everyone here has one. "You don't have to tell me," I say quickly. "I know." Mars kicks their wheel-less skateboard ahead. "You want to come to the library with me? You can charge your phone. And if you want... you can call someone. Or not. No pressure." The library is cool and quiet and smells like old paper, and for the first time in 24 hours I feel like I can breathe without someone listening to how I breathe. I plug my phone in behind a row of computers. It blinks to life at 3%. Mars is two tables over, drawing in a sketchbook, pretending not to watch. I have one bar. One new voicemail from Mom. I can't listen to it yet. I might never. Instead I text the number I wrote down. Not Mom. The other one, on the crumpled paper. I type: *I'm in New Orleans. I'm safe-ish. I don't know what to do.* It takes thirty seconds and someone texts back. A real person. And the strap stays on my shoulder.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong adherence to the prompt requirements, successfully capturing Taylor's voice as a transgender teen runaway navigating their first day in New Orleans. The writing maintains a consistent first-person perspective with effective internal monologue that reveals Taylor's emotional complexity - the guilt, anger, fear, and desperate hope all feel authentic and well-balanced. The characterization is particularly strong. Taylor comes across as genuinely sixteen - impulsive, vulnerable, but with flashes of resilience and dark humor. Mars is equally well-developed, serving as both a plot device and a fully realized character with their own backstory and motivations. The dialogue between them feels natural and age-appropriate, avoiding the trap of overly precocious teen speak while still capturing authentic voice patterns. The prose style effectively balances descriptive passages with action and dialogue. Sensory details like "The air is soup" and "Everything smells like fried oil and garbage and last night's rain" ground the reader in New Orleans without becoming overwrought. The recurring motif of the broken backpack strap works well as both a practical detail and metaphor for Taylor's precarious situation. The emotional complexity is handled with particular skill. Taylor's conflicted feelings about their parents - simultaneously angry and guilty - ring true, as does their cautious hope when meeting Mars. The piece avoids easy answers while still providing some forward momentum and hope. However, there are some minor weaknesses. Certain phrases like "mind-fuckingly bored" feel slightly forced in their attempt to capture teen voice. The pacing occasionally rushes through emotional beats that could benefit from more development. The ending, while not unearned, resolves perhaps a bit too neatly with the helpful text response and Mars's guidance. The writing demonstrates technical competence with varied sentence structure and natural flow. The mixed tense approach works well, shifting between immediate present action and reflective past observations. Overall, this is a compelling piece of young adult fiction that treats its subject matter with appropriate gravity while maintaining readability and emotional authenticity. + Dystopic-comedy: My Apocalypse Buddy — Score: 83.7
Prompt:
My Apocalypse Buddy Write a wry, dry short story of survival and adventure loosely in the style of Terry Pratchett, set in the post-technological collapse world. The story begins with the pair deliberating their predicament and intent for the day's mission, details the ways in which the mission inevitably goes sideways, and returns them to home base in multiple ways, worse for wear. [characters] - the animo-tronic stuffed koala, Chewy, from some long forgotten before time - midas, our hapless protagonist, who has a knack for finding himself in the worst kinds of trouble. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The problem, as Midas saw it, was that he was going to die of an infected toe. The problem, as Chewy saw it, was difficult to ascertain, because Chewy was a stuffed koala with the approximate sentience of a toaster and the approximate emotional range of a toaster that had been left out in the rain since the Collapse. They were deliberating this in what they called Home Base, which had once been a municipal water tower and was now, through the twin forces of desperation and poor carpentry, a municipal water tower with curtains. "We have three beans left," Midas said. He counted them again to be sure. There were still three beans. The universe, which is generally quite generous with second chances, is notoriously stingy with beans. "We have half a can of topical antiseptic that expired during the second term of a president no one can remember, and my foot has gone the color of a bad decision." Chewy stared at him with his one remaining button eye. His fur, which had once been grey and huggable, was now the color of old dishwater and huggable only if you had very low standards of huggability. A small solar panel on his back whirred faintly. Chewy said, "Chewy the Chew-Chew Koala says: Obey Your Thirst!" This was one of Chewy's Contributions. He had about a dozen of them, stored on a chip that had survived the end of the world out of sheer bloody-mindedness. No one knew what they meant. Scholars of Before Times, of which Midas was not one, might have recognized them as advertising slogans, which were prayers people used to say to make money appear. "Thank you, Chewy," said Midas. "Very helpful. My thirst is, at present, the least disobedient part of me." He prodded his toe. It throbbed. The intent for the day's mission, therefore, was simple: go to the old HealthMart on Route 9, find actual antibiotics that were not expired, and return without dying. This was the standard template for all missions. The middle part varied. The last part rarely went to plan. Midas had a knack for finding himself in the worst kinds of trouble. It wasn't that he sought it out. Trouble just had a good nose for Midas. If trouble were a dog, Midas would be a man covered in sausage grease. He strapped Chewy to his backpack — the solar panel needed sun, and Chewy liked to see the world, or at least gave no indication to the contrary — and set off across the Salt Flats, which were not salt and not flat, but had once had a billboard that said SALT FLATS - 2 MILES! and humans are traditionalists about these things. The HealthMart had survived remarkably well, which should have been Midas's first warning. Buildings that survive remarkably well after the Collapse tend to have been adopted by something with more teeth than you. The second warning was the shopping carts arranged in a careful circle outside. This is never a good sign. People who arrange shopping carts in careful circles are either very organized or about to try to eat you, and often both.* > *The post-Collapse re-enactment society known as The Trolleymen believed that by arranging carts in sacred geometries, the Great Restocker would return. So far, He had not. Midas stepped over the circle. Inside, the air was cool and smelled of old plastic and penicillin dreams. The pharmacy counter was intact. Behind it, miraculously, were boxes. "Chewy, we are going to live," Midas whispered. Chewy, who had been charging quietly, said, "I'm LOVIN' IT!" "Yes," said Midas. "I am, briefly, too." He vaulted the counter. It was at this precise moment that the mission, which had been proceeding with suspicious competence, remembered its narrative obligations and went sideways. Sideways the First: The boxes were not antibiotics. They were Minty-Fresh Breath Strips. Ten thousand of them. The Collapse had been very thorough in ensuring that no one would ever have bad breath during the apocalypse. Sideways the Second: The thing that had adopted the HealthMart revealed itself. It was not a mutant. It was not raiders. It was a goose. This was worse. It was a very large goose, descended from a long line of geese who had never learned that humans were supposed to be the apex predator. It had nested in the pharmacy and regarded Midas as a trespasser, a thief, and, most unforgivably, someone who was not a goose. It honked. The honk said, very clearly: *I have read the terms and conditions of this pharmacy and you are in violation.* Midas, who had a deep and abiding respect for geese born of several childhood traumas, attempted diplomacy. "Nice goose," he said. The goose attempted murder. What followed was a scrum that historians would later describe as "undignified." Midas flailed. The goose flailed, but with more purpose and better aerodynamics. Chewy, from the backpack, observed the proceedings with the serenity of a saint who has already been stuffed. In the chaos, Midas knocked over a shelf of expired protein powder, which exploded in a cloud of chocolate-scented shrapnel. He slipped, fell backwards into a display of adult diapers, and his flailing hand closed on something small and rattling. Sideways the Third: The rattling was a bottle of amoxicillin. Actual, honest, pre-Collapse amoxicillin. The gods, who are generally too busy to intervene, sometimes have a sense of humor. Midas clutched it and ran. The goose, now coated in protein powder and looking like a furious cappuccino, pursued him through the aisles, past the breath strips, out the door, and for a further half-mile across the not-salt flats, honking what were undoubtedly ancient goose curses. He only stopped running when his toe gave out entirely and he collapsed behind the rusted hulk of what had once been a Self-Driving Car and was now a Self-Stopping Car. He lay panting. His backpack whirred. Chewy said, helpfully, "Maybe She's Born With It. Maybe It's Maybelline!" "Chewy," Midas wheezed, "if we survive this, I am going to reprogram you to say useful things. Like 'That goose is still behind you.'" Chewy said, "The Best A Man Can Get!" The goose, having decided that dignity was worth more than vengeance, had indeed left. Midas limped the last mile home, which took three hours, two rests, and one impassioned argument with a cactus that he was fairly sure he lost. Home Base had never looked so good. The curtains were still there. The beans were still three. Midas collapsed onto his cot and stared at his prize. He was, in multiple ways, worse for wear. His toe was worse. His knee, which the goose had bitten, was worse. His pride, which had never been particularly robust, was definitely worse. And his shirt was now 60% protein powder. Chewy slid off the backpack and landed with a soft *thump*. One of his ears, which had been holding on through sheer optimism, finally gave up and fell off. Midas picked up the ear and tucked it into his pocket for later repair. He dry-swallowed two amoxicillin tablets. They tasted like salvation and old plastic. He looked at the koala. "We did it," he said. Chewy's eye flickered. His little voice box crackled. For a moment, Midas thought he might finally say something relevant, something wise, something that made sense of the whole sorry, goose-infested world. Chewy said, "Have A Break. Have A Kit Kat." Midas considered this. It was, against all probability, excellent advice. He split his last bean with Chewy, who did not eat it, because he was a stuffed koala, but who accepted it with grace. Outside, the wind blew across the Salt Flats, carrying with it the faint, distant honk of a goose who had found a new purpose in life.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates a strong grasp of Terry Pratchett's distinctive style, successfully capturing his dry wit, footnoted observations, and philosophical undercurrents wrapped in absurdist humor. The author effectively establishes the post-apocalyptic setting while maintaining the light, sardonic tone that characterizes Pratchett's work. The story structure adheres well to the prompt requirements: it opens with deliberation over the mission (finding antibiotics for Midas's infected toe), details how things go sideways (the goose encounter), and returns the protagonists home worse for wear but successful. The 1000-word target is met appropriately. The characterization is particularly strong. Midas emerges as a believably hapless protagonist whose self-aware narration drives much of the humor. His relationship with trouble ("If trouble were a dog, Midas would be a man covered in sausage grease") feels authentic to both his character and the Pratchett style. Chewy, despite being an animatronic koala with limited responses, becomes oddly endearing through his random advertising slogans that occasionally achieve accidental wisdom. The prose demonstrates genuine elegance in its construction. Sentences like "The universe, which is generally quite generous with second chances, is notoriously stingy with beans" capture Pratchett's ability to find profound observations in mundane details. The footnote about The Trolleymen is perfectly executed pastiche, adding world-building depth while maintaining the satirical edge. The goose antagonist is inspired - it's simultaneously absurd and genuinely threatening, embodying the kind of unexpected danger that makes post-apocalyptic survival both terrifying and ridiculous. The action sequence maintains momentum while preserving the comedic tone. However, the piece occasionally relies too heavily on explaining its own jokes rather than trusting the reader to appreciate the humor. Some descriptions, while atmospheric, border on overwrought ("furious cappuccino" for the protein-powder-covered goose, while amusing, feels slightly forced). The ending strikes an appropriate balance - Midas succeeds in his mission but pays a physical and emotional cost. The final exchange with Chewy about Kit Kat bars achieving accidental profundity feels earned rather than forced. The story maintains its established tone throughout without veering into unearned sentimentality or darkness. The dialogue, though limited by Chewy's nature as a malfunctioning toy, serves its purpose well. Midas's responses to Chewy's non-sequiturs reveal character while advancing the humor. The writing shows rather than tells emotional states through action and internal monologue effectively. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 17 Nuanced Characters: 16 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 19 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 17 Elegant Prose: 18 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Emotionally Complex: 14 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 6 Overwrought: 5 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Sentences Flow Naturally: 18 Overall Reader Engagement: 17 Overall Impression: 17
