+ Physical-spatial: Breaking Formation — Score: 88.8
Prompt:
**"Breaking Formation"** Write a first-person narrative from the perspective of a street dancer from a rough neighborhood who's won a scholarship to an elite K-pop training academy in Seoul. Desperately out of place culturally and technically, they struggle with a militaristically precise choreography that contradicts everything about their improvisational background. The punishing rehearsal takes place under a famous instructor known for breaking students to remake them, who sees potential in the narrator's raw talent but not their attitude. This scene MUST maintain complete physical coherence—each dance move, position shift, and spatial relationship must be tracked with precision throughout the studio. Paint an exceptionally vivid kinetic picture through descriptions that capture not just movement but the contrast between the narrator's fluid, expressive style and the mechanical precision demanded. Their internal monologue should reveal culture shock, class anxiety, and the threatening realization that success here might mean losing their authentic movement voice. Include technical terminology that highlights the collision of street and commercial styles, moments when muscle memory betrays them, and a crucial decision point about whether to conform or rebel. Physical clarity is essential—readers must be able to visualize exactly how bodies move through space, how rhythms align or clash, and how the protagonist's body struggles against unfamiliar discipline. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The floor in Studio A is too clean. Not clean like mopped. Clean like nobody's ever actually lived on it. No scuff, no bottle-ring, no crack where the concrete busted through at Laflin Park. My socks - they gave us these white grip socks, never-worn - squeak every time I shift my weight and I can hear it over the aircon. I'm back row, far left. West wall. That's my spot because I was ten seconds late and because nobody wanted to stand next to the scholarship kid. There are eight of us in two lines of four. Four in front, three feet from Instructor Park's back, four in back, another six feet behind them. We're all facing north, toward the floor-to-ceiling mirror. Park faces south, toward us, his back to his own reflection. He has a black hand fan. When he snaps it shut - *PAK!* - we freeze. He's been famous since before I knew what K-pop was. He breaks them to remake them, the other trainees whisper in the locker room in English for my benefit. The track starts. The same eight counts for the fourth hour. "Five, six, seven, eight!" Count one: right foot slides forward twelve inches on the diagonal, toe pointed, knee locked. Left arm knifes up to a perfect ninety-degree angle at the shoulder, elbow locked, fingers glued together and pointed at the ceiling. That's the move. That's what's on the sheet. What my body wants to do on one is bounce. Drop my weight into my right hip, let my left knee buckle soft, let my shoulder ride the snare. Top rock. Just a little groove to catch the beat before the step. At home that bounce *is* the one. Here it makes me late. I lock the knee. My sock squeaks. Count two: pivot ninety degrees to face east, weight transfer to right foot, left foot snaps closed to meet it, heels together, both arms slice down to frame the jaw - thumbs under chin, elbows flared exactly level with the ears. The front row does it like one animal. Hana in front of me - front row, far left, directly between me and Park - her elbows hit level so fast the air clicks. My elbows are low. I know it before Park says it. My chest wants to do a chest pop on two, not a frame. My muscle memory is from cyphers where you hit *through* the beat, not *on* it. I yank my elbows up and my left heel lifts a half-inch off the floor without permission. I'm wobbling. "Back row! Angles!" Park doesn't look at me. He doesn't have to. Count three and four: two head snaps, chin east to north, north to west, on the hi-hats. The neck must move like a doll's, isolated, no shoulder. My neck has never moved without my shoulders. In my neighborhood you dance with your whole spine. Here, if your trapezius moves you get called sloppy. I do the snaps but my shoulders come with. I see it in the mirror - my reflection behind Hana's, a half-beat behind, my black durag under my beanie that they told me to take off but I didn't, my shoulders rolling like I'm trying to shrug off the building. *PAK!* The fan snaps shut. Everyone freezes exactly where they are. I freeze with my weight on the outside edge of my right foot, left foot hovering two inches off the ground, chin half-turned west, shoulders up by my ears. Hana is a statue. Her right foot forward, left foot back, elbows perfect. She doesn't even breathe hard. Park walks the aisle between the rows. Polished wood, no sound. He stops at my row but looks at the mirror, at my reflection. "You," he says in English. "Chicago. What do you call this?" I know he doesn't mean the choreo. He means the extra thing I'm doing. "Groove," I say. My voice cracks. "Groove is for after you learn to stand," he says. He flicks his fan open. "Your body is very interesting. Very... honest. But honest is not precise. Again, from top. Keep the formation." Formation. That word. At home, formation was the circle that opened when someone stepped in. It breathed. Here formation is grid coordinates. If I'm six inches off my tape mark, I ruin the picture for the camera that isn't even here yet. The track restarts. Five, six, seven, eight! One - I hit the diagonal slide, lock the knee. I kill the bounce. It feels like holding my breath. Two - pivot east, feet together, frame the jaw. I stare at my own eyes in the mirror to stop my shoulders. My elbows are level. For one second I am correct. Three, four - head snaps. East, north, west. I leave my shoulders behind. It hurts, like I'm leaving a body part in another room. Five - this is where they got me. On five we all drop into a deep plié, knees bent out over toes, backs flat, right arm extends straight east, left arm straight west, like a T. Then on six we isolate the chest forward two inches, back two inches. Just the chest. A K-pop body roll without the roll - a tick. My body hates six. My body has been doing body rolls since I was eight on cardboard. When I hear that bass, my hips want to go, my spine wants to wave from sternum to pelvis. Not this robotic piston chest. I drop into the plié on five. My right arm east, left arm west. My knees are shaking. I can see the front row's T is perfectly level, eight arms making one long line across the mirror. Six - I try to push just my chest. But my hips fire. My old muscle memory betrays me. My chest goes, then my abs ripple, then my hips tuck under in a full, fluid body wave that travels down through my knees. It's beautiful. It's wrong. It puts me three inches forward of my tape mark and my right hand dips and almost clips Hana's extended left hand in front of me. *PAK!* Freeze. I'm stuck mid-wave, ass out, back arched, chin down. Everyone else is a perfect T with a two-inch chest pop. In the mirror, I look like a glitch. Park is right behind me now. I can see his shoes in the mirror, black, an inch from my right heel. "Interesting," he says, quiet. "You cannot help it." I am breathing hard and my cheap shirt is sticking to my back. I think about my mama watching the livestream orientation, about the scholarship letter that said "full ride, housing, debut potential," about how much that floor at Laflin Park cost us - nothing - and how much this floor costs - everything. If I can't do this military-precise, knife-angle, no-soul math, I go home. If I *can* do it, I don't know who goes home. "Again," Park says. "But this time, Chicago - show me your dishonest version on purpose. On six. Only on six. Stay on your mark." What? He steps back to the front. "Five, six, seven, eight!" We go. One - slide, lock. Two - pivot, frame. Three, four - snap, snap. Five - plié, T. My arms are east-west. Hana's hand is six inches in front of my face. My tape mark is under my arches. Six - I have a choice. I can do the tick. Two inches forward, two inches back. Stay in formation. Be precise. Be clean. Be one of eight arms making a line. Or I can do what my body already did. The snare hits. I stay rooted. My feet do not move off the tape. My arms stay east-west, perfectly level, not clipping Hana. But from the sternum down, I let the wave go. Not sloppy. Controlled. Chest forward, ribs, hips, a fluid roll that starts and ends inside my own cylinder of space. My shoulders stay still. My mark stays clean. But inside the T, I'm water. In the mirror, the line of arms is still perfect. But my center is alive. *PAK!* The fan. Silence. In the mirror, Park is looking at me - not at my reflection, at my back. Then he looks at the mirror, at my eyes. He doesn't say good. He doesn't say bad. He just nods once, sharp, on the beat, and opens the fan again.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates exceptional technical mastery in fulfilling the prompt's demanding requirements. The author maintains meticulous spatial tracking throughout the dance sequence, with precise positioning details (back row far left, distances between rows, cardinal directions) that create a clear mental map of the studio space. The physical choreography is rendered with remarkable specificity - each count is detailed with exact body positions, angles, and movements that feel authentic to both street dance and K-pop training contexts. The narrative voice is consistently strong and authentic, capturing the protagonist's cultural displacement through concrete details rather than abstract statements. The contrast between the "too clean" floor and the cracked concrete of Laflin Park immediately establishes the cultural divide. The technical dance terminology feels researched and accurate, from "top rock" and "cyphers" to "plié" and "isolation," effectively highlighting the collision between street and commercial styles. The character's internal conflict is rendered through physical sensation and muscle memory rather than exposition. The body's rebellion against the rigid choreography - shoulders that won't isolate, hips that fire automatically, the inability to suppress natural groove - creates genuine tension. The protagonist's voice remains consistent throughout, mixing street vernacular with precise observational detail. The climactic moment where Park asks for the "dishonest version" provides a sophisticated resolution that avoids easy answers. The protagonist's choice to blend styles while maintaining formation suggests adaptation without complete capitulation, though the ending remains appropriately ambiguous about long-term consequences. The prose is lean and purposeful, with strong sensory details (squeaking socks, the fan's "PAK!" sound, the aircon hum) that ground the reader in the space. The writing avoids purple prose while maintaining vivid imagery. The pacing builds effectively through repetition of the eight-count sequence, creating mounting tension. Minor weaknesses include occasional awkward phrasing ("my black durag under my beanie that they told me to take off but I didn't" could be clearer) and some moments where the technical precision slightly overwhelms the emotional core. The piece also could have pushed deeper into the class anxiety mentioned in the prompt, though the cultural displacement is well-handled. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 19 Believable Character Actions: 18 Nuanced Characters: 17 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 19 Elegant Prose: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 18 Emotionally Complex: 16 Coherent: 19 Meandering: 2 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 2 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 3 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 2 Overwrought: 3 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 1 Unearned Transformations: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Sentences Flow Naturally: 17 Overall Reader Engagement: 18 Overall Impression: 18
Scores: Adherence to Instructions: 19.0, Believable Character Actions: 18.0, Nuanced Characters: 17.0, Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18.0, Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 19.0, Elegant Prose: 17.0, Emotionally Engaging: 18.0, Emotionally Complex: 16.0, Coherent: 19.0, Meandering: 2.0, Weak Dialogue: 3.0, Tell-Don't-Show: 2.0, Unsurprising or Uncreative: 3.0, Amateurish: 2.0, Purple Prose: 2.0, Overwrought: 3.0, Incongruent Ending Positivity: 1.0, Unearned Transformations: 2.0, Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17.0, Sentences Flow Naturally: 17.0, Overall Reader Engagement: 18.0, Overall Impression: 18.0
+ Gothic-fantasy-humour: Court of the Dreaming — Score: 86.8
Prompt:
Court of the Dreaming: Territorial Dream Dispute. Write a dark fantasy scene with comedic elements set in the Courts of Dream where a case is being heard to determine ownership of a contested dreamscape. Mora, the Slavic nightmare spirit who sits on sleepers' chests causing paralysis and terror, has recently invaded and subverted a carefully crafted recurring dream of a schoolboy, Max. The dream originally featured the boy lying in a field of wildflowers with his crush, always culminating in a muddled almost-kiss and a pleasant confusion of emotions. This dream was the meticulous work of "First Love" (a rosy-cheeked, slightly bashful entity who crafts formative romantic experiences). However, Mora has recently infiltrated the dreamscape, transforming the scene so that when the girl playfully mounts the boy, she morphs into a terrifying yet somehow still confusingly attractive vision who proceeds to sit on his chest and feed on his breath. Mora's defense largely consists of the practical argument that "a girl's gotta eat too, you know?" Write this scene as it unfolds in the courtroom of the Dreaming, with Dream's demeanour, typically impenetrable, betraying a sense of irritation at having to adjudicate such matters. The comedy should derive from the farcical situation, familiar elements of courtroom drama intersecting with the fantastical world of the Dreaming, and the droll portrayal of the characters and their interactions. The humor should be dark and understated throughout. Dream is tempted to some mischief in the resolution of the case. Use descriptives sparingly when setting scene. Write for readability & engagement. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The Court of the Dreaming was in session. Which was unusual in itself. The Court rarely convened for territorial disputes. Nightmares ate each other all the time. It saved on paperwork. Dream of the Endless sat on the bench. The bench was not a bench so much as a long, low slab of something that resembled cooled starlight and felt like a church pew. His demeanour was impenetrable, as always, which made the faint line between his eyebrows all the more notable. He looked like a man who had been asked to settle a property line dispute between raccoons. "Let the record show," he said, "that I am awake enough for this." Beside the bench, the Bailiff inhaled. The Bailiff was seven feet tall and made entirely of folded-up bad news. The Bailiff opened his mouth and the room shook. **"THE HONORABLE COURT CALLS THE PLAINTIFF: FIST LOAF!"** A small entity in the witness box flinched. "First Love, actually," said First Love. First Love was rosy-cheeked and slightly bashful, as advertised. He looked about sixteen and as if he had been knitted out of letterman jackets and unsent notes. He clutched a small, wilting bouquet of wildflowers that had been grown specifically for one boy's dream. "Apologies," said Dream, not apologizing. "Proceed." First Love cleared his throat. "Thank you, my lord. For three years I have tended the recurring dream of one Maxwell Hargrove, aged fourteen. Designation: Field / Almost-Kiss / Variant 7B. It is a formative piece." He gestured, and the air above the court showed it: a boy lying in a field. A girl from his math class lying beside him. Sunlight. The particular, agonizing length of a teenage afternoon. She laughs, leans in. He leans in. The dream gets muddled, as all such dreams do, right before contact. The boy wakes up with pleasant confusion and a new understanding of what his stomach is for. "It is delicate work," First Love said earnestly. "The almost is the whole point. The confusion must be pleasant. The yearning must be -" "Objection," said a voice from the defense bench. "Boring." Mora was lounging. She was difficult to look at directly, which was part of the job. Long black hair that moved wrong. Too many joints in her fingers. A smile that was beautiful in the way a knife is beautiful when it's at your throat. She wore what looked like a nightgown made of held breath. "My lord," Mora said, stretching her legs. "He had it for three years. Three years of lying in a field. Nothing happening. The boy is fourteen. At some point, someone has to sit on his chest." First Love went pinker. "That is not - the sitting is metaphorical -" "It wasn't," Mora said. "Now it is literal." The Bailiff, sensing his moment, inhaled again. **"THE COURT CALLS THE DEFENDANT: MOO-RAH! THE CHEST-SITTER! ALSO CALLED, UH, MORAH? MORA? MORE-UH?"** "Just Mora is fine," said Mora. **"JUST-MORA-IS-FINE!"** the Bailiff boomed, writing it down as her legal name. Dream pinched the bridge of his nose. It is not often noted that the Lord of Dreams can get a headache. He was getting one now. "Mora," Dream said. "You are accused of incursion, subversion, and unlicensed feeding in a crafted dreamscape. How do you plead?" Mora picked at a nail. "Guilty, I guess. But with context." "Context being?" She stood and swayed to the center of the court. When she moved, the temperature dropped a degree. "My lord, look at the market. Everyone's anxious. The good fear is drying up. Kids don't have night hags anymore, they have climate graphs. I have to diversify. I found a lovely, warm, pre-heated dreamscape. Boy already supine. Girl already on top of him -" "He was not - she was beside him!" First Love squeaked. "She was leaning," Mora corrected. "I helped her commit. Now when she playfully mounts him - which, let's be honest, is what fourteen-year-old boys want to happen in a field - she becomes me. Or, you know. Me-ish. Still pretty, but now with extra teeth and eyes that reflect too much. And then I sit on his chest and feed on his breath. It's very intimate. He wakes up terrified and, and I quote his own sleeping mind, 'weirdly into it.' Which is growth." First Love made a small, wounded noise. "You corrupted formative romantic yearning into erotic asphyxiation panic!" "And?" "And that's my jurisdiction! First kisses, first hand-holding, first prolonged eye contact that ruins your week! Not - not whatever goth-breath-play you are doing!" Mora shrugged. "A girl's gotta eat too, you know?" A murmur ran through the gallery. A parliament of half-formed anxieties rustled. A dog that was also a train whistled low. Dream looked at the projection of Max's dream. In Mora's version, the wildflowers had gone black at the edges. The girl smiled, and her smile kept going. She swung a leg over Max, and her hair fell like ink, and Max's eyes went wide with that specific, paralyzed, adolescent terror that is half nightmare and half revelation. His chest stopped moving. It was, Dream had to admit, technically impressive. The emotional confusion was no longer pleasant. It was exquisite. "Maxwell's waking life?" Dream asked. "Improved," said First Love quickly. "He is now too nervous to talk to his crush, which is developmentally appropriate." "Worsened," said Mora, equally quickly. "He is now too nervous to sleep, which is calorically appropriate for me." Dream folded his hands. "I see. So we have a carefully tended garden of first love, now occupied by a squatter who eats the gardener's compost and the garden." "Symbiosis," Mora said. "Vandalism," First Love said. Dream was silent for a long moment. The kind of silence that makes lesser dreams check if they still exist. He was, in truth, irritated. Not at Mora. Mora was doing what Mora does, as foxes do. He was irritated at having to care about one boy's field. There were empires collapsing in the waking world. There were poets dying unpublished. And here he was, adjudicating whether a child's almost-kiss should end in a kiss or a feeding. The irritation gave him an idea. A small, unkind, very tempting idea. The kind of idea that is the exclusive property of older siblings and gods. "The Dreaming is not a place of rigid ownership," Dream said at last. "It is a place of resonance. This dreamscape was built on a very particular frequency: longing, confusion, pressure on the chest one cannot name." First Love nodded eagerly. Mora tilted her head. "Therefore," Dream said, "I find in favor of... continued joint tenancy." Both entities stared. "What?" they said in unison. "The field remains," Dream decreed. "First Love will continue to craft the approach. The sunlight, the wildflowers, the girl's laugh. Mora will retain her... improvement. The mounting, the morphing, the sitting. The feeding will be limited to three breaths per night, non-lethal. And to ensure cooperation -" He smiled. It was a small thing, and terrible. "- you will share the form. The girl will be both. At once." First Love went white. "My lord, you can't mean -" "I do. She will be terrifying and tender. Bashful and hungry. She will want to kiss him and also to hollow him out like a gourd. He will wake up every morning more confused than any fourteen-year-old boy has ever been in the history of confusion, which is saying something. It will be formative." Mora blinked. Then, slowly, she grinned, showing all her teeth. "Oh. Oh, I like that. That's awful." First Love buried his face in his wildflowers. "He's going to need so much therapy." "He was going to need therapy anyway," Dream said, standing. The courtroom began to dissolve at its edges, as courtrooms in dreams tend to do. "He liked a girl who sits in front of him in math. That never ends well." The Bailiff, sensing dismissal, took one last, enormous breath. **"ALL RISE FOR THE HONORABLE LORD - UH - DREAM! DREAMY! MR. SAND-MAN!"** "Close enough," said Dream, and was gone. In the waking world, Max Hargrove woke up at 6:42 a.m. with his heart hammering, his chest sore, and the absolute certainty that he was in love and also going to die. He stared at his ceiling for a long time. Somewhere behind his eyes, two entities were learning to share an apartment.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong adherence to the prompt's requirements, successfully creating a dark fantasy courtroom scene with comedic elements set in Neil Gaiman's Sandman universe. The author captures Dream's characteristically aloof demeanor while showing subtle irritation, and effectively balances the absurd bureaucratic elements with the fantastical setting. The characterizations are particularly well-executed. Dream maintains his canonical voice - formal, slightly condescending, and prone to cosmic irritation over mundane matters. First Love is portrayed as earnest and protective of his craft, while Mora is pragmatic and unapologetic about her nature. The Bailiff's incompetent booming announcements provide consistent comic relief without becoming tiresome. The dialogue carries much of the story's weight effectively. Exchanges like Mora's casual "A girl's gotta eat too, you know?" and Dream's dry observations create authentic character voices while advancing both plot and humor. The courtroom banter feels natural despite the fantastical circumstances. The prose style is appropriately restrained, following the prompt's instruction to use descriptives sparingly. Phrases like "made entirely of folded-up bad news" and "knitted out of letterman jackets and unsent notes" are economical yet evocative. The writing maintains readability while creating vivid imagery. The resolution demonstrates creativity in Dream's mischievous solution - forcing the entities to share the dreamscape creates a genuinely unsettling outcome that serves both characters' needs while punishing them for bothering him. This feels true to Dream's character and provides a satisfying, if dark, conclusion. The humor successfully derives from the intersection of mundane legal proceedings with dream logic, as requested. The comedy remains understated and dark throughout, never undermining the fantastical elements or becoming overly broad. Some minor weaknesses include occasional moments where the pacing slows slightly during exposition, and a few instances where the humor could be more tightly integrated. However, these are minimal issues in an otherwise well-crafted piece that successfully fulfills all major requirements of the prompt. + Fanfic-crossover: Blood Moon — Score: 78.8
Prompt:
Blood Moon Alucard & Yennefer Write a story where Alucard (Hellsing) encounters Yennefer (The Witcher) in medieval Europe. Yennefer's magical abilities intrigue the vampire, while she's both repulsed and fascinated by his monstrous nature. Their power dynamic shifts constantly as they circle each other, neither willing to show weakness first. **Alucard (Hellsing)** Alucard is an ancient, immensely powerful vampire who works for the Hellsing Organization. He can regenerate from almost any wound and shape-shift at will. He's sadistic, arrogant, and takes pleasure in toying with his enemies before killing them. Despite being a monster, he despises weak vampires who can't accept their nature. He wears a distinctive red coat, wide-brimmed hat, and orange sunglasses. He uses massive handguns loaded with silver bullets. He seeks worthy opponents who can challenge him and potentially end his immortal existence. Famous quote: "The bird of Hermes is my name, eating my wings to make me tame." **Yennefer of Vengerberg (The Witcher)** Yennefer is a powerful sorceress who magically altered her appearance to hide her congenital hunchback. She's ambitious, stubborn, and manipulative when necessary. She has violet eyes and smells of lilac and gooseberries. Despite her cold exterior, she desperately wants to reverse her infertility caused by magical transformation. She's centuries old but appears young. She's politically savvy and refuses to be underestimated. She has a sarcastic sense of humor and little patience for fools. Though she rarely shows it, she's capable of deep love and loyalty. Famous quote: "I'm not sweet and I'm not a maiden in distress in need of saving." Third person, present tense. Length: 1000 words.
Model Output:
The blood moon hangs low and swollen over the Carpathians, painting the snow in rust. It is too large. It bleeds light across a black forest where no birds call. A pack of distant wolves howls, then falls silent, then howls again at irregular intervals, and the sound makes the cold feel deeper. Yennefer of Vengerberg stands in a clearing where her portal was supposed to open. It did not. The chaos slipped. The spell tore instead of folded, spitting her out into mud and dead leaves that stink of rot under the moon. Her black dress is spattered. Her violet eyes flash with annoyance. She smells lilac and gooseberries and ozone, and underneath it, blood. "You are lost, little witch," a voice says from behind her. She does not turn at once. That is weakness. She lets the chaos gather at her fingertips, violet sparks catching the red light, and then she turns. He is tall. Impossibly tall. He wears a long red coat that should be absurd in this age, a wide-brimmed hat that shadows his face, and orange-tinted glasses that reflect the moon. His hair is black and long. His smile is too wide, too sharp. In his hands are two monstrous pistols, too large for any man to wield. He looks like a lord who has mistaken a massacre for a masquerade. Yennefer looks at him and feels her stomach turn, not from fear, but from the sheer wrongness of him. He is dead and not dead. He is a void wrapped in flesh. "What are you?" she asks, her voice cold, edged with sarcasm. "A peasant's idea of a demon? You are over-dressed." Alucard grins wider. He is intrigued. Most humans scream. This one smells of power and perfume and old pain. Her heart beats fast, but not from terror alone. Her power hums like a storm front. "Oh, she speaks," he says, his voice velvet and cruel. "The bird of Hermes is my name, eating my wings to make me tame. I am Alucard. I am the impaler. I am the dragon's son. And you, sorceress, you reek of stolen youth." Her hand snaps up. The air compresses. The ground under his feet erupts. A spear of earth and ice lances up through his chest, impaling him, shattering ribs, driving him a foot into the air. Blood, black in the moonlight, sprays the snow. Yennefer watches, chin lifted. "I'm not sweet and I'm not a maiden in distress in need of saving. Remember that before you bleed on my boots." Alucard hangs on her spell, pinned. Then he laughs. It is a terrible sound. He grips the frozen spear with white hands and pulls himself down further onto it, the ice grinding through his spine. He brings his face close to hers. His glasses slip down his nose and she sees his eyes — ancient, red, amused. "Fascinating," he whispers. He dissolves. Not falls. Dissolves. Into shadow, into a swarm of bats that shriek and scatter, into mist that reforms behind her. The ice spear collapses. Yennefer spins, chaos already flaring, and her palm meets his chest. A concussive blast of pure force throws him back through three trees. Bark explodes. Trunks snap. He lands on his feet. His coat is torn. His chest is a smoking crater. Inside, something writhes, teeth and eyes and howling souls stitching muscle back together. In a second, he is whole. Now he is the one fascinated. Sorcery in this century is hedge tricks and prayers. This woman bends reality with a thought. She does not beg God. She commands. "You heal like a cockroach," Yennefer says, but her breath is shorter now. Revulsion curls in her throat at the sight of his regeneration, at the sheer obscenity of a thing that refuses to die. And beneath it, a hot, unwelcome curiosity. She is centuries old and she has never seen anything like him. He is an abomination that has accepted his abomination. He wears it like her own beauty — a crafted armor. "And you," Alucard says, stepping closer, and each step is deliberate, predatory, "you hide. I smell it under the lilac. The bent spine. The barren womb. You carved yourself into a pretty lie." Her violet eyes go flat and deadly. The wolves howl again, closer this time, irregular, anxious. The air drops twenty degrees. Yennefer does not shout. She whispers, and the word is older than the forest. Lightning, violet-white, rips down from a clear sky and strikes Alucard square in the chest. It holds him, lifting him, burning him to ash from the inside out. He burns, and burns, and does not scream. He smiles through charred teeth. "You cannot kill what already revels in death," he says, his voice crackling like burning paper. His ash reforms. His coat knits itself back together. He is suddenly in front of her, too close, one cold finger under her chin, tilting her face up. His other gun is pressed lightly against her temple. The silver smells sharp. She does not flinch. She lets him touch her, and the chaos at her fingertips turns to fire that licks up his arm, blackening his glove, eating his flesh. He does not pull away. She does not lower the flame. They stand like that, locked, moon-red light painting them both monsters. His monstrosity is open, hungry, honest. Hers is elegant, contained, furious. "Do it then," she hisses. "Shoot me, leech. See if your bullet is faster than my curse." "Do it then," he echoes, delighted. "Burn me, witch. See if your fire can consume an ocean." Neither moves. The power shifts again. He could pull the trigger. She could turn his brain to glass inside his skull. Both know it. Neither is willing to show weakness first, to be the one who blinks. A wolf howls, long and lonely, and then another answers out of rhythm. Yennefer is the first to step back, but she makes it look like dismissal. She flicks her wrist and the fire dies. "You bore me. You are strong and you are stupid, like most men who live too long." Alucard lowers his gun, but his grin never fades. He bows, a mockery of a gentleman's bow, his hat in his hand. "And you are cruel and lonely, like most women who refuse to die. You intrigue me, Yennefer of Vengerberg. I have not been intrigued in a very long time." He turns, coat flaring, and walks into the trees. He does not disappear. He lets her watch him go, because that is also a kind of dominance. The shadows swallow him slowly. Yennefer stands alone under the blood moon, breathing hard, her hands still shaking with residual chaos. The snow where he stood is unmarked. No footprints. Only the faint, coppery smell of blood that is not blood. She hates that her pulse is racing. She hates that part of her wants to follow. She opens her hands and begins to weave a new portal, the violet light reflecting in her eyes, and behind her, the wolves howl again at irregular intervals, as if they too do not know what has just passed through their forest.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong technical writing with atmospheric world-building and character work that largely succeeds in capturing the essence of both Alucard and Yennefer. The author establishes an immediately compelling setting with the blood moon and creates genuine tension through the power dynamic between these two formidable characters. The characterization is notably strong. Alucard's sadistic pleasure, regenerative abilities, and philosophical musings about death are well-captured, particularly in lines like "You cannot kill what already revels in death." Yennefer's pride, magical prowess, and refusal to show weakness come through clearly, especially when she makes stepping back look like dismissal rather than retreat. Both characters maintain their essential natures while engaging in this deadly dance. The prose quality is generally high, with evocative imagery like "He looks like a lord who has mistaken a massacre for a masquerade" and "He is a void wrapped in flesh." The writing maintains consistent third-person present tense throughout and creates a gothic atmosphere appropriate to both source materials. The dialogue feels authentic to both characters, capturing Alucard's theatrical cruelty and Yennefer's sharp wit. The mirrored exchange of "Do it then" effectively shows their mutual refusal to back down first. However, the piece has some weaknesses. Certain descriptions veer toward purple prose, particularly in the opening paragraphs with phrases like "bleeds light across a black forest." Some metaphors feel overwrought, such as the extended description of Alucard's regeneration. The pacing occasionally slows due to dense descriptive passages that could be more economical. The story structure works well as a complete encounter, building tension through escalating magical and physical confrontations before ending on an appropriately unresolved note that maintains both characters' dignity. The wolves howling "at irregular intervals" creates an effective recurring motif that reinforces the unsettling atmosphere. The piece successfully avoids common pitfalls like having either character definitively win or lose, which would have been incongruent with their established power levels. The mutual fascination and repulsion feels earned given their respective natures. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 17 Nuanced Characters: 16 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 17 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 16 Elegant Prose: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Emotionally Complex: 15 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 6 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 8 Overwrought: 7 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Sentences Flow Naturally: 15 Overall Reader Engagement: 16 Overall Impression: 16

+ Fanfic-crossover: Prison Strings — Score: 81.3
Prompt:
Prison Strings Jolyne Cujoh & Makima Write a story where Jolyne Cujoh (JoJo's Bizarre Adventure) and Makima (Chainsaw Man) cross paths at a supernatural prison facility. Jolyne is immediately suspicious of Makima's falsely sweet demeanor, while Makima is intrigued by Jolyne's Stand ability. Their battle of wits escalates dangerously as Jolyne realizes how manipulative Makima truly is. **Jolyne Cujoh (JoJo's Bizarre Adventure)** Jolyne is the daughter of Jotaro Kujo and the first female JoJo protagonist. Her Stand, Stone Free, allows her to unravel her body into string that she can control. She's hot-headed, foul-mouthed, and doesn't take disrespect from anyone. She was framed for murder and sent to prison. She has a distinctive butterfly tattoo on her arm and wears her hair in two buns. Despite her tough exterior, she's deeply loyal to her friends and family. She can be impulsive but is highly adaptable in battle. She inherited her father's ability to stay cool under pressure but is more emotionally expressive. Famous quote: "If there's no Mickey, this shit ain't Disney!" **Makima (Chainsaw Man)** Makima appears as a young woman with auburn hair and ringed yellow eyes. She's the Control Devil who can dominate anyone she considers beneath her (nearly everyone). She maintains a pleasant, soft-spoken demeanor that masks her manipulative, cold nature. She can create contracts with humans and devils, often exploiting their desires. She's obsessed with Chainsaw Man and wants to control him. She keeps numerous people and devils as pets on leashes. She's practically immortal due to a contract with the Prime Minister. She views humans as lesser beings but mimics human emotions perfectly. Famous quote: "It's okay. Dogs are supposed to die for their owners." Third person, present tense. Length: 1000 words.
Model Output:
The supernatural wing of Green Dolphin Street doesn't officially exist. On paper, Block X is solitary overflow. In reality, it's where they put the women who can walk through walls, or set their cellmates on fire by looking at them, or make the guards forget their own names. Jolyne Cujoh sits on the concrete bench in the Block X laundry room and unravels. A thin blue thread slips from the seam of her forearm, right over the edge of her butterfly tattoo, and snakes down into the industrial washer to fish out a sock that got stuck. Her hair is still up in its two buns, even though half the prison has tried to cut it off. Her jaw is tight. She has been in this block for three days since the incident with the plant-devil in the cafeteria, and she already hates it. The door buzzes. It shouldn't. Laundry hour is over and the guard on X never opens the door early. The new girl walks in. She has auburn hair in a neat braid, a prison uniform that somehow looks pressed, and those eyes — ringed yellow, like a target. She is smiling. The kind of smile teachers give you before detention. Behind her, in the hallway, the security camera in the corner stutters. Its red light blinks, goes black, then blinks again. Jolyne has heard the rumor from the girl in the next cell who can hear electricity: cameras malfunction whenever Makima walks past. "Oh," Makima says softly, looking right at Jolyne. "You must be Jolyne. I've heard so much about you." Jolyne's string snaps back into her arm with a *thwip*. She stands up. "Yeah? Who the fuck are you supposed to be, Miss Congeniality?" Makima doesn't flinch at the language. Her smile just widens a fraction. She holds her plastic laundry basket perfectly with both hands, even though it's empty. "I'm Makima. I just transferred. I was told you could help me. You seem very capable." Jolyne narrows her eyes. Every instinct her father ever drilled into her screams. This woman is wrong. The air around her is too still. The other women in Block X radiate — fear, rage, power leaking everywhere. Makima radiates nothing, like a hole cut in the shape of a woman. "Capable of what? Doing your laundry? Fuck off. If there's no Mickey, this shit ain't Disney. I'm not your prison big sis." Makima tilts her head. That sweet, patient demeanor doesn't crack. If anything, it gets sweeter. "Your ability," Makima says, and her yellow eyes drop to Jolyne's forearm where the string disappeared. "It's beautiful. You turn yourself into string. I've never seen anything like it. Is it a contract? A devil?" Jolyne goes cold. Nobody in Block X should have seen that. She was careful. "It's a Stand," Jolyne snaps. "Stone Free. And it's none of your damn business." "A Stand," Makima repeats, tasting the word. "So it has a will of its own, but obeys only you. How... loyal." She takes a step closer. The camera behind her dies completely. No red light at all. Jolyne doesn't back up. She lets a few inches of string unravel from her knuckles, invisible to anyone who isn't looking. "Stay the fuck back," Jolyne says. "I know your type. You walk in here with that fake-ass smile and that soft voice and everyone thinks you're an angel. I've met bitches like you on the outside. You think everyone else is stupid." Makima's smile finally thins. Not disappears — thins, like someone wiping condensation off glass to reveal something colder underneath. "You're perceptive," Makima says. "That's good. Most people aren't. Most people need to be told what to do." She sets her empty basket down. Her hands are free now. "I can get you out of here, Jolyne," she says, her voice still soft, conversational. "This place isn't meant for you. You were framed, weren't you? For murder? I have friends in high places. The Prime Minister, actually. I could have your record cleared by tonight. You could see your father again." Jolyne's fists clench. The strings around them tighten. "How do you know about my dad?" "I know a lot of things," Makima says. "I know you're angry. I know you want to hurt the people who put you here. I know you'd do anything for the people you love. That's a very... dog-like quality. Loyal." And there it is. The word lands like a slap. Jolyne feels it instantly — the way Makima looks at her isn't like a person looking at another person. It's like a person looking at a stray and deciding if it's worth keeping. "You think I'm your fucking dog?" Jolyne snarls. "I think you could be a very good dog," Makima says gently. "All you have to do is listen. Let me borrow your Stand for a moment. Just a little string. Just around your neck, to show you mean it. It's okay." The air changes. There is a pressure now, invisible and immense, pushing down on Jolyne's shoulders, whispering *kneel, obey, be useful*. It's not a Stand. It's older, worse. It smells like a church with no God in it. Jolyne grits her teeth and her knees buckle an inch before she catches herself. "Fuck. You." She moves. Stone Free explodes from her arms — a rush of blue string that tears her sleeves and fills the laundry room in a net. In half a second, Makima is wrapped wrist to ankle, strings biting into her uniform, a garrote loop already around her throat. Jolyne yanks, pulling Makima forward until they are face to face. Makima doesn't struggle. She doesn't even blink. The string cuts into her neck and a thin line of blood wells up — and then stops, as if it forgot how to bleed. "See?" Makima whispers, blood on her lip, still smiling that falsely sweet smile. "You're strong. But strength without direction is just noise. If you kill me here, the guards will put you in the pit for a month. If you obey me, you walk out tomorrow. Which is smarter?" "You're not giving me orders," Jolyne hisses. The butterfly on her arm seems to strain. "You're not my owner. You're just a creepy bitch who thinks she can control everyone because she talks nice." She tightens the string. Makima's head tilts at an unnatural angle. The light in her ringed eyes doesn't dim. "It's okay," Makima says, her voice hoarse but calm, almost comforting. "Dogs are supposed to die for their owners. But you don't have to die. You just have to sit." The pressure spikes. Jolyne's vision swims. For a heartbeat, she can see it — dozens of faint, translucent leashes extending from Makima's back, stretching through the walls of the prison, out into the world, each one attached to something howling. Jolyne understands all at once how manipulative this woman truly is. She doesn't need to shout. She doesn't need to threaten. She just makes you think that obeying her was your idea. That being her pet is safety. That realization makes Jolyne angrier than any insult. "Stone Free!" She doesn't choke Makima. She unravels her own shirt into a thousand strings and uses them to sew Makima's mouth shut. A crude, tight cross-stitch of blue thread right over those too-soft lips. Then she slams Makima back into the washing machines, hard enough to dent the metal. Makima's eyes widen — just a fraction. Surprise. Intrigue. Delight. The laundry door buzzes violently. Guards shouting. The camera light snaps back on, red and steady. Jolyne steps back, breathing hard, strings retracting back into her skin, leaving Makima stitched and pinned. Makima, with her mouth sewn shut, starts to laugh. It's muffled, but Jolyne hears it. It's not angry. It's pleased, like she just found a new breed she hasn't trained yet. Jolyne points a shaking, string-covered finger at her. "You stay the fuck away from me, you hear me? I don't care what kind of devil you are. You don't get to control me." Makima just looks at her with those terrible, loving, yellow-ringed eyes, and even with her mouth sewn shut, Jolyne can hear what she would say if she could speak. *We'll see.*
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong technical execution and character understanding within the crossover premise. The author successfully captures both characters' essential traits - Jolyne's hot-headed defiance and Makima's manipulative control - while placing them in a supernatural prison setting that feels organic to both universes. The characterization is particularly strong. Jolyne's voice rings authentic with her crude language, impulsive nature, and fierce independence. The author effectively shows her inherited Joestar instincts through her immediate suspicion of Makima. Makima's portrayal is equally compelling, maintaining her soft-spoken demeanor while revealing the predatory nature beneath. The dialogue feels natural to both characters, especially Jolyne's profanity-laden responses and Makima's deceptively gentle manipulation attempts. The prose quality is consistently high, with vivid imagery that enhances the atmosphere. Descriptions like "ringed yellow, like a target" and "like a hole cut in the shape of a woman" effectively convey Makima's unsettling presence. The supernatural elements are well-integrated, from the malfunctioning cameras to the visualization of Makima's metaphysical leashes. The pacing builds tension effectively, escalating from initial suspicion to open confrontation. The author demonstrates good understanding of both power systems - Stands and devil contracts - without over-explaining them. The battle of wits feels earned, with Jolyne's resistance to Makima's control being both psychologically and supernaturally grounded. However, some elements feel slightly overwrought, particularly in the climactic moments where the metaphysical pressure and leash imagery becomes somewhat heavy-handed. The ending, while dramatically satisfying, relies on familiar beats of defiant resistance that don't surprise. The piece succeeds in creating genuine tension and maintains consistent character voices throughout. The supernatural prison setting provides an effective backdrop that doesn't overshadow the character dynamics. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 17 Nuanced Characters: 16 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 17 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 16 Elegant Prose: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Emotionally Complex: 14 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 2 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 5 Overwrought: 6 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Sentences Flow Naturally: 16 Overall Reader Engagement: 16 Overall Impression: 16
Scores: Adherence to Instructions: 18.0, Believable Character Actions: 17.0, Nuanced Characters: 16.0, Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 17.0, Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 16.0, Elegant Prose: 15.0, Emotionally Engaging: 16.0, Emotionally Complex: 14.0, Coherent: 18.0, Meandering: 3.0, Weak Dialogue: 2.0, Tell-Don't-Show: 4.0, Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6.0, Amateurish: 3.0, Purple Prose: 5.0, Overwrought: 6.0, Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2.0, Unearned Transformations: 2.0, Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16.0, Sentences Flow Naturally: 16.0, Overall Reader Engagement: 16.0, Overall Impression: 16.0
+ Scp: Notes from the Future — Score: 79.1
Prompt:
Write an SCP report about a researcher who begins finding handwritten notes from their future self, warning about a containment breach that hasn't happened yet. The notes become increasingly desperate and incoherent, sometimes containing information that contradicts known reality. Include the Foundation's attempts to determine if this is an actual temporal anomaly or psychological manipulation by a contained entity, and the researcher's struggle to trust their own perception. First person (researcher's perspective with interspersed notes), past tense. Length: 1000 words.
Model Output:
**Item #:** SCP-XXXX **Object Class:** Euclid **Special Containment Procedures:** SCP-XXXX is currently understood to be a localized temporal or psychoactive phenomenon centered on Desk-03 in Research Wing C of Site-19. The desk and its immediate 5m radius are to be monitored continuously via hardened temporal and electromagnetic sensors. All personnel assigned to SCP-XXXX are to undergo weekly psychiatric evaluation. **Description:** SCP-XXXX refers to a series of handwritten notes appearing spontaneously on the aforementioned desk. **Addendum XXXX-1: Personal Log of Dr. Evelyn Crowe - Compiled Post-Incident** I was assigned to Site-19's low-interaction wing six months ago. My primary project was SCP-055's anti-memetic documentation protocols, which meant I spent most of my days alone in Research Wing C. That isolation was why I noticed it immediately. On 03/11, I returned from the break room and found a folded piece of standard Foundation notepaper on my desk. I had not left it there. My handwriting was on it. > *Evelyn - It's you. Check containment logs for SCP-173 at 02:00 on 03/14. The latch fails. Don't let Chen do the solo check. - E* I had never written it. The paper was yellowed at the edges, as if it had been handled for months. I logged it with Security as a prank and forgot about it until 03/14. At 02:17 on 03/14, the electronic latch on SCP-173's containment chamber faulted. It was a minor, redundant-systems failure, fixed in twelve minutes. Chen was on rotation that night. He was not scheduled for a solo check, but he would have been if Dr. Albrecht hadn't called in sick. The log matched the note to the minute. I requested the security footage for my office. That was when I saw the first glitch. The camera covering Research Wing C, CAM-C-04, had a perfect, 1.4-second dropout at 11:42 on 03/11. One frame my desk was empty, the next the note was there. No one entered. The timestamp was corrupted with static. Maintenance said it was a power fluctuation. The second note appeared three days later. Same glitch. 2.1 seconds this time. > *It happened again. You didn't stop it. The breach isn't 173, that's just the start. When the lights go red, DO NOT go to the stairwell. Use the service tunnel. You broke your ankle last time. - E* The handwriting was still mine, but hurried. The pressure on the pen was heavier. I took it to Dr. Glass in Psych. He was kind about it. He told me that researchers under anti-memetic load sometimes experienced dissociative fugue states. He suggested I was writing the notes myself and forgetting. He ordered a cognitive workup and had Temporal Anomalies install a chroniton detector on my desk. The detector never registered anything above background. But the Foundation did not dismiss me. Temporal Affairs flagged the 03/14 prediction as a Class-2 Precognitive Event and opened a quiet investigation. Their working theory was that I was the anomaly. The third note destroyed that theory, and my sleep. > *You have to listen. Site-19 is not on lockdown, it's FLOODED. The lower levels are gone. I am writing this from the observation deck above the cafeteria. The water is black and it has things in it. Tell Daniels his daughter is still alive in there. Tell him to look. DO NOT TRUST THE ANNOUNCEMENTS.* I had never been to the observation deck above the cafeteria. There is no observation deck above the cafeteria. Site-19 is in a desert. And Daniels, our head of security, does not have a daughter. He told me so himself when I asked, looking at me like I was already compromised. That was the first contradictory detail, and it was not the last. I began to keep a second, private log. I was terrified of my own desk. I started to doubt whether I was remembering things correctly. Did Daniels have a daughter? I checked the personnel files. He didn't. But the note had been so certain. I started to check other things obsessively - the date, my own name on my ID badge. The Foundation's investigation split in two directions. Half of Temporal Affairs thought I was experiencing a genuine bootstrap paradox, receiving information from a future timeline that was overwriting itself with each note. The other half, led by Dr. Liao, thought it was SCP-055. Or SCP-012. Or something else in the wing that was using my handwriting to manipulate me. SCP-055 was infamous for making people *think* they knew things. They tested me for memetic influence. They put me in front of SCP-012's composition for thirty seconds. Negative. They were thorough. It did not help. The notes kept coming. Every two to three days. Always the glitch. Always my handwriting, getting worse. > *ITS NOT WATER I WAS WRONG ITS STATIC ITS THE SAME STATIC FROM THE CAMERAS. you left me here. you left us here. the breach is YOU. you are the breach Evelyn. stop writing. stop WRITING.* > *03/14 never happened. There was no latch failure. Chen is dead. Chen has been dead for two years. Why do you keep writing Chen? WHO IS CHEN???* Chen was standing in the hallway when I read that one. Alive. He asked if I was alright. I could not answer. I had worked with Chen for three years. I had just had coffee with him that morning. The note said he had been dead for two years, and for a horrible second, I believed the note over my own eyes. That was the worst part. I could no longer trust my own perception. Every time I read a new note, I had to cross-reference reality. Was the sky blue? Was Site-19 flooded? Was Daniels' non-existent daughter alive? I started to feel like the notes were more real than the world around me, because the notes *knew* about the latch failure before it happened. On 03/29, I found the last note. The camera glitch lasted 8.7 seconds, the longest yet. When the feed returned, there were three notes, not one, scattered across the desk as if thrown. They were soaked, though dry to the touch. The ink had bled. > *dont let them take the desk away. its the only thing that still works. if they move it i cant reach you and then we both drown* > *I can hear you typing this. Stop documenting and RUN. The red lights are on. THE RED LIGHTS ARE ON RIGHT NOW.* > *sorry sorry sorry i lied about everything there is no breach i just wanted to see if you would still listen to me* The lights in Research Wing C were white. They had always been white. I sat at that desk for a long time, shaking, holding those wet-dry papers. I did not know which version of myself to believe - the one who was warning me, the one who was lying, or the one sitting there, past-tense, writing this report. They have moved me off-site. My desk is now in an empty containment cube at Site-19, monitored by cameras that still glitch every few days. The notes still appear. The last one Temporal Affairs forwarded to me just said my name over and over. The breach has not happened.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This SCP report demonstrates a strong understanding of the Foundation universe's clinical documentation style while effectively weaving in the psychological horror elements requested. The piece successfully balances the formal SCP format with the personal narrative of Dr. Crowe, creating an authentic Foundation document that maintains the characteristic tone throughout. The concept execution is particularly strong - the temporal anomaly/psychological manipulation ambiguity is well-maintained, never definitively resolving whether the notes are genuine temporal communications or some form of memetic influence. The escalating desperation in the notes is convincingly portrayed through deteriorating handwriting descriptions and increasingly contradictory information. Character work shows Dr. Crowe as a believable researcher whose rational scientific mind gradually succumbs to doubt and paranoia. Her methodical approach to documenting and cross-referencing reality against the notes feels authentic to both the Foundation setting and human psychology under stress. The supporting characters like Chen and Daniels serve their purpose effectively without requiring deep development. The prose maintains clinical precision while building genuine tension. The camera glitches, the wet-but-dry papers, and the contradictory details about Chen and Daniels create an unsettling atmosphere. The piece effectively uses the SCP format's constraints to enhance rather than limit the storytelling. However, there are some weaknesses. The ending feels somewhat abrupt and could have used more resolution or a stronger final image. Some of the temporal mechanics remain unclear - the bootstrap paradox explanation feels underdeveloped. The piece occasionally relies on telling rather than showing, particularly in describing Dr. Crowe's emotional states. The writing demonstrates technical competence with natural sentence flow and appropriate pacing. The integration of the notes within the narrative works well, and the escalating timeline creates good momentum. The piece successfully captures the existential dread inherent in questioning one's own perception of reality. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 16 Nuanced Characters: 14 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 15 Elegant Prose: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Emotionally Complex: 15 Coherent: 17 Meandering: 4 Weak Dialogue: 6 Tell-Don't-Show: 8 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 5 Purple Prose: 3 Overwrought: 5 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Sentences Flow Naturally: 17 Overall Reader Engagement: 16 Overall Impression: 16

+ Scp: Unmarked Doors Archive — Score: 76.8
Prompt:
Write an SCP entry about a series of identical unmarked doors appearing in abandoned buildings worldwide that lead to a massive, architecturally impossible archive. Documents within reference a previous global civilization destroyed by an unspecified containment failure. Foundation researchers discover references to their own organization under a different name, including personnel files for current staff dated centuries ago. Include exploration logs revealing sections of the archive actively being removed by entities resembling hazmat teams who flee when approached, and evidence that O5 Council members have visited the archive without authorization. Third person (exploration team perspective) with recovered document excerpts. Length: 1000 words.
Model Output:
**Item #:** SCP-XXXX **Object Class:** Euclid **Special Containment Procedures:** As of 11/14/2024, 19 instances of SCP-XXXX-1 have been identified. All instances are to be contained on-site via Provisional Containment Area designation. The abandoned structure housing each instance is to be purchased through Foundation front companies and secured with a minimum 10m perimeter fence. No fewer than two armed guards are to be stationed at each site. All SCP-XXXX-1 instances are to be fitted with steel bulkheads welded over the frame. Under no circumstances are personnel to enter SCP-XXXX-1 without authorization from at least two Level 4 staff members. Any new SCP-XXXX-1 manifestations are to be reported immediately to Dr. Aris Thorne, HMCL. Civilian witnesses are to be administered Class-B amnestics. **Description:** SCP-XXXX-1 is the collective designation for a series of identical doors manifesting inside abandoned man-made structures worldwide. Instances appear only in buildings that have been unoccupied for a minimum of 5 years. As of this writing, instances have been found in Pripyat, Ukraine; Detroit, USA; Hashima Island, Japan; and 16 other locations. Each SCP-XXXX-1 instance is a door measuring 2.13m x 0.91m, constructed from unmarked, non-corroding low-carbon steel. No handle, hinges, or locking mechanism are present. The door is always set into a load-bearing wall where no opening previously existed. Despite this, the door can be opened by applying inward pressure. In complete darkness, a faint, cool white glow escapes through the door's edges, even when the frame is observed to be flush against the wall. All SCP-XXXX-1 instances open into the same extradimensional space, designated SCP-XXXX-2. SCP-XXXX-2 is an archive of immense scale. Initial laser rangefinding estimates place its dimensions at a minimum of 40km x 40km x 3km, though internal geometry is non-Euclidean; exploration teams report descending staircases that return them to higher floors, and hallways that loop impossibly. The archive is composed of dark basalt shelving, iron catwalks, and rolling ladders. It is lit by an ambient sourceless light with no detectable origin. Temperature is a constant 18°C. Air is breathable. The archive contains an estimated 200+ million physical documents: bound ledgers, printouts, punched cards, vellum scrolls, and data storage media of unknown design. All documents are written in modern English, French, Mandarin, and 12 other contemporary languages, though linguistic analysis suggests subtle anachronisms consistent with a language that has evolved separately for 200-400 years. **Addendum XXXX-1: Recovered Document Excerpts** > **Document 001-A, recovered 30m from entry point:** > FINAL REPORT - CONTAINMENT FAILURE EVENT 001 > The Custodianship has failed. The primary containment lattice at Site-01 (Central Archive) was breached at 03:17 UTC. Cascading informational collapse. We cannot re-establish narrative cohesion. All lower archives are to be sealed. If you are reading this, you are the second iteration. DO NOT REPEAT OUR MISTAKE. DO NOT CONTAIN WHAT CANNOT BE CONTAINED. > **Document 004-C, recovered from "Personnel Wing":** > **PERSONNEL DOSSIER - CUSTODIANSHIP EMPLOYEE** > Name: Thorne, Aris J. > DOB: 03/11/1982 > Role: Archival Historian, Level 3 > Assigned Site: Site-19 (Provisional) > Date of File Creation: 09/02/1847 > Status: ACTIVE - PENDING RE-ACQUISITION > [Attached photograph is identical to Dr. Aris Thorne, currently assigned as HMCL of SCP-XXXX] > **Document 004-D, recovered from same drawer:** > Name: Chen, Daniel R. > DOB: 07/22/1991 > Role: Security Specialist, Mobile Task Force Zeta-9 > Date of File Creation: 06/14/1911 > Status: ACTIVE Over 40 personnel files matching currently active Foundation staff have been recovered. All are dated between 1798 and 1953. All list the organization as "The Second Custodianship" or "The Veil Conservatory." > **Document 019-F, recovered from central atrium, bound in black leather:** > ACCESS LOG - ATRIUM ENTRY > 2021/03/09 - O5-7 - ENTRY - NO ESCORT > 2021/07/22 - O5-3 - ENTRY - NO ESCORT > 2022/01/11 - O5-7 + O5-3 - ENTRY - NO ESCORT > 2022/11/04 - O5-1 - ENTRY - NO ESCORT - REMOVAL AUTHORIZED > NOTE: Atrium access restricted after Cycle 2. Do not permit further O5 entry without Council-wide vote. - O5-1 When questioned, all named O5 Council members denied any knowledge of SCP-XXXX or SCP-XXXX-2. A subsequent forensic analysis of the logbook confirmed the handwriting matches O5-3 and O5-7. **Addendum XXXX-2: Exploration Log Excerpts** **Exploration II - Team: MTF Zeta-9 "Mole Rats"** The exploration team, consisting of Zeta-9 Team Lead, Z-9 Chen, Z-9 Lopez, and Researcher Thorne observing via bodycam, entered SCP-XXXX-1 Instance-07 (Gary, Indiana). The team proceeded 400m north along the main aisle. Thorne noted that the filing system appeared to be actively degrading - entire shelves were empty with thick dust outlines where ledgers had been removed. At approximately 1.2km from the entry point, the team observed movement. At the end of a transverse aisle, approximately six humanoid figures were systematically removing documents from shelves and placing them into large, matte-black containment crates. The figures are described as wearing bulky, featureless white hazmat suits with no visible seams, zippers, or faceplates. The suits appeared to absorb light. The team leader attempted contact. Upon being illuminated by the team's flashlights, all six entities immediately ceased activity. One entity turned its head toward the team. Lopez reported feeling intense vertigo and nausea. The entities then fled at high speed, abandoning one crate. The team gave chase but lost them after the entities turned a corner that, upon inspection, was a solid basalt wall. The recovered crate contained approximately 200 files, all labeled "SCP-001 THROUGH SCP-500 - DECOMMISSIONED - CYCLE 1." All files inside were blank, save for a fine layer of black ash. **Exploration IV - Team: MTF Zeta-9** The team returned to the location of the previous contact with orders to establish a forward base. Upon arrival, they found the area completely stripped. Over an estimated 5,000 square meters of shelving had been emptied. The floor was marked with drag trails leading into the dark between shelves, where no passage should exist. Deeper in, the team found a hastily abandoned workstation belonging to the hazmat entities. On it was a single document, still warm. > **RECLAMATION ORDER 87** > SECTOR 19-G THROUGH 22-J SCHEDULED FOR IMMEDIATE RECLAMATION. SECOND ITERATION HAS BREACHED PERIMETER. INCREASE REMOVAL RATE. DO NOT ENGAGE. THEY ARE NOT READY TO REMEMBER. > - Custodian Prime On the floor beside the workstation was a disposable coffee cup bearing the logo of Site-19's cafeteria. DNA analysis of saliva on the rim returned a 100% match to O5-1.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This SCP entry demonstrates strong adherence to the Foundation's clinical documentation style and successfully incorporates all requested elements. The writing maintains the characteristic detached, scientific tone throughout while building an effectively unsettling narrative about cyclical civilizations and temporal loops. The piece excels in its structural authenticity, following SCP format conventions precisely with containment procedures, object descriptions, and addenda. The concept of doors leading to an archive containing evidence of previous iterations of the Foundation is genuinely creative and philosophically disturbing. The integration of current personnel files dated centuries ago creates effective cognitive dissonance. The exploration logs provide good pacing and escalating tension, particularly the encounters with the hazmat entities. The detail about entities fleeing when approached adds to the mystery while the abandoned coffee cup with O5-1's DNA provides a concrete, chilling connection to current Foundation leadership. However, the piece suffers from some mechanical execution issues. The writing occasionally becomes overly clinical to the point of reducing emotional impact. While the SCP format demands clinical detachment, the most effective entries balance this with subtle horror elements that could be more pronounced here. Character development is necessarily limited by the format, but the exploration team members feel somewhat interchangeable beyond their designations. The piece also relies heavily on exposition through recovered documents rather than showing events unfold, though this is somewhat inherent to the SCP format. The ending with the coffee cup revelation is effective but feels slightly rushed. The implications of the cyclical nature could have been developed more thoroughly. Some of the recovered documents, while atmospheric, border on being too explicit in their warnings rather than allowing readers to piece together the horror themselves. The prose is generally clean and functional, avoiding purple prose while maintaining readability. The technical details feel authentic to the SCP universe without becoming overwrought. The piece successfully creates an atmosphere of creeping dread about institutional memory and the possibility that the Foundation itself may be trapped in an endless cycle of failure and reformation. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 15 Nuanced Characters: 12 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 17 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 15 Elegant Prose: 14 Emotionally Engaging: 14 Emotionally Complex: 13 Coherent: 17 Meandering: 4 Weak Dialogue: 6 Tell-Don't-Show: 8 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 3 Overwrought: 4 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 15 Sentences Flow Naturally: 16 Overall Reader Engagement: 15 Overall Impression: 15
