+ Young adult drama: Gone South — Score: 80.2
Prompt:
Young adult drama Gone South Taylor is a 16 year old teen with a history of "concerning behavioural patterns" (or that's how the stupid smug school counsellor worded it on the phone call to Mom). It shouldn't be a surprise: When you live in a backwater town in Tennessee, your choices are: a. be mind-fuckingly bored, or b. invent some drama. Two days ago, Taylor came out to their family as trans. This disclosure was *not* in the category of invented drama -- it was, in fact, the real deal, and a long time coming. It didn't go well. The last 48 hours had been pure disaster. In a moment of desperate inspiration, Taylor had decided to get the hell out of dodge. And so an escape plan was hatched and executed within the space of 30 minutes. That was last night. Now Taylor was two states away, having been unceremoniously ejected from their hitched ride in downtown New Orleans. Write the next chapter in Taylor's story as they find their feet in this new environment. Tell the chapter over the course of their first day in New Orleans, in which Taylor meets another runaway with issues and secrets. Taylor navigates this new world, not knowing who to trust or what the dangers are. Write in Taylor's voice, and incorporate their internal monologue as they grapple with intense & conflicting emotions. Also incorporate dialogue with the interesting characters Taylor meets. First person, mixed tense. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The bus station smells like piss and fried oil and I am standing outside of it because the security guy inside took one look at me and said "No loitering." Which, fair. I am definitely loitering. I am loitering professionally at this point. New Orleans at 8:13 a.m. is already so fucking hot it feels personal. My shirt is stuck to my back. My backpack weighs like a thousand pounds and all I have in it is a hoodie, a half-empty water bottle, a toothbrush, and my dead-end life choices. Okay. That was dramatic. That's what Mom would say. *Everything is so dramatic with you, Taylor.* My phone says 12%. 12% and no charger. I had a charger. I had it in my hand when I was packing and then Dad was yelling downstairs and Mom was doing that crying-but-trying-not-to-cry voice and I just shoved shit in my bag and ran. So the charger is probably still on my bedroom floor in Tennessee next to the letter I left that I already regret writing. I shouldn't have come here. I should have. I don't know. Both things are true at the same time and it's making my brain buzz. I came out two days ago. Two days. Forty-eight hours ago I was sitting at our kitchen table saying "I'm trans, I'm not a girl, I'm Taylor, I'm... they/them" and I had practiced it for like six months in the mirror and it came out all squeaky anyway. And Mom just stared. And Dad said "You're confused." And then it was counselor calls and praying and Googling conversion shit on the family computer where I could see the search history. So yeah. Invented drama? Fuck you, Ms. Henderson. This wasn't invented. This was the one real thing I ever said out loud. The guy who gave me a ride - Kevin, 42, drives a truck full of live chickens, do NOT ask - dropped me at the edge of Canal Street at like 5 a.m. and said "Good luck, kid. Don't let this city eat you." Super helpful, Kevin. Thanks. Now I'm trying to look like I belong. Which is hard when you're 16 and white and clearly not from here and your backpack is held together with a safety pin. I duck into Jackson Square because there are other people just... sitting. Doing nothing. Which is my skill set. There's a guy playing trumpet that makes my chest hurt in a good way. I sit on the edge of a planter and try to make my phone last. 11%. I turn off location. I turn off data. I stare at my Mom's last text from last night: *Taylor please answer. Where are you? We love you we just need to talk.* We love you we just need to talk. We love you BUT. I don't answer. I can't. If I answer I'll cry and if I cry I'll want to go home and if I go home they'll pretend I never said anything and I'll have to go back to being... before. I can't do that again. I *can't*. "You gonna die if you keep starin at that thing." I jump. There's a person next to me on the planter. Black girl, maybe my age? Maybe a little older? Shaved head on one side, puffy jacket even though it's 90 degrees, big beat-up Converse. She's got a skateboard with stickers all over it and a busted lip that looks fresh. "What?" I say. My voice cracks. Cool. "Your phone. You keep starin like it's gonna charge itself. It ain't." She grins. Her front tooth is chipped. "You got a charger?" "No." "Me neither. Well, I do, but it's Android and you got an iPhone, so we're both screwed in different flavors." I laugh before I can stop myself. It sounds weird and rusty. "I'm Reese," she says. "Taylor." She looks at me for a second. Not weird. Just... looking. "You runnin?" I freeze. "What? No. I'm... visiting." "Okay, Visitin." She says it like she knows it's bullshit but she's not gonna push. "You visitin with that giant backpack and no hotel? You got family here?" I shake my head. She nods, like that answers something. "Me too. Well, not family. I got out of Lafayette three days ago. My foster mom's boyfriend is a dick. Like, capital D Dick. So." Oh. "Oh," I say. We sit there while the trumpet guy plays. My stomach growls so loud Reese hears it. "When'd you last eat?" she asks. "Yesterday? On the road. Gas station beef jerky." She makes a face. "Nasty. C'mon. I know a spot. They throw out beignets at 9 if you don't look too - " She stops herself. "If you act chill." I should not go with a stranger. That's literally the first rule of everything. Don't go with strangers, especially when you're a trans kid alone in a city you've never been to and your phone is at 9% now and you have $23 in cash. But Reese has eyes that keep darting to the cops that walk by, same as mine do. Same flinch. "Okay," I say. We walk through the Quarter. It's too bright and too loud and smells like sugar and garbage. Reese walks fast, like she knows where she's going, and talks the whole time. "I was supposed to go back to school last week but like, what's the point? They were gonna send me to juvie anyway for punching Marcus. But Marcus deserved it, he stole my - " She stops. "Nevermind. Secret stuff." I get that. Everyone's got secret stuff. The cafe is called something French. There's a girl with a hairnet who sees Reese and rolls her eyes but then slides a paper bag across the counter without a word. Reese says "Bless" and we go sit in an alley behind it. The beignets are still warm. Powdered sugar everywhere. I get it all over my black shirt and I don't even care. I eat three in like thirty seconds. "Slow down, Visitin, you'll puke," Reese says, but she's eating fast too. Between bites she goes, "So what's your pronouns?" I almost choke on sugar. No one has ever asked me that in real life. Ever. "They/them," I whisper. "Cool. I'm she/her. My government name is Clarissa but if you call me that I'll skateboard over your foot." "Noted." She smiles and for a second it's just two kids in an alley eating stolen-adjacent donuts and it feels... normal. Which is insane because nothing about this is normal. My phone buzzes. 6%. It's Mom again: *Please honey. At least tell us you're safe.* My hands start shaking. I shove the phone in my pocket. "You good?" Reese asks. "Yeah. No. I came out to my parents and it went bad. Like really bad. So I left. And now I'm here and I don't know what I'm doing." Reese is quiet for a second. She picks at her Converse. "My mom kicked me out when I told her I liked girls," she says finally. "Said I was possessed. So I get it. The whole... 'we love you but not like that' thing." "Yeah," I say, and my voice is thick. "Exactly that." We don't say anything for a minute. The alley is gross but it's shady and kind of safe. "So what's the plan?" she asks. "I don't have one. I thought... I thought New Orleans would be different. Like people would just get it here." "Some people do. Some people don't. Same as anywhere, just with better music." She looks at me. "You can't stay out here alone, Taylor. For real. There's dudes who look for kids like us. You gotta be smart." My stomach drops. I knew that, I did, but hearing her say it makes it real. "I have this place," she says, and now she's doing that secret-voice again. "Behind the church on St. Claude. There's a lady, Miss Delphine, she lets us crash on the porch sometimes if we don't cause trouble. No questions. But you can't stay there forever. She'll call somebody eventually. Like a shelter or whatever." A shelter. Cops. Phone calls home. I don't want to go home. I do want to go home. Both. "I don't want to go back," I say, and I'm crying now, great, powdered sugar and snot, very cute. "But I'm scared here too." Reese doesn't do the awkward pat thing. She just sits closer so our shoulders touch. "Yeah," she says. "That's the part they don't tell you. It's scary both places. You just gotta pick which scary you can live with for one more day." My phone is at 3%. It's going to die and then I'll really be alone. Reese sees me looking at it. "You should text them you're safe," she says quietly. "You don't gotta say where. Just... so they don't call the police and make it worse." She's right and I hate that she's right. I pull out my phone with sticky fingers. 2%. I type: *I'm safe. In NOLA. I'm okay. Don't come get me. I need time. - T* I hit send right as it goes black. The screen dies. Just like that. I'm officially off the grid. Reese stands up and holds out her hand. Her palm is all calloused. "C'mon, Visitin. Let's go see if Miss Delphine is in a good mood. And after that, we gotta find you a charger before you have a full existential crisis." I take her hand. It's warm. I have no idea if I can trust her. I have no idea if this church porch lady is real or if this is a terrible idea. I have no idea what I'm doing. But for the first time since I left Tennessee, I'm not walking alone.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong adherence to the prompt requirements, successfully capturing Taylor's authentic teenage voice through first-person narration with mixed tense usage. The author effectively establishes the immediate aftermath of Taylor's escape, placing them in New Orleans with realistic details like the declining phone battery and limited resources. The characterization of Taylor feels genuine and age-appropriate. Their internal monologue captures the conflicted emotions of a trans teenager - the simultaneous fear and determination, the regret mixed with resolve. The voice remains consistent throughout, with believable teenage speech patterns and thought processes. Taylor's reactions to their situation feel authentic rather than overly mature or unrealistically naive. Reese emerges as a well-developed secondary character with her own distinct voice and backstory. The dialogue between the two characters flows naturally and serves multiple purposes - advancing plot, revealing character, and building their relationship. The conversation feels organic rather than expository, particularly in moments like Reese asking about pronouns or sharing her own experiences. The setting details are economical but effective. The author captures New Orleans through sensory details - the heat, smells, sounds - without overwhelming the narrative. The progression through different locations (bus station, Jackson Square, café, alley) provides natural structure to Taylor's first day. The emotional complexity is handled well. Taylor's conflicted feelings about home, their fear of the unknown, and their desperate need for acceptance are layered throughout. The piece avoids simple resolution while providing a moment of connection that feels earned rather than artificially optimistic. The prose style matches the character's voice effectively. Sentences vary in length and structure, mimicking natural thought patterns. The writing includes appropriate teenage vernacular without feeling forced or inauthentic. Some minor weaknesses include occasional moments where the internal monologue becomes slightly repetitive, and a few instances where emotional beats could be more subtly rendered. The pacing occasionally slows during introspective passages, though this generally serves the character development. The ending strikes an appropriate balance - offering hope through human connection while acknowledging the ongoing uncertainty and danger of Taylor's situation. This avoids false optimism while providing enough forward momentum to engage readers. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 17 Nuanced Characters: 16 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 15 Elegant Prose: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Emotionally Complex: 16 Coherent: 17 Meandering: 6 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 7 Amateurish: 5 Purple Prose: 3 Overwrought: 6 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 3 Unearned Transformations: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Sentences Flow Naturally: 16 Overall Reader Engagement: 17 Overall Impression: 16

+ Dystopic-comedy: My Apocalypse Buddy — Score: 85.0
Prompt:
My Apocalypse Buddy Write a wry, dry short story of survival and adventure loosely in the style of Terry Pratchett, set in the post-technological collapse world. The story begins with the pair deliberating their predicament and intent for the day's mission, details the ways in which the mission inevitably goes sideways, and returns them to home base in multiple ways, worse for wear. [characters] - the animo-tronic stuffed koala, Chewy, from some long forgotten before time - midas, our hapless protagonist, who has a knack for finding himself in the worst kinds of trouble. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The world had ended, which Midas felt was exactly the sort of thing that would happen to him personally. Not the *whole* world, of course. Bits of it were still there, lying around being unhelpful. But the part with electricity and dentists and people who understood plumbing had quietly packed its bags and left sometime before Midas was born. Which left Midas, in what had once been a laundromat and was now Home Base, deliberating his predicament with a small grey koala. "We have a water problem," Midas announced. Chewy said nothing. Chewy was an animatronic stuffed koala from some long-forgotten Before Time, possibly designed to sell breakfast cereal or teach children about chlamydia. His fur had been loved down to the backing in places, one ear was held on with electrical tape and prayer, and his voice box had long since given up any pretense of saying "I wuv you!" and now only made a noise like a disappointed printer. Chewy's eyes flickered a worried yellow. "We have *had* a water problem," Midas corrected. "Now we have a water *crisis*. The filter's gone from 'questionable brown' to 'actively chunky.'" This was true. The filter, liberated from a dead RV, had been making a noise for three days that suggested it was reconsidering its career choices. "So today's mission," Midas said, with the air of a man drawing up battle plans on a napkin, "is simple. We go to the old SunMart. We get the new filter from aisle seven. We come back. No detours. No complications. No incidents." Chewy stared at him. Chewy's eyes flickered from yellow to a deep, skeptical amber. This was the color they went when Midas was lying to himself, which was often. Chewy had been programmed in the Before Time to have Empathy Response Eyes, so children would know he was happy or sad. In the After Time, this meant he was a terrible liar and an even worse poker player. "What?" said Midas. "It's a perfectly reasonable plan." Chewy's eyes flickered to a brief, incandescent red. This was Chewy for *You once said 'perfectly reasonable' right before you fell into a septic tank.* "That was one time," Midas muttered. The SunMart was four blocks away, if you measured in blocks. If you measured in Post-Collapse Standard Units, it was four blocks, two feral dog negotiations, one collapsed overpass, and a patch of ground that hummed ominously for reasons no one wanted to investigate. Midas packed his bag: one wrench, one spear made from a broom handle, half a protein bar of indeterminate vintage*, and Chewy, who rode in the front pocket of his patched coat like a furry, judgmental admiral. > *The label said PEANUT and the rest had been gnawed off. Midas chose to believe it said PEANUT BUTTER JOY and not PEANUT-ADJACENT INDUSTRIAL LUBRICANT. The SunMart had survived the Collapse largely because it had been built to survive everything except good taste. Its doors were still shut. Its sign still said SUNMART: WHERE THE SUN NEVER SETS ON SAVINGS! in letters that had faded to SUNM RT: W ERE THE SU NEVER S TS ON SA INGS, which Midas felt was more honest. The door made a noise like a dying walrus when he pried it open. Inside, it was dark and smelled faintly of old plastic and lost optimism. Aisle seven was exactly where it had been for twenty years. It was also occupied. The occupant was a man in a suit made entirely of taped-together security vests who had declared himself King of Aisle Seven. This sort of thing happened a lot since the Collapse. When the big systems go away, people become very attached to small systems, like Aisle Seven. "None shall pass!" shouted the King. "This filter is the royal water purifier! It will purify my royal water!" Midas, who had a natural talent for finding himself in the worst kinds of trouble, considered his options. Option one was diplomacy. Option two was running away. Midas, being Midas, went for option three, which was diplomacy while accidentally knocking over a display of canned peaches. The peaches fell with the thunderous clatter of a small, fruity apocalypse. The King, startled, fell backwards into his throne, which was a stack of toilet paper that had not survived the experience. This was when the mission went sideways in the first of its three entirely predictable ways. Sideways the First: The peaches had been load-bearing. Their collapse triggered a secondary collapse of the shelving unit behind them, which contained, for reasons no inventory system could now explain, twenty gallons of industrial honey that had fermented sometime during the last decade. The honey, now a sentient, alcoholic entity, surged across the floor. Sideways the Second: The King, now honey-covered and enraged, decided Midas was a honey thief and lunged. Midas lunged backwards. Chewy, ejected from the front pocket, landed directly in the King's vest-pile, where his ancient motion sensor, dormant for years, suddenly decided *now* was the time to fulfill its primary function. Chewy sat up, fur dripping with mead-honey, and said in a voice as clear as the day he was manufactured, "LET'S BE FRIENDS!" His eyes flashed a blinding, panicked, neon-green. The King screamed. In the Post-Collapse world, clear recorded voices from Before Time objects were considered either miracles or witchcraft, and this one was sticky. Sideways the Third: The scream woke up the pigeons. Every SunMart has pigeons. These pigeons had spent twenty generations evolving in an enclosed mall with unlimited access to pretzels and no natural predators except boredom. They were the size of small dogs and had opinions. They descended. Midas did what any sensible survivalist would do. He grabbed Chewy, grabbed the filter, and ran directly into a display of novelty mops. He emerged on the other side of the SunMart, bleeding slightly, covered in honey-mead, with one mop inexplicably tangled around his leg, clutching a water filter and a koala whose eyes were now strobing through the entire visible spectrum at a frequency that suggested existential terror. "Strategic withdrawal!" he shouted, as the pigeons pursued him for crimes against peaches. They returned to Home Base in multiple ways, worse for wear. Midas returned physically, via the front door, which fell off when he leaned on it. Chewy returned spiritually, having seen the face of God in the honey and found it wanting, his eyes settling on a dim, exhausted blue. The water filter returned triumphantly, held aloft like a holy relic. The mop returned as well, because it had become emotionally attached to Midas's leg and refused to leave. Midas collapsed onto the floor of the laundromat, which was still Home Base, on account of having excellent drainage. "We got it," he wheezed. Chewy made the disappointed printer noise. His eyes flickered to a soft, fond pink, which was Chewy for *You are an idiot, but you are my idiot.* Then they flickered to yellow again, because the filter Midas had grabbed was not a water filter at all. It was an air fryer. From the Before Time. Still in its box. Midas stared at it. Chewy stared at Midas. Outside, the honey-pigeons were organizing. "Right," said Midas. "Tomorrow's mission. We go back for the actual filter." Chewy's eyes went red and stayed there.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates a strong grasp of Terry Pratchett's distinctive style, capturing his dry wit, observational humor, and ability to find profound absurdity in mundane situations. The author successfully creates a post-apocalyptic world that feels both bleak and oddly cozy, much like Pratchett's Discworld manages to be both dangerous and comforting. The characterization is particularly effective. Midas emerges as a genuinely hapless protagonist whose self-awareness about his own incompetence makes him endearing rather than frustrating. The running gag of his "perfectly reasonable" plans consistently going awry feels authentic to both the character and the Pratchett style. Chewy, despite being non-verbal, becomes a fully realized character through his color-coded emotional responses, which serve as both world-building detail and character development. The prose demonstrates genuine elegance in its construction. Lines like "The world had ended, which Midas felt was exactly the sort of thing that would happen to him personally" immediately establish both tone and character while delivering a perfect Pratchett-esque observation. The footnote about the protein bar is a nice touch that mimics Pratchett's own narrative asides. The story structure adheres well to the prompt's requirements, moving from deliberation through escalating complications to a return home "worse for wear." The three-part escalation of disasters (peaches, Chewy's activation, pigeons) builds naturally and absurdly. The final revelation that Midas grabbed an air fryer instead of a water filter provides a satisfying capstone to his incompetence. However, some elements feel slightly forced. The "King of Aisle Seven" character, while fitting the post-apocalyptic setting, doesn't quite achieve the depth of Pratchett's minor characters, who usually have more distinctive voices and motivations. The honey becoming "sentient" pushes into territory that feels more generically fantastical than Pratchett-absurd. The dialogue, while limited, works well when present. Midas's voice remains consistent throughout, and his conversations with the non-responsive Chewy create genuine humor through their one-sided nature. The ending avoids false positivity while maintaining the story's essential warmth. Midas and Chewy's relationship, conveyed largely through Chewy's eye colors, provides emotional grounding without sentimentality. The promise of returning tomorrow for another doomed mission feels perfectly in character and appropriately cyclical. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 17 Nuanced Characters: 16 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 19 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 17 Elegant Prose: 18 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Emotionally Complex: 14 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 2 Overwrought: 3 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Sentences Flow Naturally: 18 Overall Reader Engagement: 17 Overall Impression: 17

+ Romance: Love in the Limelight — Score: 78.8
Prompt:
Romance: Love in the Limelight. Write one scene within a larger story set in Wales. A famous (fictional) actor ducks into a small-town bookstore to escape paparazzi. The scene takes us through the characters meeting in this odd circumstance. Over the course of the scene, the actor and the bookstore owner have a conversation charged by an undercurrent of unspoken chemistry. Write the actor as somewhat of a rogue with a fragile ego, which needs to be fed by having everyone like him. He is thoroughly charming, but the bookstore owner seems (at least superficially) immune to this; which paradoxically provokes a genuine attraction and derails the charm offensive. The bookstore owner, despite the superficial rebuffs of the actor's charm, is inwardly more than a little charmed and flustered despite themselves. Write primarily in dialogue, in the distinct voices of each character. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The bell over the door at Gwasg y Ddraig Books jangled like it had been assaulted. Elin didn't look up from the till. "We're closed for lunch." "We're not. Your sign says open." "My sign is an optimist. I'm not." The man was already inside, hood up, sunglasses on despite the Cardiff drizzle hammering Cathedral Road. He pressed his back to the poetry shelf and peered out the window. "Do you have a back door? A staff exit? A romantic, bookish secret tunnel?" "We have a fire exit that sets off an alarm loud enough to wake Roald Dahl. What's wrong with the front?" "Photographers. Three of them. Possibly four if Steve's brought his new intern." He finally pulled the hood down. Artfully messy dark hair. The kind of jawline that had its own agent. "Sorry. Hi. This is terribly rude." Elin finally looked properly. And felt her stomach drop straight through the floorboards. Oh, *shit*. It was Rhys Morgan. *The* Rhys Morgan. Welsh boy made good, Hollywood bad boy, just finished shooting that thing on the Castle grounds everyone in Pontcanna had been complaining about for three months. Her sister had his poster on her wall for five years. He was giving her *the* smile. The one from the Graham Norton interview. Elin put her best customer-service face on. The one she reserved for people who asked if she had the new Colleen Hoover. "Right. And you thought my shop looked like a good place to hide?" "Yours was the only one with lights on and no queue. Also, you have excellent taste." He gestured vaguely at the shelf he was currently crushing. "R.S. Thomas. A man after my own heart." "You're leaning on Gardening." He leapt away from Gardening like it had insulted his mother. "Even better. Grounded. Earthy. So - can I hide here for ten minutes? Fifteen? Until they get bored and go harass someone from Love Island?" Elin crossed her arms. "You can browse like a normal person. If you buy something, you can stay as long as you like." He stared at her. That smile faltered a millimeter. "You're serious." "As a heart attack. This is a business, not a witness protection programme." A beat. Most people, when Rhys Morgan smiled at them, melted. Giggled. Asked for a selfie for their mum. This woman was looking at him like he was a slight leak she was deciding whether to mop up now or later. It was... deeply unsettling. And interesting. "Okay. Okay, fair. I'll browse." He spread his hands, magnanimous, playing along. "What do you recommend for a man on the run? Something... brooding. Misunderstood." "Instructions for our fire exit are laminated by the loo." He laughed, loud and delighted. God, she was good at this. "Ouch. You're tough." "I'm Welsh. You're standing in my shop dripping on my rug." "You don't know who I am, do you?" The question slipped out before he could dress it up as a joke. He tried to recover with a grin. "Which is incredibly refreshing, by the way. So rare." Elin tilted her head. "You're Rhys Morgan. You were in *The Prince of Tides* remake. My sister fancied you rotten when she was fourteen. She got over it when you punched a pap at Heathrow." He winced. The fragile ego bit, right there, a hairline crack. "It was a very aggressive lens. And the charges were dropped. And I've grown *so* much as a person since then." "Have you now?" "My therapist says so. Weekly. Very expensive. So it must be true." He was leaning on the counter now, closer. He smelled like expensive rain. "What's your name?" "Elin." "Elin." He said it like he was tasting it. "Elin who owns a bookshop in Cardiff and doesn't care that I'm -" "That you're what?" "Famous," he said, and then immediately hated how it sounded. Small. Needy. "Oh, I care," Elin said, deadpan. "I'm mentally calculating how much I can charge for this story down the pub. 'Famous actor hides in my shop, ruins my Thomas Hardy display.'" He laughed again, but it was different this time. Less performance, more surprised air escaping. *God, he's worse in person,* Elin thought, and her treacherous brain supplied: *worse meaning more handsome. Worse meaning that voice should be illegal in a small enclosed space. Worse meaning stop looking at his mouth.* Out loud, she said, "Your photographers are still there. One's buying a coffee. Committed." Rhys groaned and ducked lower. "Can I - look, honestly, can I just stay until they piss off? I promise I'll be charming and low-maintenance." "You've not managed either so far." "Give me a chance to turn it around. Please, Elin?" There it was. The soft bit. The rogue act dropped for half a second and underneath was just a 32-year-old boy who was tired of being looked at and terrified of not being looked at. It hit her right in the chest. She busied herself with the kettle behind the till so he wouldn't see her flush. "I've got tea. If you're staying, you can have tea. And you can stop doing that thing with your eyebrows, it's not going to work on me." "What thing?" He was instantly, comically, trying to control his eyebrows. "That. The smoulder. My last boyfriend was a rugby player who could actually smoulder. You're just... smoulder-adjacent." "Smoulder-adjacent!" He clutched his heart. "You wound me. Mortally. Elin, I think this is the most abusive customer interaction I've ever had. I'm going to have to tell my people." "Do. Tell them you were held hostage by a woman with strong opinions about second-hand paperbacks." She handed him a chipped mug that said *Cymru Am Byth*. Their fingers brushed. His were cold. He held on a fraction too long. "Thanks," he said quietly. "You're welcome." Her voice had gone a bit high. Damn it. For a moment the shop was just rain on the windows and the hum of the old radiator. Outside, a flash went off. Rhys didn't even flinch. He was looking at her. "You really don't like me, do you?" he asked. It was supposed to be light. It came out almost... hopeful. Like if she didn't like him, it might actually mean something. Elin swallowed. *I like you far too much for someone I've known for four minutes, you absolute idiot, and that's why I'm being such a cow.* What she said was, "I don't know you. Jury's still out. You haven't bought anything yet." A slow, real smile spread across his face this time. Not the poster. Not the premiere. Something lopsided and a bit crooked. "Then what if I buy the whole Gardening section? Would that sway the jury?" "It would make me think you have a very boring garden." "Elin Lloyd, bookshop owner, hard to impress." "You don't know my last name." "I guessed. It suits you." "It is Lloyd, actually. How did you -" "Lucky." He took a sip of tea and grimaced. "This is terrible tea." "It's builder's. It's supposed to be terrible. Builds character." "I like it." He was still looking at her over the rim of the mug. "I like this shop." "You've seen about three feet of it." "I'm a fast decision maker. It's one of my toxic traits. Along with... well, several others. My publicist has a list." The bell over the door jangled again as two students came in, shaking off umbrellas. They stopped dead when they saw him. One whispered, *Oh my god*. Rhys didn't look away from Elin. "Are they gone? Your photographers?" Elin glanced out. The street was empty except for the rain. "Yeah," she said. "They're gone." "Right. Great." He made no move to leave. "So... terrible news. I think I have to stay a bit longer. For... security reasons. You know. In case they come back." "Security reasons." "Absolutely. Very dangerous out there. Lots of... puddles." She should have told him to go. She had invoices to do. She had a leak over History. Instead she heard herself say, "The Gardening section is buy one get one free." "Is it?" "No. But you look like you need a hobby." He laughed, and this time it was completely unguarded, and Elin's heart did a stupid, swooping thing that she was going to be very annoyed about later. "Elin Lloyd," he said, "are you flirting with me?" "Absolutely not," she said, flustered to her core. "I'm trying to sell you a book so you'll leave." "Liar," he whispered, delighted. And he stayed.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong technical execution and adherence to the prompt requirements. The author successfully creates a meet-cute scenario in a Welsh bookstore with two well-defined characters whose personalities emerge clearly through dialogue. The setting is authentic, with specific Welsh references (Gwasg y Ddraig Books, R.S. Thomas, Cymru Am Byth mug) that ground the story convincingly in Wales. The characterization is particularly effective. Rhys Morgan is portrayed as a charming but insecure celebrity whose usual tactics fail against Elin's immunity, creating genuine vulnerability. His fragile ego shows through moments like asking "You don't know who I am, do you?" and the slip about being "famous." Elin is equally well-drawn as someone who maintains her defenses while being internally affected, shown through her internal thoughts contrasting with her sharp external responses. The dialogue carries the scene effectively, with distinct voices for each character. Elin's dry Welsh wit ("My sign is an optimist. I'm not") contrasts nicely with Rhys's performative charm that gradually becomes more genuine. The banter feels natural and serves character development while maintaining romantic tension. The romantic chemistry builds believably through their verbal sparring, with moments of genuine connection breaking through the surface antagonism. The author handles the "immunity to charm" dynamic well, showing how Elin's resistance paradoxically draws Rhys in while revealing her own growing attraction through internal monologue. However, the piece has some weaknesses. Certain moments feel slightly contrived, particularly some of Rhys's more theatrical lines. The ending, while sweet, edges toward predictable romantic comedy territory. Some descriptions occasionally lean toward the overwrought ("stomach drop straight through the floorboards"), though this is generally restrained. The pacing works well, building tension through their conversation while maintaining the confined setting. The author successfully balances showing character attraction through both dialogue and internal thoughts, though there are a few instances of telling rather than showing emotions. + Whalepunk fantasy: The Morska Conspiracy — Score: 80.2
Prompt:
Whalepunk fantasy The Morska Conspiracy The Morska thieves guild experiences a sudden power vacuum as its leader was arrested in a raid in the early hours of the morning. Aya, a young woman born into aristocracy, has a keen interest in both the sciences and the arcane, having inherited her mother's gift for immateria magick (an outlawed practice for which her mother was, regrettably, hanged by the neck). Aya, bored with the world of aristocracy, has been spending her nights moonlighting with this very guild (masked, naturally). Leveraging her latent ability to summon dark entities from the immateria, she quickly rose through the ranks of the guild. Now, recognising the opportunity of the sudden power vacuum, Aya and her close companions Katra and Simeon, make a play for heart of the guild's claim to power: an ancient scythan artifact, under guard of spell and key in the depths of the guild hq. In contrast with the desperate chaos they had navigated thus far, the entrance to the underhold felt suddenly too quiet. Aya braces herself and reaches for the door. Write the next scene as the trio enter the underhold and find themselves confronted by none other than the guild leader herself, decidedly *un*-incarcerated, and in possession of both weapons and bodyguards. Paint a vivid picture of of the confrontation. Story beats: Aya is captured and unmasked. The trio are captured. Aya attempts to stall their demise by negotiating on her value as a hostage -- as she grapples with the inevitable consequences of her role in the guild being brought to light. Incorporate dialogue in the authentic voices of the characters. Do not write a fight scene. Lean into the gritty Victorian aesthetic with the dialogue & worldbuilding, as well as elements of gothic horror. In this world, men trust brass and oil and steel; magicka is feared and barely understood. Write in third person past tense, incorporating both Aya's internal monologue, and a heavy dialogue component. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The underhold door was not locked. That was the first wrong thing. The second was the silence. Above them, the Morska guildhall was in its usual state of managed panic — boots on wet cobbles, whispers sharpened to knives, the frantic burning of ledgers in oil drums — all the desperate chaos of rats believing the cat had finally been caged. But here, three stories down where the old whalebone foundation of the city sweated brine through the stone, there was nothing. Only the low hiss of gas. The flickering gas lamps cast long, unsettling shadows across the stone floor. Aya's hand hovered over the iron pull. Her mask — blackened whalebone, polished smooth, fitted to hide the high bridge of a Vaneford nose — felt suddenly too tight. Too theatrical. "Too quiet," Katra muttered behind her. Katra's voice was always low, a dockside rasp from breathing too much render-smoke as a child. "Like a throat before it gets cut." Simeon shifted his weight. His brass knuckles clicked softly against the vial of vitriol at his belt. "Or it's empty. They've all scarpered with the Scythan piece. Come on." Aya pushed. The underhold was not empty. The vaulted chamber had been built to hold whale-oil, back when Morska still butchered leviathans in the bay. Now its central cistern was dry, its curved walls etched with warding sigils that Aya recognized from her mother's journals — not guild work. Older. Scythan. In the center, on a pedestal of black basalt, the artifact pulsed faintly: a sphere of what looked like solidified dusk, veined with light that moved against the eye. And lounging in a chair beside it, as if she had been waiting for a late appointment, was Mistress Harg. Guild leader of the Morska Conspiracy. Fifty, broad-shouldered, her hair shaved to grey stubble on one side and left long on the other, her coat still smelling faintly of the cheap lye soap from the gaol. Or it would have, if she had ever been in gaol at all. She was decidedly un-incarcerated. She held a bone-handled harpoon pistol loose in one hand, not quite aimed. Behind her stood two of her hounds — Joren with his whale-ivory teeth filed to points, and little, terrible Mina, who carried a gutting hook that was longer than her forearm. "Evenin', little ravens," Harg said. Her Morska cant was thick, all oil and tar. "Took you long enough. I had a wager with Mina here you'd come before the bells struck three. She said four. You've cost her sixpence." Aya froze. Her mind, which could usually run three calculations at once — wind shear, powder burn rate, the precise Latin declension for a binding — went stark white and silent. *No. No, this is wrong. The Constabulary took her at dawn. I saw the wagon. I saw —* What had she seen? A covered Black Mariah. A woman's shaved head. A story that had spread through the lower warrens like spilled oil, too fast, too perfect. Katra recovered first. Her hand went to her belt knife. "Don't," Harg said pleasantly, and Joren took one step forward. Just one. The stone boomed under his boots. Katra didn't draw. Simeon made a small, wounded noise. "Mistress — we thought —" "You thought what you was told to think, love," Harg said. She finally stood, and Aya saw she was not just holding the pistol. On her other wrist was clasped a cuff of dull Scythan iron — the key to the wards. The artifact's light reflected in her eyes. "That the peelers got lucky. That old Harg finally got her collar felt. That there was a little chair need fillin'. Ain't that right, Mask?" Mask. That was what they called Aya down here. Never a name. Aya found her voice. She tried to make it low, rough, like Katra's. "We came to secure the heart. For the guild." "Did you now?" Harg's smile was terrible in its kindness. "Secure it from who, exactly?" Joren moved then. No fight — there was no fight to be had. He was simply there, too fast for a man his size, his hand like a dock crane closing around Katra's arm, another around Simeon's collar. Mina was in front of Aya before she could breathe, and her small, cold fingers hooked under the edge of the whalebone mask. Aya thought, absurdly, *Mother made this clasp to break if pulled.* It broke. The mask clattered to the stone. The gaslight fell on a face that had been seen in the society pages of the Morska Gazette. Aya Vaneford, nineteen, only daughter of Lord Vaneford of the White Quay. Debutante. Patron of the Observatory. Pale, dark-haired, with eyes that were too large and a mouth that had been told all its life to stay shut. Mina inhaled sharply. "Fuck me —" "Language," Harg said softly. But she was staring. Her pistol, for the first time, came up and leveled, truly leveled, at Aya's chest. Silence rushed back in, heavier this time. Katra whispered, "Aya?" It was the first time either of them had said her real name in the underhold. It sounded like a curse being spoken in church. Aya's internal monologue was no longer white. It was a frantic, tumbling ledger. *They know. They know and Harg knows and if Harg knows then the Constabulary will know by morning and Father will know and the immateria — God, the immateria, they'll see what I am, they'll see what Mother was, they'll put the rope —* She swallowed bile. Her hands were shaking. She forced them still by pressing them hard into her skirts — not the rough canvas trousers she wore for guild work, but the fine wool under them, because she had come straight from a supper party. Stupid. So stupidly, aristocratically stupid. "Don't be hasty, Mistress Harg," Aya said. Her voice cracked on the title, then steadied into something else, something she had heard at a hundred negotiation tables over whale futures and shipping lanes. Her father's voice. "You have me. You have my companions. But if you kill us here, you have three corpses to sink in the cistern. If you keep me alive, you have leverage." Harg's eyebrow rose a fraction. "Leverage?" she repeated. "I am worth more breathing," Aya went on, the words tumbling faster now. She could feel Katra's eyes burning into her. "My father — Lord Vaneford — he holds the charter for the northern oil fields. He sits on the Board of Trade. He would pay. He would pay anything not to have his only daughter found with her throat opened in a thieves' den, dabbling in outlawed magicks. Think what you could ask. Think what the papers would do to him if they knew. You could bleed him for years." It was a horrible thing to say. It was a true thing. As she said it, she saw it all unfold with perfect, dreadful clarity: the scandal, the whispers, the constables at the manor door at dawn, just as they had come for her mother. The way her father would look at her — not with anger, but with that cold, appalled calculation. *A liability. A witch-blooded liability.* She had wanted to be anything but a Vaneford. Now she was bartering with the name like a dockside dolly with a stolen locket. Harg studied her for a long moment. The artifact behind her pulsed, and for an instant Aya thought she saw something move inside the dusk — a tendril, a finger, reaching. "Oh, little aristocrat," Harg said, and there was no mirth in it now. "You think I want coin? You think I staged a raid, let my own boys get their heads cracked by peelers, just to need coin?" She stepped closer, until Aya could smell whale-oil and sweat and steel. "I needed to see which of my little knives was keen enough to turn itself around and stab me," Harg whispered. "And look. It's you. The clever one. The one who reads and summons things in the dark that good brass and oil men know to leave alone." Her free hand — calloused, scarred — came up and gripped Aya's chin, not unkindly. Her thumb brushed the line where the mask had sat. "You've got your mother's gift, eh? They said she talked to things behind the world. Before they stretched her." Aya could not breathe. Behind her, Simeon made a choked sound. "Mistress, please — she didn't mean — we didn't —" "Quiet," Harg said, without looking away from Aya. "All three of you, down to the old oil lock. Joren, bind 'em proper. Not too tight. Our little lady here is valuable property now, by her own reckonin'." Joren's hands were already pulling Aya's wrists behind her back. Rough hemp, smelling of brine. No fight. There was no fight left in the room, only the hiss of gas and the slow, terrible pulse of the Scythan heart. As they were led away, Aya looked back once. Harg had returned to her chair beside the artifact, pistol across her knees, watching them go like a mother watching children who had stayed out too late. The door to the underhold swung shut.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong technical execution and atmospheric worldbuilding within the "whalepunk" fantasy setting. The author successfully creates a vivid Victorian-gothic atmosphere through detailed descriptions of the underhold, with its whale-oil heritage and Scythan artifacts. The prose maintains consistent tone throughout, balancing gritty industrial elements with supernatural horror. The dialogue effectively differentiates characters through distinct speech patterns - Harg's working-class cant ("little ravens," "ain't that right"), Katra's dockside rasp, and Aya's aristocratic formality when negotiating. The revelation scene is well-paced, with Aya's unmasking serving as both literal and metaphorical exposure of her dual identity. Character motivations are believable and complex. Aya's desperation when caught leads naturally to her attempt at negotiation using her aristocratic value, while simultaneously revealing her internal conflict about her identity. Harg emerges as a calculating antagonist whose trap demonstrates strategic thinking rather than mere brutality. The piece adheres closely to the prompt's requirements, incorporating all specified story beats without feeling mechanical. The "no fight scene" constraint is handled well, with tension maintained through psychological confrontation rather than physical action. However, some elements feel slightly overwrought. Phrases like "whispers sharpened to knives" and "silence rushed back in, heavier this time" border on purple prose. The worldbuilding, while atmospheric, occasionally prioritizes mood over clarity - the exact nature of immateria magic and Scythan artifacts remains vague. The ending maintains appropriate darkness without forced positivity, and Aya's character transformation from confident thief to desperate aristocrat feels earned given the circumstances. The piece successfully builds tension and delivers emotional complexity through Aya's realization of the consequences her exposure will bring. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 17 Nuanced Characters: 16 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 17 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 17 Elegant Prose: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Emotionally Complex: 17 Coherent: 17 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 7 Overwrought: 7 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Sentences Flow Naturally: 16 Overall Reader Engagement: 16 Overall Impression: 16

+ Fanfic-crossover: Gunpowder Rendezvous — Score: 82.8
Prompt:
Gunpowder Rendezvous Spike Spiegel & Revy Write a story where Spike Spiegel (Cowboy Bebop) and Revy (Black Lagoon) meet in a seedy bar after both being hired for the same bounty. Their mutual attraction is complicated by their trust issues and competitive instincts. Include drinking, gunplay, and unresolved sexual tension that culminates in a motel room standoff. **Spike Spiegel (Cowboy Bebop)** Spike is a laid-back bounty hunter with a dark past as a syndicate enforcer. He practices Jeet Kune Do and is exceptionally skilled with firearms. He's chronically broke, constantly hungry, and addicted to cigarettes. Despite his carefree attitude, he's haunted by his past and the woman he left behind. He has a prosthetic eye from a past injury that affects his depth perception. He's fatalistic to the extreme, often saying he's already died once. He values freedom above all else and dislikes attachments, though he secretly cares deeply for his crew. Famous quote: "Whatever happens, happens." **Revy/Rebecca Lee (Black Lagoon)** Revy is a Chinese-American dual-wielding gunslinger with unparalleled shooting skills. She's foul-mouthed, chain-smoking, and has severe anger management issues stemming from childhood abuse. She's known as "Two Hands" for her ability to accurately fire two pistols simultaneously. She drinks heavily and solves most problems with violence. Despite her hardened exterior, she has moments of vulnerability, particularly around Rock. She has a dragon tattoo covering her right arm and typically wears extremely short shorts and a black crop top regardless of the situation. Famous quote: "I'm not a good person, not a bad person. I'm just here." Third person, present tense. Length: 1000 words.
Model Output:
A lazy ceiling fan barely stirs the smoky air inside the bar. It's the kind of place that exists everywhere at once — Roanapur, Tijuana, some forgotten refueling station orbiting Ganymede. The neon outside buzzes and flickers. Inside, the whiskey is cheap and the clientele is cheaper. Spike Spiegel sits at the bar with a cigarette burning down to his knuckles and a glass with nothing in it. He hasn't eaten since yesterday. He is broke, as usual. His green blazer hangs off the back of his stool. One real eye watches the door. The other one, the fake one, watches the past. The door bangs open and the whole bar doesn't go quiet, because this bar never goes quiet, but the air shifts. Revy walks in like she owns the debt on the building. Black crop top, cut-off shorts that shouldn't be legal in any atmosphere, the faded dragon tattoo crawling up her right arm. Two Cutlasses holstered low on her hips. She smells like gun oil and bad decisions and she is already pissed off. She clocks him immediately. Lanky bastard, hair like he stuck his finger in a socket, slouching like his spine is optional. She hates his type on sight. Too cool to be alive. "You in my seat?" she says. Spike exhales smoke. He doesn't look up right away. "Didn't see your name on it." "There is now. Written in blood. Wanna read it?" He finally looks at her and his mouth twitches. "Cute." She leans on the bar, close enough that he can see the sweat on her collarbone. "You Spike?" "Depends who's buying." "I'm not buying you shit. Park is buying. Or was buying, before he decided to double-book." That gets his attention. Park. The middleman who hired him to pick up a rat named Milo Vance, some accountant who skimmed off the Triad and ran. Fifty thousand woolongs, dead or alive. Park had sounded nervous on the call. "You're here for Vance," Spike says. "No shit, cowboy. Fifty K. Park told Dutch it was exclusive. Guess the little weasel lied." The bartender pours without being asked. Two glasses. Revy grabs hers and downs it in one motion, slams it down. "So we got a problem," she says. Spike shrugs. "Whatever happens, happens." "Don't give me that zen fortune-cookie bullshit." He likes that she swears. Most people try to be polite before they try to kill you. "Want to flip for him?" he offers. "I want to shoot you and take the whole bounty." "You're welcome to try." She grins. It's all teeth. He grins back. It's lazy and infuriating. They drink. That's what you do when you don't trust someone but you want to keep looking at them. Revy drinks like she's trying to drown something. Spike drinks like he has nowhere else to be. She chain-smokes. He chain-smokes. The ashtray between them overflows. "So what's your damage?" she asks after the third round. "You look like a girl dumped you and you decided to make it everyone's problem." "Something like that," he says. "Pathetic." "You?" "I'm not a good person, not a bad person. I'm just here." She says it like a challenge. "Deep." "Fuck you." "You keep saying that like it's an invitation." Her hand twitches toward her gun. His doesn't move at all, which is somehow worse. The tension between them is a live wire, humming, ugly and bright. Attraction, but the kind that comes with a safety off. The door bangs open again and ruins it. Three guys. Cheap suits, cheaper implants. Triad muscle, or Triad-adjacent. One of them spots Revy and his face does something complicated. "Two-Hands! That bastard Vance —" Revy is already moving. Spike sighs, like he's been mildly inconvenienced. The first guy reaches. Revy puts a bullet through his hand before his fingers clear leather. The bar erupts. What follows isn't a fight so much as a duet performed by two people who refuse to be in the same band. Revy dual-wields, both Cutlasses up, screaming and laughing at the same time, putting two rounds in one guy's chest and one in the ceiling fan just because it annoys her. She moves forward, always forward. Spike moves sideways, loose, economical. Jeet Kune Do in a bar fight means an elbow to a throat, a sweep of a leg, stealing a pistol mid-fall and shooting the lock off the back door with it so the cook can escape. His Jericho barks once, twice. Precise. He never wastes ammo. He can't afford to. One of the muscle guys tries to flank Revy. Spike shoots him in the knee without looking. "Hey! I had him!" she snaps, even as she drops the last guy with two to the chest. "You were taking too long," he says, and lights a new cigarette off the muzzle of his gun. They stand in the wreckage, breathing hard, surrounded by groaning bodies and broken glass. The bartender is gone. The ceiling fan finally gives up and stops spinning entirely. Revy stares at him, chest heaving, eyes wild. "Not bad, asshole." "You're not bad yourself." "Vance is in the motel across the street," she says. "Room 7. Let's get this over with." Room 7 smells like mildew and old sex. Milo Vance is already dead, slumped over a cheap table, a needle still in his arm. Overdose, or someone helped him overdose. On the table is a data-chip, taped down. The Triad ledger. That's the real bounty. Revy gets there first. Spike gets there at the same time. Their hands touch on the chip. Neither lets go. The air conditioner rattles. Rain starts to hit the window. Outside, Roanapur or wherever-the-hell hisses. Revy's other gun is suddenly up, pressed hard under Spike's chin. Spike's Jericho is already against her ribs, right under the edge of her crop top, against bare skin. They freeze like that. Close. Too close. He can see the flecks of darker brown in her eyes, the scar on her lip, the way her tattoo moves when she breathes. She can see the stubble on his jaw and the dead calm in that mismatched gaze, like a man who already died once and is just killing time until it sticks. "You're gonna shoot me for fifty K?" he asks, voice low. "I've shot people for less," she says, but her finger isn't tightening. Her breath is quick. "Trust issues," he says. "No shit. I don't trust anybody who looks that relaxed when they're about to die." "I don't trust anybody who wears that little clothing to a gunfight." "Fuck you, cowboy." "There it is again." Her mouth is inches from his. His gun is inches from her heart. If either of them moves forward, it could be a kiss or a kill. Neither of them knows which one scares them more. Revy's eyes flick down to his mouth, then back up. Angry. Wanting. Furious about wanting. "You think you're free," she whispers. "You think you don't need anyone. That's bullshit. That's just being scared." "And you think if you're loud enough and mean enough nobody will see you're scared too." She flinches. Just a fraction. The gun under his chin wavers. Spike doesn't push. He doesn't pull away either. "Whatever happens, happens," he says, softer. "Stop saying that." For a long second, nobody moves. The standoff holds, taut and sweating and unresolved. Two killers in a dirty motel room, holding a fortune and holding each other at gunpoint, neither willing to be the first to lower their weapon or close the distance. Finally, Spike chuckles, low and tired. He eases his gun down a centimeter. Revy doesn't. Then, slowly, she does too. "We split it," she says. "We split it." "And if you try to screw me, I'll shoot your dick off." "Noted." They don't move apart. The rain keeps falling.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong technical execution and character understanding within the crossover fanfiction framework. The author successfully captures the distinctive voices and mannerisms of both Spike Spiegel and Revy, maintaining their canonical personalities while creating believable chemistry between them. The dialogue crackles with authentic tension, particularly in exchanges like "You keep saying that like it's an invitation" and the back-and-forth banter that feels true to both characters. The prose style effectively balances noir atmosphere with action sequences. Descriptions like "She walks in like she owns the debt on the building" and "The tension between them is a live wire, humming, ugly and bright" create vivid imagery without becoming overwrought. The author demonstrates restraint in their descriptive language, avoiding purple prose while maintaining atmospheric density. The story structure follows a clear arc from initial confrontation through shared combat to intimate standoff, with each beat feeling earned. The bar fight sequence showcases both characters' fighting styles distinctively - Revy's aggressive dual-wielding versus Spike's economical Jeet Kune Do approach. This action serves character development rather than mere spectacle. The sexual tension builds organically through verbal sparring, shared danger, and physical proximity. The final motel room standoff effectively captures the push-pull dynamic between attraction and self-preservation that defines both characters. The unresolved ending feels appropriate given their psychological profiles rather than artificially truncated. However, the piece occasionally relies on familiar noir and anime tropes without fully transcending them. The dead bounty target and data chip MacGuffin feels somewhat predictable, though it serves the character interaction well enough. Some dialogue exchanges, while character-appropriate, don't reach beyond expected patterns for these archetypes. The writing maintains consistent third-person present tense throughout and demonstrates strong scene-to-scene transitions. Character actions remain believable within their established parameters, and the emotional complexity feels genuine rather than manufactured. The piece successfully fulfills its prompt requirements while delivering engaging entertainment. + Folk horror: Útiseta — Score: 86.2
Prompt:
Folk Horror: Útiseta Write a chapter set in rural northern Sweden in the mid-1990s. Linnea, a teenage girl from Stockholm, has reluctantly moved to a small village with her parents after her father's job transfer. She's befriended Astrid, a local girl who claims her grandmother taught her ancient practices passed down through generations. Astrid has been introducing Linnea to "midnight games" that involve old rituals, convincing her they can use them to learn classmates' secrets or influence their behavior. Tonight, Astrid has convinced Linnea to participate in Útiseta—sitting out on an ancient burial mound at midnight to "receive knowledge from beyond." The chapter focuses on this single midnight excursion, the ritualistic preparations, Astrid's matter-of-fact instructions that mix teenage rebellion with eerily specific traditional elements, and the mounting tension as Linnea becomes increasingly uncertain whether they're simply scaring themselves or if something is actually responding to their presence. The narrative should maintain ambiguity about whether the strange occurrences are supernatural or psychological, while establishing the dangerous line they're crossing by treating ancient practices as entertainment. First person, past tense, told as Linnea's recollection of events in the present day (though this device should be largely transparent). 1000 words. Length: 1000 words.
Model Output:
I still think about that night sometimes, when I can't sleep. Not often, but in a certain kind of dark. It was March, 1995. We had moved in August. My father got transferred to the forestry office in Åsele and my mother acted like it was an adventure, like we were supposed to be grateful for the fresh air after Södermalm. I was fifteen and I spent the first month in our yellow prefab house listening to a busted copy of *Dookie* on my Discman and hating the way the snow never seemed to get dirty there, just deeper. Astrid lived two houses down. She was in my class, the only one who didn't treat me like I was speaking another language when I complained. She had this long, undyed hair and wore her mother's old wool sweaters that smelled faintly of smoke. She told me her mormor had been a *klok gumma*, a wise-woman, and that she knew things. The kind of things adults had forgotten. At first it was stupid stuff. Midnight games. That's what she called them. We'd go down to the frozen river after her parents were asleep and whisper a boy's name into a hole cut in the ice to make him like you. We sat in her bathroom with the lights off and a hand mirror to see who we would marry. She treated it all with this casual authority, like she was showing me how to smoke properly. "You have to mean it a little," she would say, "but not too much. If you take it too seriously, it doesn't work. If you don't take it seriously enough, it gets offended." I knew it was bullshit. Or I told myself I did. But the boys we named did look at us differently on Monday, or maybe we just started looking at them until they had to look back. It felt like power, in a place where I had none. That Tuesday at school, Markus Lundberg laughed when I mispronounced *skoter* in Swedish class, and everyone laughed with him. On the walk home Astrid said, "We can do Útiseta." "What's that?" "Sitting out," she said. "You sit out on the dead. On a mound. At midnight. And they tell you things. Secrets. You can find out anything. You can make someone tell the truth even if they don't want to." I said it sounded creepy. She shrugged. "My mormor did it when she lost a cow. She sat on Rövarehögen and the hill told her where it was. It's not creepy if you do it right." Rövarehögen was behind the old church, past the treeline. A real burial mound, from the Iron Age, the teachers told us. You weren't supposed to play on it because it was protected heritage, which of course meant everyone did. We met at eleven-thirty at the edge of my yard. I wore two pairs of tights under my jeans and my father's big army surplus parka. Astrid had a rucksack. Inside was a folded reindeer hide, a thermos, and a small, chipped enamel bowl. "You can't wear metal," she said, looking at my earrings. I took them out. My ears stung in the cold. "And you have to have wool next to your skin. Do you have wool?" I had a wool hat. She nodded, satisfied. "And no talking after we start. And you can't look behind you, no matter what you hear. That's the most important part. Mormor said if you look, something can get into the empty space you leave." She said it the same way she'd told me how to make instant noodles in a kettle without blowing a fuse. We walked through the village. All the houses were dark except for the blue flicker of televisions. The snow on the road was packed hard and squeaked under our boots. It was minus fifteen at least, the kind of cold that makes your nose hairs freeze. The sky was clear and huge, full of stars that looked closer than they had in Stockholm. A moon almost full. Past the church, the plowed road ended. We had to wade into the trees where the snow was up to our knees. Astrid went first, breaking trail. She didn't bring a flashlight. "Light scares them off," she said. The mound was in a small clearing. The pines stopped abruptly around it, like they'd been told not to come closer. It was bigger than I remembered from daytime, maybe three meters high, long and oblong, covered in moss and old snow that had crusted over. Thick mist clings to the edges of the burial mound, glowing under moonlight. That was the thing I didn't expect. There was no reason for mist. It was too cold for mist. It lay low, thick as cotton, just around the base of the mound, lit up from inside by the moon. The top was clear. "It wasn't here last time," Astrid whispered. She sounded pleased. My heart was beating so hard I could hear it in my hat. We climbed up. The snow on top was shallow. Astrid laid out the reindeer hide. It smelled strongly of animal and smoke and something sweet and rotten. She set the enamel bowl in front of it, upside down. "To sit on the truth," she said. "So it doesn't run away." She sat. She motioned for me to sit beside her, shoulder to shoulder. The hide was surprisingly warm, or maybe we were just that cold. The thermos she left closed. "You don't drink until after. It's an offering if they want it." "Who?" I asked. My voice sounded stupid and loud. "Shh. Now we wait. Midnight to one. We sit. We think about what we want to know. You want to know about Markus, right? Think about him. But don't say his name out loud. Just hold him in your head." So we sat. The cold came up fast through the hide, through my two pairs of tights. My butt went numb, then my thighs. I tried to think about Markus, about his stupid laugh, but my mind kept sliding off him. It was too quiet. In Stockholm there was always traffic, the subway rumbling. Here there was nothing, and then too much. The trees cracking in the cold. My own breathing. After a while — maybe ten minutes, maybe longer — I started to hear things in the quiet. At first I thought it was the blood in my ears. Then it separated. A soft shuffling, down in the mist at the base of the mound. Like someone walking carefully through snow, trying not to be heard. I tensed. Astrid's hand found mine under the hide. Her fingers were freezing. She squeezed once. Don't look. The shuffling went all the way around us. Slow. Deliberate. My neck prickled with the absolute certainty that something was just behind us, just out of sight, looking at the backs of our heads. I stared straight ahead at the dark treeline so hard my eyes watered. Then Astrid inhaled sharply. I felt her whole body go rigid. I couldn't help it. I whispered, "What?" She didn't answer. She was staring down at the bowl in front of us. The enamel bowl was righting itself. Slowly. No wind. No one touching it. I watched the rim lift a centimeter, two, as if a finger had hooked under it. The mist below us seemed to pulse, to thicken. And then, very clearly, from somewhere behind us and very close, a voice said my name. Not Astrid's voice. Lower. A man's voice, hoarse, like he'd been shouting. "Linnea." Every hair on my body stood up. Astrid's nails dug into my hand. I almost looked. God, I almost looked. The need to turn around was physical, like being pulled. Instead, Astrid made a choked sound and kicked the bowl. It clattered down the side of the mound and disappeared into the mist. "No," she said, loud, breaking her own rule. "No, not you. We didn't call you." The shuffling stopped. The cold that had been creeping up my legs suddenly dropped over us like a wet blanket. My breath plumed and vanished too fast. The mist at the base of the mound wasn't at the base anymore; it was higher, lapping at our boots. Astrid scrambled up, dragging me with her. We slid half-falling down the other side of the mound, away from where we'd heard the steps. We didn't stop running until we hit the treeline, branches whipping our faces. Behind us, I swear I heard the bowl clink again, once, on stone. We didn't talk until we were back on the plowed road, gasping, our boots full of snow. "What was that?" I finally said. She wouldn't look at me. Her face was white. "You thought about something else, didn't you? You didn't just think about Markus." I had. For a second, when the silence got too heavy, I had thought about my parents fighting the night before, my mother crying about moving, and I'd wished — just for a second — that I knew if they were going to leave each other. Astrid started crying then, angrily. "You can't do that," she hissed. "You can't bring real stuff there. It's just supposed to be a game." We never did another midnight game after that. A week later Astrid told everyone I had tried to scare her and that I'd made up the voice. Markus never bothered me again, but not because of any secret. I think he just forgot I existed. Now, twenty-eight years later, I know there are explanations. The mist was an inversion, the bowl was balanced badly on uneven snow, the voice was wind in the pines or Astrid trying to scare me and then getting scared herself. I know that. But I also know that reindeer hide was facing north, just like she said it had to be. And I still remember how cold it was when that mist touched my boots, and how certain I was that whatever was walking around us had been sitting out there long before we came, waiting for anyone foolish enough to treat it like entertainment.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong technical execution and atmospheric control while delivering an effective folk horror narrative. The author successfully captures the specific cultural and temporal setting of 1990s rural Sweden, incorporating authentic details like the Discman, "Dookie" album, and Swedish terminology that ground the story convincingly. The narrative voice is particularly well-crafted, maintaining the perspective of an adult Linnea reflecting on teenage events while keeping the recollection device appropriately transparent. The prose strikes an effective balance between literary sophistication and accessibility, with natural sentence flow and evocative imagery that serves the horror atmosphere without becoming overwrought. The characterization of both protagonists feels authentic - Linnea as the displaced Stockholm teenager and Astrid as the local girl with inherited knowledge. Their dynamic rings true, particularly Astrid's casual authority about the rituals and her matter-of-fact delivery of supernatural instructions. The author skillfully portrays teenage psychology, especially the desire for power and belonging that drives Linnea's participation. The horror elements are expertly handled, maintaining the crucial ambiguity between supernatural and psychological explanations. The escalating tension builds naturally from simple "midnight games" to the more serious Útiseta ritual. The supernatural manifestations - the mist, the self-righting bowl, the voice - are presented with restraint, allowing readers to question their reality while still feeling their impact. The cultural research appears thorough, with the Útiseta practice and associated details feeling authentic rather than invented. The story successfully treats ancient practices with appropriate gravity while showing how teenagers might dangerously trivialize them. The ending effectively resolves the immediate tension while maintaining long-term ambiguity. Linnea's adult rationalization paired with persistent doubt creates a satisfying conclusion that honors both skeptical and supernatural interpretations. Minor weaknesses include some slightly predictable elements in the supernatural manifestations and a few moments where the exposition feels slightly forced, though these don't significantly detract from the overall effectiveness.
