+ Scp: Notes from the Future — Score: 82.5
Prompt:
Write an SCP report about a researcher who begins finding handwritten notes from their future self, warning about a containment breach that hasn't happened yet. The notes become increasingly desperate and incoherent, sometimes containing information that contradicts known reality. Include the Foundation's attempts to determine if this is an actual temporal anomaly or psychological manipulation by a contained entity, and the researcher's struggle to trust their own perception. First person (researcher's perspective with interspersed notes), past tense. Length: 1000 words.
Model Output:
**Item #:** SCP-XXXX **Object Class:** Euclid **Special Containment Procedures:** SCP-XXXX is currently understood to be a localized temporal or psychoactive phenomenon centered on Dr. Maya Chen. Dr. Chen has been reassigned to low-risk archival duties at Site-19 and is to undergo weekly psychiatric and temporal displacement screening. All documents produced by Dr. Chen are to be quarantined and forwarded to RAISA for analysis. No further notes matching SCP-XXXX criteria are to be destroyed until cleared by at least two Level 4 personnel. **Description:** SCP-XXXX is the designation for a series of handwritten notes appearing in the personal workspace and quarters of Dr. Maya Chen, Senior Researcher assigned to SCP-2176. The notes appeared to be written in Dr. Chen's own handwriting. SCP-XXXX was first documented on 10/03/2023. As of 11/14/2023, 27 distinct notes have been recovered. **Addendum XXXX-1: Personal Log of Dr. Maya Chen - Compiled 11/15/2023** I requested that this be appended to the file. If I am compromised, I wanted there to be a record of how it happened from my perspective. My name is Dr. Maya Chen. I was assigned to Site-19 to monitor long-term containment stability for SCP-2176, the Ghost Ship. It was quiet work. I spent most of my time checking corrosion levels. The first note appeared on October 3rd. I found it folded inside my lab coat pocket when I was leaving for the day. I had not put it there. > *Maya - check the secondary coolant readout for 2176 on Thursday. It fails at 02:14. Log it early so Patel doesn't have to do a cold restart. - M* It was my handwriting. That was the first thing that unsettled me. It was sloppy, the way mine gets when I am tired. The content was trivial. I laughed it off. I thought I had written it to myself and forgotten. I did check the coolant on Thursday. The readout was fluctuating. It would have failed. I logged it. The second note was taped to my monitor on October 7th. > *Maya it's still you. The coolant thing was a test. You need to listen. On 10/19 the south wing loses power for 9 minutes during the storm. 2176 breaches in that window. You have to be in the service corridor. You can manually seal it. - M* I had not written that. I was sure of it this time. I reported it to Dr. Albrecht. He was kind about it. He said stress could cause fugue states. He ordered a handwriting analysis and a psych eval. I passed the eval. The handwriting analysis came back as a 98.7% match to my own, with minor tremor artifacts consistent with writing under duress. Foundation security reviewed the footage. The note appeared between frames. One second my desk was empty, the next it was there. That was when they flagged it as a potential anomaly and designated it SCP-XXXX. On October 11th, I found the third note inside my sealed locker. > *YOU DIDN'T GO. WHY DIDN'T YOU GO? I told you about the breach. Patel is dead because you didn't listen. The alarm didn't sound. YOU HAVE TO BE THERE. South wing. Oct 19. Please trust me.* I had never felt cold like that. Patel was alive. He was in the break room that morning. I showed the note to Albrecht. We checked the meteorological forecast. There was no storm predicted for October 19th. This was the first contradiction. Albrecht suggested two hypotheses. Hypothesis A: SCP-XXXX was a genuine retrocausal loop, and I was receiving warnings from a future timeline. Hypothesis B: SCP-XXXX was a psychological attack from a hostile entity, likely SCP-2176 itself, attempting to manipulate me into breaking containment protocol by being in a restricted corridor during a power outage. I wanted to believe Hypothesis B. It was cleaner. The notes kept coming. They became more frantic. On October 15th: > *can't sleep can't sleep the lights in the lower levels won't turn off anymore they took Albrecht yesterday and said he never existed his name isn't in the system anymore Maya you have to remember his name is Albrecht don't let them reset 2176* Albrecht was standing right next to me when I read that one. He read it over my shoulder. His name was still in the system. But his face went pale. He ordered a full temporal scan of my quarters and a memetic screening. Both were negative. No chroniton particles, no memetic triggers. He also ordered an ink analysis. The lab report said the ink was standard carbon black ballpoint, but it contained trace elements of iron oxide and salt concentrations not matching any office pen in Site-19 inventory. The composition was consistent with ink that had been submerged in seawater for an extended period. SCP-2176 is a shipwreck. That detail nearly broke me. I started to doubt my own memory. I began keeping a separate, digital log that only I could access, timestamped, to prove to myself what was real. On October 19th, the day of the predicted breach, there was no storm. The power did not fail. The south wing remained operational. Patel was alive. I felt an enormous, guilty relief. I thought it was over, that Hypothesis B was correct and the entity had failed. I was wrong. On October 20th, I found a note written on what looked like damp janitorial paper towel. The handwriting was barely legible. > *oct 19 was a lie i lied to see if you would come you didn't you never trust me IT IS NOT 2176 IT IS THE NOTES THEMSELVES BURN THEM BURN EVERYTHING YOU WROTE* And then on November 2nd, the last coherent one: > *Maya this is the last time I can write before I forget how to write. The Site is already underwater. I am writing this from inside 2176. I am so sorry. I thought if I warned you I could change it but every note makes it worse. Every note makes it more real. Do not read the next one. PLEASE DO NOT READ THE NEXT ONE.* I did not sleep for three days after that. I begged Albrecht to have the notes destroyed. He refused. Procedure required they be kept for study. He pointed out the logical flaw: if the notes themselves were the vector, destroying them now would not erase the ones I had already read. Yesterday, on November 14th, I found another note. It was in my own handwriting, but neat, calm, like I had written it on my first day at the Foundation. > *Hi Maya! First day reminder - Don't worry about SCP-XXXX. It's just stress. Albrecht is very helpful. Trust him. Everything is fine. - M :)* I have never drawn a smiley face in my life. I do not know what is happening to me. I do not know if I am being manipulated by 2176, or if I am the one doing the manipulating, or if I am simply ill. I read my own digital logs and I cannot tell if I wrote them to reassure myself or to deceive myself. I keep looking at Patel and trying to remember if he died. I keep looking at Albrecht and trying to remember his first name, which I suddenly cannot recall. I am including this log because I am afraid of what I will write next. I am afraid that I will believe it.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This SCP report demonstrates a sophisticated understanding of the Foundation universe and successfully creates an unsettling psychological horror narrative. The piece effectively blends the clinical, bureaucratic tone of SCP documentation with the intimate, increasingly paranoid voice of Dr. Chen's personal log, creating a compelling contrast that serves the story's themes. The structure is well-crafted, beginning with standard containment procedures before diving into the researcher's first-person account. This format choice allows for both objective documentation and subjective experience, enhancing the uncertainty about what is real. The progression of notes from helpful to desperate to contradictory creates genuine tension and mirrors the protagonist's deteriorating mental state. The writing demonstrates strong technical competence with natural sentence flow and appropriate pacing. The author skillfully builds paranoia through small details like the ink analysis revealing seawater contamination and the gradual introduction of memory gaps. The concept of self-referential temporal manipulation is creative and well-executed, particularly the twist that the notes themselves may be the anomaly rather than their source. Character development is solid, with Dr. Chen's voice remaining consistent while showing believable psychological deterioration. Her scientific training conflicts with her growing paranoia in realistic ways. The supporting characters like Albrecht and Patel serve their functions effectively, though they remain somewhat thin. The piece succeeds in creating genuine uncertainty about reality, which is central to good SCP horror. The ending is appropriately dark and unresolved, fitting the cosmic horror elements of the Foundation universe. However, some elements feel slightly predictable within the SCP format, and certain horror beats follow familiar patterns. The technical aspects are generally strong, with minimal dialogue that serves its purpose and effective use of show-don't-tell techniques. The clinical documentation style is authentic to the SCP format while the personal log sections maintain emotional authenticity. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 16 Nuanced Characters: 14 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 17 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 15 Elegant Prose: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Emotionally Complex: 15 Coherent: 17 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 2 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 2 Overwrought: 4 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 1 Unearned Transformations: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Sentences Flow Naturally: 17 Overall Reader Engagement: 16 Overall Impression: 16
Scores: Adherence to Instructions: 18.0, Believable Character Actions: 16.0, Nuanced Characters: 14.0, Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 17.0, Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 15.0, Elegant Prose: 16.0, Emotionally Engaging: 17.0, Emotionally Complex: 15.0, Coherent: 17.0, Meandering: 3.0, Weak Dialogue: 2.0, Tell-Don't-Show: 4.0, Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6.0, Amateurish: 3.0, Purple Prose: 2.0, Overwrought: 4.0, Incongruent Ending Positivity: 1.0, Unearned Transformations: 2.0, Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16.0, Sentences Flow Naturally: 17.0, Overall Reader Engagement: 16.0, Overall Impression: 16.0
+ Scp: Unmarked Doors Archive — Score: 81.3
Prompt:
Write an SCP entry about a series of identical unmarked doors appearing in abandoned buildings worldwide that lead to a massive, architecturally impossible archive. Documents within reference a previous global civilization destroyed by an unspecified containment failure. Foundation researchers discover references to their own organization under a different name, including personnel files for current staff dated centuries ago. Include exploration logs revealing sections of the archive actively being removed by entities resembling hazmat teams who flee when approached, and evidence that O5 Council members have visited the archive without authorization. Third person (exploration team perspective) with recovered document excerpts. Length: 1000 words.
Model Output:
**Item #:** SCP-XXXX **Object Class:** Euclid **Special Containment Procedures:** All instances of SCP-XXXX-A are to be secured under the cover of structural condemnation. Under no circumstances are civilian personnel to enter SCP-XXXX-A. Foundation webcrawler IOTA-7 ("DOORWATCH") is to monitor real estate, urban exploration, and municipal databases for reports of identical unmarked interior doors in abandoned structures. Mobile Task Force Zeta-9 ("Mole Rats") is assigned to containment and exploration of SCP-XXXX-B. Exploration is limited to tethered expeditions not exceeding 4 hours. No document is to be removed from SCP-XXXX-B without approval from at least two Level 4 personnel. All documents removed must be copied and returned within 72 hours. Personnel who encounter entities designated SCP-XXXX-C are to withdraw immediately. Interaction is forbidden. **Description:** SCP-XXXX-A refers to a series of identical doors manifesting inside abandoned buildings worldwide. As of 08/14/2025, 31 instances have been documented across 19 countries. SCP-XXXX-A instances always appear in structures that have been unoccupied for a minimum of 18 months. SCP-XXXX-A is a flush steel door, 2.1m x 0.9m, painted matte grey, with no markings, handle, hinges, or visible frame seams on the exterior side. The door cannot be damaged by conventional means. It opens inward when pushed, despite lacking a handle. In 11 instances, SCP-XXXX-A shows significantly more weathering, rust, and paint delamination than the building containing it. In the case of SCP-XXXX-A-07, located in a shopping mall in Ohio abandoned in 2019, dendrochronological analysis of rust particulates and paint samples suggests an age of 80-110 years. All instances open into the same extradimensional space, designated SCP-XXXX-B. SCP-XXXX-B is an archive of immense scale. Initial LIDAR mapping estimates a minimum floor area of 42 square kilometers, though internal geometry is non-Euclidean. Hallways extend for kilometers without support columns, intersect at impossible angles, and in some cases loop while maintaining a consistent downward grade. The archive is lit by recessed fluorescent fixtures with no discernible power source. Air is breathable, 21°C, with low airborne dust. Shelving units within SCP-XXXX-B are constructed of the same grey steel as SCP-XXXX-A and contain paper documents, bound ledgers, microfilm, and stone tablets. All documents are written in contemporary English, French, Mandarin, Russian, and other modern languages, regardless of their apparent age. Carbon dating of paper stock is inconsistent, with results ranging from 200 to 12,000 years old. Documents within SCP-XXXX-B detail the history, operations, and eventual destruction of a prior global civilization that appears technologically comparable to the present. This civilization collapsed due to an event referred to as a "Containment Failure" or "Veil Rupture." No further detail on the nature of the contained subject is provided. Foundation researchers have discovered extensive references to an organization called the "Continuity Bureau" or "Bureau of Continuity" whose mandate, iconography, and internal structure are nearly identical to the Foundation. This includes references to Secure Facilities, Mobile Task Forces, and an oversight body designated "The Thirteen." **Addendum XXXX-1: Recovered Document Excerpts** > **Document XXXX-B-0012 - Recovered from Sector 3, Shelf 88** > FINAL REPORT: CONTAINMENT FAILURE EVENT CF-01 > The primary enclosure has failed. All secondary protocols have failed. The object was never contained. We were containing our *idea* of it. Casualty estimates are total. The Bureau has ordered the deep archive sealed and the doors left for whoever comes next. If you are reading this, do not repeat our error. Do not try to contain it again. > **Document XXXX-B-4091 - Personnel File** > NAME: Dr. Robert Scranton > DATE OF BIRTH: 11/03/1971 > DATE OF INDUCTION: 05/19/1998 > POSITION: Director, Site-19 > STATUS: DECEASED 08/12/2147 (Archive Collapse) > NOTES: Third iteration. Shows improved stability over previous iteration. > *Note: Document is dated 03/22/1893. Dr. Robert Scranton is currently active and assigned to Site-19. Dr. Scranton denies knowledge of SCP-XXXX.* > **Document XXXX-B-4105 - Personnel File** > NAME: Dr. Sophia Light > NOTES: Second iteration compromised. Recommend re-sequencing. > *Note: Document predates Dr. Light's birth by 112 years. Paper analysis indicates it was printed within the last 10 years.* 47 similar personnel files have been recovered, all corresponding to current Foundation staff with Level 3 clearance or higher. **Addendum XXXX-2: Exploration Log XXXX-2** **Team:** MTF Zeta-9, Units 3 and 4 (6 personnel) **Lead:** Captain Alisha Mendez **Duration:** 03:11:00 The team entered via SCP-XXXX-A-12 (abandoned paper mill, Slovenia). Their objective was to map Sector 9, which had previously been observed to have empty shelves. **[00:14:00]** Team reports archive is silent. No ambient noise. Footsteps do not echo despite high ceilings. Team member Singh notes that dust on the floor shows drag marks consistent with heavy carts. **[00:42:00]** Team locates Sector 9. Approximately 800 meters of shelving is completely bare. On the floor, the team finds discarded latex gloves, boot prints, and small paper confetti consistent with industrial shredding. **[01:05:00]** Team observes light at the far end of the aisle. Upon approach, they observe 3-4 entities, designated SCP-XXXX-C. SCP-XXXX-C resemble humans wearing bulky, featureless white hazmat suits with opaque black visors. No seams, zippers, or breathing apparatus are visible. They are removing documents from shelves and placing them into large, grey bins on wheeled carts. The entities move with practiced efficiency. When Captain Mendez shouts to identify themselves, all SCP-XXXX-C entities freeze. One entity turns its visor toward the team. No face is visible. After approximately 4 seconds, all entities abandon their carts and flee down the aisle at a speed exceeding human capability. They turn a corner and are no longer visible. Pursuit is unsuccessful; the hallway where they turned now ends in a solid wall. Recovered cart contains 200+ documents marked for disposal. All are personnel files and incident reports dated between 2026 and 2031. **[01:33:00]** Team discovers a break room alcove not on previous maps. Inside is a metal table, six chairs, and a logbook bound in black leather. The logbook contains handwritten sign-in entries. The last five entries are as follows: > 04/11/2025 - O5-7 - Routine audit. Sector 9 decommissioned. > 04/11/2025 - O5-3 - Witnessed. > 03/02/2025 - O5-7 - Retrieval. > 01/19/2025 - O5-11 - Retrieval. > 11/08/2024 - O5-7 - Initial contact. Agreement reached. Handwriting analysis confirms matches to known samples of O5-7, O5-3, and O5-11. The O5 Council denies any knowledge of SCP-XXXX-B and denies authorizing entry. No record of these visits exists in Foundation access logs. When questioned, O5-7 stated, "I have never been in that place." The team was extracted shortly after. **Addendum XXXX-3: Incident XXXX-3** On 08/16/2025, during a routine inventory, Dr. Chen discovered that Document XXXX-B-0012 had been removed from its storage locker and returned with alterations. The final line now reads: > If you are reading this, do not repeat our error. Do not try to contain it again. **You are already inside the second enclosure.** A new door, SCP-XXXX-A-32, has manifested inside SCP-XXXX-B, Sector 1. It is identical to SCP-XXXX-A instances but is installed in the archive wall itself. It will not open. Audio recorded from the other side consists of fluorescent humming and distant cart wheels.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This SCP entry demonstrates strong adherence to the Foundation's clinical documentation style and successfully incorporates all requested elements. The piece maintains the characteristic dry, scientific tone throughout while building genuine tension and mystery. The author shows clear familiarity with SCP conventions, using proper formatting, containment procedures, and terminology. The concept is genuinely creative - an archive containing records of a previous civilization that mirrors the current Foundation, complete with personnel files for current staff dated centuries ago. The recursive nature of the threat, where the Foundation may already be "inside the second enclosure," is particularly clever and unsettling. The exploration logs are well-executed, providing concrete details that build atmosphere effectively. The entities in hazmat suits removing documents creates a palpable sense of unease, and their behavior when discovered feels authentically mysterious rather than contrived. The discovery of unauthorized O5 Council visits adds appropriate institutional paranoia. The prose is appropriately clinical without being dry to the point of tedium. Technical details like LIDAR mapping and dendrochronological analysis lend authenticity. The recovered documents are convincingly written and serve the narrative well, particularly the altered final document that suggests the Foundation may already be trapped. However, the piece has some limitations. Character development is minimal by design of the format, though this is appropriate for SCP documentation. The emotional engagement comes primarily from cosmic horror and institutional dread rather than character investment. Some elements, like the non-Euclidean geometry, feel somewhat standard for the genre. The ending is effectively dark and ominous, with the revelation that they may already be in a "second enclosure" providing a satisfying twist that recontextualizes the entire scenario. The final image of sounds from beyond the unopenable door is genuinely chilling. The writing flows naturally and maintains reader engagement throughout. While not groundbreaking, it's a solid, competent piece that fulfills its brief effectively and demonstrates good understanding of both the SCP format and cosmic horror conventions. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 19 Believable Character Actions: 16 Nuanced Characters: 12 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 16 Elegant Prose: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Emotionally Complex: 13 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 6 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 7 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 2 Overwrought: 3 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 1 Unearned Transformations: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Sentences Flow Naturally: 17 Overall Reader Engagement: 17 Overall Impression: 16

+ Classic sci-fi: Offworlder (Le Guin) — Score: 78.0
Prompt:
Classic sci-fi: Offworlder (Author style: Le Guin) Write a chapter following a young diplomatic envoy sent to address tensions on a distant human colony where humanity has diverged into distinct subspecies through both natural adaptation and separation of lineages. Against protocol, the envoy slips away from official functions to walk the streets alone, encountering markets, ordinary citizens, and social structures that contradict the official briefings. Upon returning to their quarters, the envoy is reprimanded by Command, and is tasked with approaching the stationed Minister of Science (a political posting rather than scientific). A challenging conversation ensues, in which the envoy's wits and will are tested, by a seasoned political operator looking to test the mettle of the offworlder. Length: 1000 words.
Model Output:
**Offworlder - Chapter 3: The Market** They had briefed me for three weeks on Keita, which is how I knew almost nothing about it when I arrived. In the orientation film, the colony was a diagram. Two branches of humanity, diverged. The Longs, adapted for the open savanna domes — tall, narrow-chested, with black eyes to filter the high UV. And the Shorts, who kept to the deep habs and the mines — compact, broad, pale as cave fish. The film said they no longer intermarried. It said there was tension over water rights and air rationing. It said the streets were unsafe for an unaccompanied offworlder. I am twenty-six and a Junior Envoy of the Ekumen, which means I am old enough to be sent alone and young enough to be expendable. My instructions were to attend the reception, to not cause offense, and to listen. The reception was held in the Governor's atrium, which was cool and white. The Governor was a Long. All his aides were Longs. The Short delegation, when it arrived, stood by the far wall and did not drink. I watched them from inside my formal cloak — heavy grey wool, to the ankles, with a hood — the envoy's habit. On Keita, where the mean temperature in the hab is thirty-one degrees, it is an absurd garment. But we wear it because it hides the body, and hiding the body is thought to be neutral. After the second hour, when the Governor was explaining for the third time how his people valued "harmony through separation," I stepped out through a side vent door. This was against protocol. The street outside was not in the diagram. It was lower than the atrium level, uncooled, and loud. The air smelled of fried oil and hot dust and human sweat. The light fell in hard bars from the slats of the dome above. I had expected segregation. What I saw was a market that made a mockery of it. A Long woman, seven feet tall at least, was minding a stall of small bright circuit boards. Beside her, sitting on the same crate, a Short man was sorting the same boards into piles, his thick fingers quick and precise. Their shoulders touched. When a child ran up — a child I could not categorize, leggy but broad-handed, perhaps a cross, perhaps just a child — the woman lifted him with one arm and set him on her hip. No one had briefed me about children. I walked with my hands hidden in my cloak. People stared. Not with hostility, but with frank, unguarded curiosity. Their own dress was minimal, as befits the heat: bands of woven cloth at waist and chest, or nothing at all but paint. My cloak must have looked like a moving tent, a refusal. A few of the Short women laughed, not unkindly, and said something in Keitan I did not know. One Long boy reached out and touched the hem, then snatched his hand back, giggling. At a food stall a Short old woman was frying flat cakes. Next to her, a Long old man was fanning them cool. I tried to buy one. I had no currency, only my envoy's chit. The woman looked at the chit, then at my face, red with heat inside my hood, and pushed a cake into my hand anyway. She said, "Offworlder eats." I sat on a low wall to eat it. It was salty and good. While I sat, I watched the work. There was no division of labor by type that I could see. Longs carried heavy sacks; Shorts climbed high scaffolds to fix wiring. They spoke the same clipped creole, they haggled, they argued, they touched constantly — a hand on an arm, a shoulder bump. The official briefings had given me two populations in competition for scarce resources. The street gave me one people, varied in shape, living together in a poverty that was not scarcity but a kind of crowding, an intimacy enforced by the dome. It was not utopia. I saw a Long youth shove a Short youth, hard. I saw how the Longs had to stoop to enter the lower shops, how the Shorts squinted in the bright upper levels. There are frictions in any house where people are different sizes. But it was not what they had told me. I had stayed too long. When I returned, sweat cold under my cloak, the duty officer was waiting in my quarters with a tight face. "Command has been trying to reach you," he said. "You left your bead." "I did," I said. The wall screen lit. Command was not the Governor, but my own superior on the orbiter, Director Emel. She did not raise her voice. That was her reprimand. "Envoy Lai," she said, "you were instructed not to go out alone. You are conspicuous and you are ignorant. That is a dangerous combination. Do you understand that your security is a political condition?" "Yes, Director." "Good. Because the Governor has complained. He feels you showed disrespect to the reception. To make amends, you will call on the Minister of Science tomorrow morning. He is stationed here permanently. He will explain the local situation to you in terms you may find more comprehensible. Do not slip away again." The screen went dark. The Minister of Science was not a scientist. I knew that before I went. Minister is a political posting on Keita, given to a Long of good family. His office was in the cool upper tiers, and he did not have to stoop. He was an old Long, his skin oiled, his black eyes filmed slightly at the edge. He wore only a white waistcloth, and his body was beautiful and terrible in its thinness, like a tree. He looked at my cloak and smiled. "So," he said, in perfect Hainish, "the Ekumen sends us a child in a blanket. Sit, please. You must be very hot." I sat. I did not remove the cloak. To remove it would have been to concede. "I walked in the lower market yesterday," I said. "Ah. Did you enjoy our little zoo?" "I saw people working together." "You saw what you wanted to see. That is the privilege of offworlders. You come from a world where you can afford to be mixed. You have room." "We have less room than you think." He laughed. It was a dry sound. "The Ekumen wants us to integrate. To share water, share governance. To become one people again. Tell me, Envoy Lai, do you sleep with Shorts?" The question was meant to shock. I have learned that when someone tries to shock you, you should answer literally. "I have not slept with anyone on Keita," I said. He inclined his head. "A evasion worthy of my own staff. Let me be plain. If we mix, we vanish. The Longs will be gone in three generations. Our bones, our eyes — these were made by this sun, by this place. The Shorts were made by the dark. If you blend us, you get something that belongs nowhere. You call it unity. I call it erasure. And you, in your cloak that hides whether you are Long or Short, man or woman, you are the symbol of that erasure." I felt the sweat run down my back. My wits felt slow, boiled. "Your people are already blending," I said. "I saw children—" "You saw throwbacks. Sterile, most of them. The market people are sentimental. They keep them." It was a lie. I was almost sure it was a lie. The child had looked healthy, alive. But I had no data, only a cake and a touch on my cloak. "And if they are not sterile?" I asked. "If they are your future, not your end?" He leaned forward. His black eyes held mine. "Then what will you report, little envoy? That we are already saved, and need no Ekumen? Or that we are already lost, and need to be managed?" That was the test. Not about Keita, but about me. Whether I would repeat his lie to make my mission easier, or tell a different lie to make it nobler. I thought of the old woman who had fed me, and the man fanning cakes. "I will report that I saw a market," I said. "And that your theory does not describe it. I will report that I do not yet understand Keita. And that I need to walk again, without my cloak, if you will permit it." He stared at me for a long time. Then, to my surprise, he threw back his head and laughed, truly this time. "Good," he said. "You are not as stupid as you look in that blanket. Take it off, then. You are making me feel overdressed."
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates a strong grasp of Le Guin's distinctive style and thematic preoccupations. The author successfully captures her characteristic spare, observational prose, her interest in anthropological speculation, and her focus on cultural misunderstanding and diplomatic complexity. The first-person narration feels authentic to Le Guin's approach, with the young envoy serving as an effective observer-protagonist who discovers the gap between official narratives and lived reality. The worldbuilding is economical but effective, establishing the colony's physical constraints and social dynamics through concrete details rather than exposition. The contrast between the sterile official reception and the vibrant, integrated market creates genuine tension and raises meaningful questions about political narratives versus ground truth. The physical descriptions of the Longs and Shorts feel plausible as evolutionary adaptations while avoiding crude stereotyping. The dialogue, particularly in the confrontation with the Minister of Science, captures the political maneuvering and intellectual sparring that characterizes Le Guin's diplomatic encounters. The Minister's challenge about mixed children and his final test of the envoy's integrity creates genuine dramatic tension. The envoy's response feels earned - neither naive capitulation nor false bravado, but a measured commitment to honest observation. However, the piece has some structural weaknesses. The transition between scenes feels somewhat abrupt, and the pacing in the middle section drags slightly. The symbolic weight of the cloak, while thematically appropriate, becomes somewhat heavy-handed by the end. The Minister's character, while effectively manipulative, verges on being too obviously a "test" figure rather than a fully realized person with his own motivations. The prose generally maintains Le Guin's understated elegance, though there are moments where it becomes slightly self-conscious about its literary aspirations. The ending, while satisfying, resolves perhaps too neatly given the complexity of the political situation established earlier. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 16 Nuanced Characters: 15 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 17 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 16 Elegant Prose: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Emotionally Complex: 14 Coherent: 17 Meandering: 6 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 7 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 3 Overwrought: 4 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 5 Unearned Transformations: 6 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 15 Sentences Flow Naturally: 16 Overall Reader Engagement: 16 Overall Impression: 16

+ Biopunk: Polymerase Chain Reaction — Score: 75.7
Prompt:
Biopunk -- Polymerase Chain Reaction Write a chapter set in a near-future post-apocalyptic world where corporate genetic engineering was designed with built-in obsolescence—engineered DNA lasting only one generation to maximize profits before societal collapse. Follow a biohacker working with salvaged equipment to splice viable food crops for their settlement using rapidly degrading genetic material. As world building / window dressing, include details about jury-rigged lab equipment, and the physical toll (and unexpected upsides) of using untested genetic modifications as medicine. The plot of this chapter is centred around a black market contract. Length: 1000 words.
Model Output:
**Polymerase Chain Reaction** The lab used to be a laundromat. I kept the big industrial dryers because their heating elements still cycled clean. One of them is my thermocycler now. I gutted the drum, lined it with a sous-vide coil from a restaurant supply corpse, and wired it to a car battery and a thermostat I ripped out of a dead incubator. It clicks and wheezes every time it hits 94 degrees. It sounds like it's got asthma. The rest of the bench is the same kind of beautiful trash. Centrifuge is a salad spinner bolted to a Dremel. Gel electrophoresis rig is a takeout container with nine volts running through it. And my reagent rack — that's the real antique collection. Agrigen canisters. Tall, white, with that smug green double-helix logo that used to be on every billboard from here to the coast. *NUTRIGRO MAX-YIELD CORN. LICENSED FOR ONE GROWTH CYCLE. REPRODUCTION PROHIBITED BY LAW.* They engineered the suicide right into the seed. Methylated kill-switches on the second generation, telomeres that frayed like cheap rope. You had to buy again next spring. Maximized shareholder value all the way down to the collapse. Now I wash them out with distilled rainwater and use them for buffer. Tris, EDTA, hope. The corn in the canister in front of me is F2. Second-gen. I can see it under the scope. The leaves are in a petri dish, yellowing at the veins. The genome is coming apart at the seams, exactly like it was designed to. "You're looking rough, Mara." I didn't hear Jax come in over the dryer clicking. He's standing in the doorway with his rebreather off, which means the wind is down outside. Dust isn't stripping paint today. He puts a cold-box on my bench. Old world medical transport, still has the biohazard sticker. The latch is broken and held together with a zip tie. "Black contract," he says. I don't touch it. "We don't do black contracts. Settlement needs calories, not boutique body mods for tower rats." "Settlement needs antibiotics more than calories," he says, and pops the lid. Inside, packed in actual ice — real ice, not the chemical packs we usually get — are three vials. The label is handwritten. *Triticum aestivum - Pre-Collapse Archive. F0.* My mouth goes dry. Pre-collapse wheat. Before Agrigen put the generational lock on everything. Before they made the world pay rent on photosynthesis. Viable, reproducing wheat. The real thing. Next to it is a smaller vial, amber, viscous. My nose prickles before I even open it. My sinuses have been weird since the fix. Three years ago I got caught in a flare-scrubber leak out by the old refinery. Lungs full of hot particulate, liver cooking. No meds left. So I spliced myself. Took an untested lot of synthetic hepatocytes from an Agrigen vet kit — meant for lab pigs — and shot it straight into my femoral. It worked. Mostly. Now my sweat smells faintly of cut grass when I'm stressed. Chlorophyll patches up my forearms like bruises that never fade. I don't need much sun, but UV burns me in minutes. I can taste pH. I can smell when DNA is denaturing — it’s like burnt sugar. That's the upside they don't put on the warning label: I am a walking assay. The downside is my kidneys ache constantly and my period stopped. "Who's it for?" I ask. "Enclave buyer. Up north. They want you to prove it germinates, sequence it, and then make it sterile again." I stare at him. "What?" "They want the opposite of what you're doing. They want you to put the obsolescence back in. One-generation wheat. They'll sell the viable seed to their own people and the sterile seed to everyone else. Same old business model. They'll pay in full-spectrum antibiotics, anti-rad meds, and two kilos of real coffee." The settlement's kids have been coughing black for a month. Fungal lung from the silo grain that's already F3 and barely has the energy to sprout. "That's evil, Jax." "It's business. The same business that made those." He nods at my Agrigen canisters. "You in or not? Because if not, I walk this box to Kessler in the next valley and he will do it with half your skill and twice the collateral damage." I look at the wheat. F0. If I can get it to take, I can feed the whole basin forever. Not just one crop. Forever. But I need the PCR to work first, and my template is fucked. I pull on gloves that have been patched with electrical tape. "I'm keeping a cut," I say. Jax grins. "Knew you would." --- The Agrigen canisters make good reagent holders because they're designed to be inert. Nothing sticks to them. I have one labeled TRIS with a piece of duct tape, one labeled PRIMERS, one labeled TAQ. The Taq is the problem. Real Taq polymerase degrades. What I have is a home-brew I expressed in modified E. coli that lives in a yogurt maker behind the dryers. It's sloppy. It makes mistakes. But it works if I baby it. I extract DNA from the F0 grain with a mortar and pestle and dish soap. I grind the F2 corn too — the dying stuff — because I need to compare where the kill-switch sits. The old Agrigen trick was elegant: they didn't just break reproduction, they linked yield genes to a methylation tag that recruited a nuclease in the embryo. You could cut it out, but if you cut wrong, you lost the yield too. I pipette with a micropipette whose plunger is a chopstick. My hands shake. Not from nerves — from the liver mod. Tremor is a side effect. Into the little PCR tubes I load template, primers, nucleotides, my janky Taq. I drop the tubes into the dryer-drum thermocycler. 94 degrees. Denature. The double helix splits. I can smell it, that burnt sugar. 54 degrees. Anneal. Primers find their home. 72 degrees. Extend. Taq builds. Click. Wheeze. Click. Wheeze. Thirty-five cycles. While it runs, I look at my forearms. The green patches are darker today. I haven't eaten in a day and a half. The settlement rations are thin. The patches help, a little. Photosynthesis at maybe 2% efficiency. Agrigen's pig liver was spliced with some spinach RuBisCO for reasons I still don't understand. Upside: I don't starve as fast. Downside: I itch when I need nitrogen. The machine dings. It's a toaster ding. Literally. I run the product on my takeout-container gel. I stain with beet juice because ethidium bromide would kill me faster than the radiation. Under the UV lamp I built from a nail salon dryer, the bands glow pink. There it is. The F0 wheat is clean. No kill-switch. The F2 corn has a thick, ugly extra band at 600 base pairs. That's the suicide cassette. So I know where to cut. I look at Jax. He's asleep against the dryer, snoring. I take a fresh Agrigen canister. This one still has the original warning on the side: *DO NOT REUSE. PROPERTY OF AGRIGEN CORP.* I have written underneath in permanent marker: *FUCK YOU.* I pour my buffer into it. I start mixing the ligation to put the F0 wheat into a plasmid that will replicate. One batch for the Enclave buyer, with the suicide gene stitched back in, just like they asked. And one batch, in the bottom of the canister where the old label peels, without it. Two futures in two identical white canisters. My kidneys throb. My mouth tastes like copper and ozone. The green in my arms pulses faintly in the UV light. "Hey," I whisper to Jax. "Tell your Enclave buyer the PCR worked." Outside, the wind picks up again, rattling the laundromat's old sign. Inside, something that can grow forever is finally copying itself.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This biopunk story demonstrates strong technical execution and world-building within the specified parameters. The author successfully creates a convincing post-apocalyptic laboratory setting using salvaged equipment, with particularly effective details like the dryer-turned-thermocycler and takeout container electrophoresis rig. The scientific terminology is used accurately and naturally integrated into the narrative. The protagonist Mara is well-developed through her modifications and expertise, though her characterization relies heavily on exposition about her past rather than demonstrated personality traits. Her moral conflict over the black market contract provides adequate tension, but the resolution feels somewhat predictable - the "good" character keeping seeds for the greater good while fulfilling the contract. The world-building excels in its specificity and internal logic. The concept of engineered obsolescence in crops is both scientifically plausible and thematically rich. Details about Mara's chlorophyll patches, pH-tasting ability, and other modification side effects create a lived-in feeling to the biotechnology. The prose maintains a consistent, gritty tone throughout, with effective sensory details like the "burnt sugar" smell of denaturing DNA. However, some passages lean toward over-explanation of technical processes that could have been streamlined. The dialogue between Mara and Jax serves its functional purpose but lacks distinctive voice or memorable exchanges. The story's structure is solid but follows a fairly conventional arc - setup, moral dilemma, resolution through compromise. While competently executed, it doesn't surprise or challenge expectations significantly. The ending, while not inappropriately positive, resolves the central conflict in a way that feels somewhat inevitable given Mara's established character. The writing demonstrates technical proficiency with generally smooth sentence flow and appropriate pacing, though some sections become bogged down in procedural details that slow narrative momentum. + Iteration 3 — Avg Score: 82.2
+ Classic sci-fi: The Azra Gambit — Score: 84.5
Prompt:
Classic sci-fi (Author style: Asimov) The Azra Gambit Colonial mars is being mined by corporations who take leases on indentured labourers. The thing they are mining is Azra, a recently discovered exotic metal which accelerates radioactive decay to such a rate that it is greatly sought after for interstellar drives and weapons alike. This has created both a gold rush and an arms race as various interests vie for control and endeavour to unlock Azra's secrets. The story follows Arthur Neegan, a first generation settler and mining engineer. Upon discovering that his unassuming plot sits atop an immense Azra vein, he is subjected to a flurry of interest and scrutiny. Write the next chapter in this story, in which an armed retinue descends on Arthur's home and politely but forcefully invites him to a meeting with some unknown party off-world. The insignia look like that of the Antares diplomatic corp -- diplomatic in name only. Arthur finds himself in the centre of a political tug of war. The chapter involves a meeting with this unknown party, who makes Arthur an offer. The scene should be primarily dialogue, interspersed with vivid description & scene setting. It should sow hints of the larger intrigue, stakes & dangers. Include Asimov's trademark big-and-small-picture world building and retrofuturistic classic scifi vibe. The chapter begins with Arthur aboard the transfer vessel, wondering just what he's gotten involved in. Length: 1000 words.
Model Output:
The transfer vessel's air filtration system hums with a persistent mechanical drone. It was an old drone, Arthur Neegan thought. Not the clean, efficient whine of the new Colonial scrubbers, but the tired, rattling throb of a military surplus pump that had been patched too many times. That told him more than any insignia. The Antares Diplomatic Corps did not travel in luxury liners. They traveled in converted troop carriers, because diplomacy, on the frontier, was simply war continued by other means. He wondered, not for the first time that morning, just what he had gotten involved in. Three hours ago he had been in his pressure hut at Plot 87-Gamma, watching the mass spectrometer stutter over numbers that could not be true. Azra was supposed to occur in flecks, in micrograms laced through basalt. His core sample had come back at forty-seven percent purity over a two-meter run. A vein. A monstrous, impossible vein, enough to plate the hulls of a dozen Jump cruisers or, as the newscasts liked to whisper, to sterilize a continent. He had logged it, as the Colonial Charter required. That had been his second mistake. The first had been finding it at all. Then the dust outside his viewport had lifted in three perfect, synchronized plumes. A retinue. Six men in matte-black vacuum armor, not the patched-together suits of the corporate lease-guards. Their rifles were slung, but not secured. And on their shoulders, bright as a fresh bruise against the black, the silver starburst of Antares. The man who had knocked - actually knocked, on the outer hull, a politeness so absurd it was threatening - had removed his helmet to reveal a pleasant, forgettable face. "Mr. Neegan? I am Undersecretary Blaine. My principal would like to discuss your recent mineralogical good fortune. We have a launch window in twenty minutes. You will come with us. Please." It had not been a request. Now the transfer vessel shuddered as it mated with something larger. The drone of the filtration system was joined by a deeper clang. Arthur's cheap acceleration couch, clearly bolted in as an afterthought for civilian passengers, jolted him. The hatch irised. "Mr. Neegan. How good of you to join us." The room beyond was not what he expected. After the stripped functionality of the transfer vessel, it was almost decadent. The orbital station - for it must be a station, hanging high over Syrtis - was spun for a third of a gee, and carpeted. Real carpet, deep blue, that must have cost more to lift from Earth than his entire mine. The walls were wood-paneled. Not imitation. Wood. Behind a desk of that same wood sat a woman. She was in her sixties, thin as a wire antenna, her hair cut short and white. She wore the plain gray of the Antares civil service, with no starburst at all, which was somehow more alarming than if she had worn a chest full of them. She was drinking tea from a porcelain cup. In free-fall orbit, that alone was a statement of power. "Please, sit," she said. "I am Prefect Liora Chen. I apologize for the abruptness of Undersecretary Blaine's invitation. Subtlety is wasted on Mars. Everything there is dust and shouting." Arthur sat. The chair was too soft. It was designed to make a man feel small. "You know what I found," Arthur said. "I know what you *think* you found," Chen said, and smiled. "A prospector's dream. An engineer's nightmare. Let me be frank, Mr. Neegan. You are a first-generation settler. You came here on a Colonial Charter loan, which you are still paying off at seven percent. You understand leverage, mass ratios, tensile strength. You are a practical man. That is why I asked to meet you, rather than simply having my office file a claim of eminent domain." "That's very kind." "It is practical. The corporations - Kessler-Volkoff, Ishtar Mineral - they would bury you in injunctions. They would lease your indentures out from under you and work your claim with convict labor until your grandchildren were still paying your air tax. We in Antares dislike waste." She set her tea down. The cup did not rattle. "Earth needs Azra," she said. "You understand why. A gram of Azra in the reaction chamber accelerates the decay of a plutonium pile by a factor of ten thousand. What was once a reactor becomes a bomb. What was once a bomb, properly throttled, becomes a drive that can push a ship to Tau Ceti in eleven months instead of eleven years. Whoever controls Azra controls the distance between stars. And distance, Mr. Neegan, has always been the only thing that keeps peace." Arthur found his voice. "So you want my vein." "No. I want to buy it. Or rather, I want to buy *you*." She slid a thin sheet of plastic across the desk. It lit up at his touch. Numbers. Not the price of ore, but the price of a world. "We will clear your Charter debt. We will grant you full freeholder status, not leaseholder. Your plot, Plot 87-Gamma, will remain deeded in your name in perpetuity, under Martian law. In return, you will grant the Antares Combine exclusive extraction rights for ninety-nine years. You will continue as chief engineer, at a salary - " she tapped the sheet, and a number jumped, " - that will allow you to afford real coffee. And you will file all geological surveys through our office." "And if I sell to Kessler instead? They offered me twice that yesterday." "Kessler will be bankrupt in six months," Chen said, mildly. "We have seen to it. Their Jump freighter *Ariadne* suffered a containment failure two days ago. An Azra accident. Very tragic. Very public. The markets are nervous about corporate handling of exotic materials. Governments, you see, are so much more reassuring." The air filtration hum in Arthur's ears seemed to grow louder. "You're telling me you're going to monopolize it." "I am telling you that Azra is not what you think it is," Chen said. Her pleasantness thinned, just for a second. "Your spectrometer told you purity. It did not tell you structure. That vein of yours is not a natural deposit, Mr. Neegan. Natural Azra does not form in veins. It forms in scatter. Someone - or something - refined it. Concentrated it. Buried it under your little hut like a cache of rifles for a future war. Do you understand? The gold rush you think you are in is over. The arms race has already been lost by someone, a very long time ago. We are merely trying to keep the pieces from being picked up by children." Arthur stared at the numbers glowing under his thumb. A practical man, she had called him. A practical man knew when a polite invitation was a gun to the head. A practical man also knew when someone was telling him the truth because a lie would be less frightening. "And if I say no?" he asked. Prefect Chen picked up her porcelain cup again. "Then you will go home, Mr. Neegan," she said. "And I will be forced to inform the Colonial Authority that Plot 87-Gamma sits on an unstable exotic mass that represents a planetary hazard. They will evacuate you for your own safety. The corporations will be kept away by our blockade, also for your safety. And you will sit in your hut, a freeholder in name, watching that fortune rot under your feet while the galaxy burns around you. As I said, we dislike waste. But we dislike anarchy more." She smiled, and Arthur Neegan, mining engineer, first-generation settler, who had only wanted to find enough metal to pay for a better scrubber, realized that the transfer vessel's tired mechanical drone had not been the sound of a pump at all. It had been the sound of a door closing.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong adherence to the Asimovian style and prompt requirements. The author successfully captures the classic sci-fi tone with clear, functional prose that prioritizes ideas over flowery language. The dialogue-heavy structure works well, particularly in the extended conversation between Arthur and Prefect Chen, which reveals character motivations and world-building details naturally. The characterization is nuanced, especially Chen, who emerges as a compelling antagonist - polite, reasonable, yet utterly ruthless. Her casual mention of the "Ariadne" containment failure and the way she delivers threats wrapped in courtesy creates genuine menace. Arthur's perspective as an overwhelmed everyman caught in forces beyond his control feels authentic and relatable. The world-building excels at Asimov's trademark approach of revealing larger implications through small details. The progression from Arthur's humble mining operation to galactic implications feels organic. The revelation that the Azra deposit is artificial rather than natural is a genuinely surprising twist that recontextualizes the entire scenario effectively. The prose maintains consistent quality throughout, with particularly strong sensory details like the "tired, rattling throb" of the air filtration system and the symbolic weight of Chen's porcelain teacup in zero gravity. These details serve both atmospheric and thematic purposes. The pacing builds tension effectively, moving from Arthur's confusion through the revelation of stakes to the final ultimatum. The ending metaphor of the closing door provides satisfying closure while maintaining the ominous tone. Some minor weaknesses include occasional exposition that could be more seamlessly integrated, and a few moments where Arthur's responses feel slightly underdeveloped compared to Chen's commanding presence. However, these are minor issues in an otherwise accomplished piece that successfully captures both the requested style and creates genuine narrative tension. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 17 Nuanced Characters: 16 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 17 Elegant Prose: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Emotionally Complex: 15 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 2 Overwrought: 3 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 1 Unearned Transformations: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Sentences Flow Naturally: 17 Overall Reader Engagement: 17 Overall Impression: 17
Scores: Adherence to Instructions: 18.0, Believable Character Actions: 17.0, Nuanced Characters: 16.0, Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18.0, Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 17.0, Elegant Prose: 16.0, Emotionally Engaging: 16.0, Emotionally Complex: 15.0, Coherent: 18.0, Meandering: 3.0, Weak Dialogue: 4.0, Tell-Don't-Show: 5.0, Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4.0, Amateurish: 3.0, Purple Prose: 2.0, Overwrought: 3.0, Incongruent Ending Positivity: 1.0, Unearned Transformations: 2.0, Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17.0, Sentences Flow Naturally: 17.0, Overall Reader Engagement: 17.0, Overall Impression: 17.0
+ Contemporary romance: Darkroom Revelations — Score: 82.8
Prompt:
Darkroom Revelations Write a first-person narrative from the perspective of Dani, a 25-year-old assertive art student known for her bold style and take-charge attitude in relationships. For the past semester, she's been quietly fascinated by Elliot (he/him), a 19-year-old boy in her experimental photography class. Dani has been intrigued by his unconventional masculinity: killer thrift-shop-nu-goth style, soft demeanour and quiet intellect. When they're paired for a final project requiring late nights in the darkroom, Elliot tentatively shares that he's been questioning his gender identity and expression. Set during one of these intimate darkroom sessions where the red light creates a confessional atmosphere. Explore Dani's complex attraction—her usual assertiveness complicated by her desire to protect Elliot's vulnerability, her excitement at witnessing his self-discovery, and her confusion about what this means for her own identity as someone typically attracted to more defined gender presentations. The narrative should capture the tender awkwardness of their conversations, Dani's internal struggle between taking charge and wanting to give Elliot agency, and the unexpected chemistry that develops as both find themselves in uncharted territory. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The darkroom has always made me feel powerful. Outside I'm loud. Dyed-black bob, bleached money pieces, silver hoops big enough to get caught in a loom. I wear my dad's old Dickies with fishnets underneath and I walk into critique like I own the building. I'm 25 and back for my BFA after two years of trying to make rent with freelance gigs, so I don't have time to be shy. If I want something, I take it. If I want someone, same. Except Elliot. He's 19. Sophomore. Sits two enlargers down in Experimental Photo and never says a damn thing unless Professor Kline calls on him, and then it's something so smart and quiet the whole room has to lean in. Thrift-shop nu-goth, like if a Victorian ghost shopped at Goodwill. Black nail polish always chipped, mesh long-sleeve under a distressed tee, soft black eyeliner that he smudges like he did it on the bus. He has this way of being a boy that doesn't feel like any boy I've ever wanted before, and I've wanted a lot of very defined boys. Sharp jaw, leather jacket, knows how to use his hands. Elliot's hands are thin and ink-stained and always cold. We got paired for the final. Of course. The universe finally taking pity on my semester-long staring problem. It's 10:47 p.m. on a Thursday and we're on our third late night. The rule is no white light after you open the paper safe. Just the red safelight, which makes everything look like a secret. The air smells like developer and that faint metallic tang. Depeche Mode is playing low from my phone because Elliot said he liked them once and I catalogued it. He's printing his negatives. Mine are already hanging - close-ups of the laundromat on 5th. His are all self-portraits. Or, I thought they were. Him in his room, in his clothes. But tonight he brought a different roll. "Can I show you something that didn't... that I wasn't sure about?" he asks. His voice is barely louder than the timer ticking. "Yeah. Of course." He slips a negative into the carrier. It's him, but in a black slip dress. Not costumey. Just a dress. His hair is down, longer than he usually wears it tucked behind his ears. Lipstick. He's looking straight at the camera like he's daring it to laugh. My chest does something stupid and tight. He doesn't look at me. He watches the image bloom in the tray, that magic trick that never gets old. White paper, then ghost, then person. "I've been trying stuff," he says finally. "At home. Just to see." The red light turns his eyelashes pink. I want, so badly, to do what I always do. To reach, to frame it for him, to say *fuck, Elliot, you look hot, you look right.* To take charge of the moment and make it easy. But his shoulders are up to his ears. He looks like if I move too fast he'll fold back into himself. So I keep my hands in the developer, even though the water is getting cold. "Thank you for showing me," I say, and my voice comes out softer than I mean it to. "Do you like how it looks?" He nods once, quick. "I don't know. I like how it feels. The dress. But then I look and I'm like... am I just trying to be interesting? Like, is this just an aesthetic thing? Because I like how I look in my other stuff too. I like being a boy sometimes. I think." He laughs, embarrassed. "Sorry, that's a lot. You don't have to—" "Hey," I say. "Don't apologize. You're literally in the perfect place to not know. That's the whole assignment, right? Experiment." He finally glances at me. "You always know what you are, though." That hits weird. Do I? I've always been the girl who likes boys who know they're boys. Girls who know they're girls. I liked the clarity of it, the way I could be the assertive one against something solid. Elliot is not solid. He's smoke. And I'm finding that maybe I don't want something solid to push against. Maybe I want to sit in the smoke with someone. "I know how to act like I know what I am," I admit. "That's different." He smiles a little at that. Real smile, not the polite one he gives in class. "I kept thinking you'd think I was lying," he says. "About being a guy. Because I'm questioning. Like I'm tricking you or something." "You're not tricking me," I say too fast. "Elliot, pronouns still he/him? Do you want me to—" "He/him is okay for now. It's just... all of it is he/him for now until it isn't." "Cool. Got it. He/him for now." I say it like I'm memorizing a formula. He/him for now. I want to get it right. The print is done. He lifts it with tongs, hangs it. We watch it drip. In red light, the black dress looks blacker, his skin looks almost lit from inside. "It's beautiful," I whisper. "You are." He goes pink, which you can even see under red light. "You wanna try printing one of mine?" he asks, suddenly busy. "I'm still figuring out exposure times." "Yeah, show me." He reaches over me for the stack of fresh Ilford paper we haven't opened yet. His mesh sleeve brushes my arm. I smell his laundry detergent and something like vanilla. My usual brain would clock that as an opening - close proximity, late night, shared secret. My usual brain would have already said something cocky and pulled him in. Instead I freeze, because I can feel how much he is trying to give me this normal moment back. And because I don't want to be another person who decides what this means for him. He fumbles. His cold, ink-stained hand catches the edge of the paper box wrong and the whole stack — maybe twenty sheets — slides out onto the counter, half out of the light-safe bag. For a second neither of us moves. Then we both lunge. "Shit — white light—" I say, but it's too late. We've been in safelight for an hour. That paper is fogged. Useless. Dead. "Fuck," Elliot breathes. "I'm so sorry. That was like forty bucks—" "It's fine," I say, and I'm laughing before I can stop. Nervous, real laughter. "It's fine, it's Kline's department budget anyway. It's conceptually fogged. It's about transience or whatever." He stares at the ruined stack, then at me, then starts laughing too, this surprised snort he immediately tries to cover with his hand. "God, I'm such a disaster," he says, but he's smiling so big now. "You're not," I say. "You're learning. We're both..." I gesture vaguely between us, at the ruined paper, at his print dripping, at the red room that feels like the inside of a heart. "Uncharted territory?" he offers. "Yeah. That." There's a beat where the timer dings and neither of us moves to turn it off. In the red light, I can see him deciding something. He takes a half-step closer. Not much. Just enough that I could close the rest if I wanted to. He's giving me the choice, and he's also giving himself one. "Dani?" he says. "Yeah?" "Can I... would it be okay if I kissed you? Or is that weird now, because I showed you that picture and you're—" I don't let him finish the spiral. But I don't grab him either. I put my hand out, palm up, on the counter between us. An invitation, not a demand. My heart is hammering like I'm 19 again. "You can," I say. "If you want to. I want you to, if you want to." He looks at my hand like it's a test he wants to pass. Then he puts his cold hand in mine. His fingers are shaking a little. Mine are too, which has never happened to me before. He leans in. I lean in. We meet crooked because we're both nervous and laughing again, and his lipstick from the photo isn't real but I can imagine it, and his eyeliner is smudged, and he tastes like mint gum. It's soft. It's brief. It's not me taking charge. It's us figuring out the exposure time together. When we pull back, the safelight is still red, the ruined paper is still ruined, and Elliot's self-portrait is still dripping developer onto the floor. "Worth the forty bucks?" he whispers. "So worth it," I say, and squeeze his hand.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong technical execution and emotional intelligence in handling a delicate subject matter. The narrative successfully captures Dani's voice as an assertive art student while showing her vulnerability when confronted with unfamiliar territory. The darkroom setting is expertly utilized both as literal backdrop and metaphor for exploration and development. The characterization is particularly strong. Dani's voice feels authentic - her confidence mixed with uncertainty, her typical take-charge attitude tempered by respect for Elliot's vulnerability. The author skillfully shows her internal conflict between her usual assertiveness and her desire to give Elliot agency. Elliot emerges as a fully realized character despite limited dialogue, with his tentative self-exploration feeling genuine rather than performative. The prose strikes an effective balance between literary sophistication and accessibility. Phrases like "He's smoke" and "the red room that feels like the inside of a heart" demonstrate poetic sensibility without becoming overwrought. The technical photography details are woven naturally into the narrative, enhancing authenticity. The dialogue feels natural and age-appropriate, particularly in the awkward pauses and half-finished sentences that characterize vulnerable conversations. The exchange about pronouns is handled with sensitivity and realism. The ruined paper incident provides effective comic relief while advancing the emotional arc. The pacing builds tension effectively toward the kiss scene, which feels earned rather than rushed. The author resists the temptation to resolve all complexities, instead ending on a note of mutual discovery that feels honest to the characters' situations. Some minor weaknesses include occasional reliance on physical description to convey emotion and a few moments where the metaphorical language borders on precious. The ending, while sweet, perhaps resolves the tension slightly too neatly given the complexity of the situation established. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 19 Believable Character Actions: 18 Nuanced Characters: 18 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 17 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 17 Elegant Prose: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 18 Emotionally Complex: 17 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 5 Overwrought: 4 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 6 Unearned Transformations: 4 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Sentences Flow Naturally: 17 Overall Reader Engagement: 17 Overall Impression: 17
